newsletters.summary: A synthetic cell built entirely from nonliving components has achieved a milestone by growing, replicating DNA, and dividing for the first time, demonstrating the feasibility of generating life from nonliving matter—a decades-long goal in synthetic biology. The newsletter covers major deep tech developments including Anthropic's entry into drug development, Realta Fusion generating direct electricity from fusion reactions, a $5.7B chip plant by Infineon in Germany, and defense innovations like Esh-Tech's laser drone defense system.
keyTakeaways: Synthetic biology is transitioning from theoretical research to demonstrable milestones with practical applications; investors should monitor companies developing artificial cellular systems and synthetic organisms for therapeutic and industrial uses.

AI companies like Anthropic are vertically integrating into biotech and drug development, signaling a major capital reallocation trend where machine learning platforms are moving into traditionally pharmaceutical territory with potentially faster development cycles.

Energy and defense sectors show accelerating government funding—Europe's chip autonomy push ($5.7B Infineon), fusion commercialization breakthroughs, and emerging defense tech like directed-energy systems suggest sustained geopolitical competition driving capital into strategic tech sectors.
newsletters.originalTitle: DTN 169: A cell built from scratch grows and divides

newsletters.title: Maddyness
newsletters.summary: A French tech newsletter highlights three investment opportunities: the Fundtruck 2026 entrepreneurial competition selected Athana and Zylio as finalists at VivaTech with record participation; BRED Banque Populaire is launching a dedicated hub with two open calls for startups to co-develop banking solutions by July 17; and Cisco is positioning itself to democratize AI adoption across enterprises with dedicated learning programs.
keyTakeaways: Traditional financial institutions are actively opening innovation hubs to collaborate with startups—BRED's dual call for proposals signals growing corporate venture appetite in France and represents potential acquisition or partnership targets for early-stage founders.

Competitive startup selection events like Fundtruck are consolidating deal flow and visibility, with record participation suggesting a strong pipeline despite macro uncertainty in European VC markets.

Enterprise AI democratization remains a major growth vector; large players like Cisco are investing in education and adoption programs, creating opportunities for B2B SaaS startups focused on AI implementation and workforce enablement.
newsletters.originalTitle: 03.07 - Les Rencontres économiques d'Aix s'ouvrent dans «un monde sans repères»

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: Market panic over AI disrupting enterprise software is creating a historic opportunity for margin expansion, as companies that successfully deploy agentic AI through orchestration layers will capture unprecedented value. Bending Spoons' 39.7% IPO pop demonstrates investor appetite for operational turnaround plays, while Medallia's $5.1 billion equity wipeout shows the cost of delaying AI adoption—though new owners immediately committed $150 million to accelerate its digital labor capabilities.
keyTakeaways: The true competitive moat in AI-driven software lies not in the large language models themselves (which will be commoditized) but in proprietary AgentOps orchestration layers that encode human judgment into scalable workflows. Investors should target distressed SaaS acquisitions where teams can rapidly build this AI infrastructure.

Timing is critical: the window to capitalize on AI-driven SaaS transformation is closing. Passive investors risk being left behind as strategic buyers (like Blackstone-Anthropic consortium) and aggressive sponsors capture the margin super-cycle through active capital deployment.

The SaaS sector is entering a historic margin boom driven by the transition from seat-based licensing to outcome-based digital labor, contradicting the 'SaaS-pocalypse' narrative currently depressing software valuations and creating tactical buying opportunities in the debt and equity markets.
newsletters.originalTitle: Software wins for the taking

newsletters.title: Term Sheet
newsletters.summary: Defense tech venture funding has exploded to record levels, with Q1 2026 seeing $19.8 billion deployed across 262 deals—more than triple the $5.7 billion from Q1 2024. Companies like Anduril ($61B valuation), Saronic ($9.25B), and Shield AI ($12.7B) are leading a sector shift that even Anduril's CEO acknowledges shows signs of a bubble, with early-stage startups commanding valuations of 17-50x revenue. The critical question for investors is not whether a bubble exists, but which corners of defense tech funding will prove fragile versus resilient as the sector matures.
keyTakeaways: Defense tech valuations have dramatically decoupled from fundamentals, with early-stage companies commanding multiples of 17-50x revenue. Investors should scrutinize which startups have sustainable government contracts versus those riding hype, as corrections are likely in overvalued segments.

Traditional Silicon Valley investors may lack the expertise and risk tolerance needed for defense sector economics, which operate on different timelines and procurement cycles than consumer or enterprise software. VCs should assess whether their existing playbooks translate to this market.

Despite bubble indicators, the sector has legitimate tailwinds: geopolitical tensions, AI-driven military innovation, and government procurement demand. The real risk isn't sector collapse, but consolidation and extended runways for companies that can't monetize hype.
newsletters.originalTitle: Is there a defense tech bubble?

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: The Nuclear Regulatory Commission proposed major regulatory overhauls to accelerate nuclear energy deployment, including replacing radiation protection standards with hard dose limits and expanding licensing pathways for novel reactor designs. Three companies in the Department of Energy's reactor pilot program—Deployable Energy, Aalo Atomic, and Valar Atomics—have achieved reactor criticality, while PJM Interconnection faces growing concerns about its ability to forecast and meet surging electricity demand from data centers and air conditioning, with demand potentially exceeding summer projections by 10 gigawatts.
keyTakeaways: Microreactor startups are rapidly advancing toward commercialization with three achieving criticality within the DOE's pilot timeline, signaling near-term revenue potential for specialized nuclear technology providers and supply chain companies serving this emerging sector.

The NRC's regulatory streamlining creates a clearer pathway for novel reactor designs to enter the market faster, potentially reducing capital requirements and timelines for advanced nuclear developers and making the sector more attractive for late-stage venture and growth equity investment.

Grid operators like PJM are systematically underestimating peak demand driven by AI data centers and climate change, creating urgent infrastructure investment opportunities in peaking power generation, grid modernization, and demand-side management solutions.
newsletters.originalTitle: Radioactive!

newsletters.title: Pro Rata
newsletters.summary: Axios Pro Rata covers Sun Valley's exclusive guest list (359 attendees including five Mag 7 CEOs but notably excluding Nvidia, Tesla, and SpaceX leadership), reports on Lime's IPO with a 4% first-day pop after raising $167 million, and highlights Microsoft's $2.5 billion investment in a new forward-deployed engineering business embedding 6,000 AI experts into customer organizations. The newsletter also flags a slowdown in megadeals and PE exits in Q2 2026.
keyTakeaways: Sun Valley attendance patterns signal shifting power dynamics in tech—the absence of Nvidia and Tesla leadership while OpenAI gets two seats reflects investor sentiment tilting toward AI infrastructure plays over hardware and EV dominance.

Lime's profitable, cash-flow-positive IPO validates the winner-take-most thesis in micromobility after Bird's failure, suggesting venture-backed companies with unit economics can exit publicly if they achieve scale and operational efficiency.

Microsoft's $2.5 billion forward-deployed engineering play indicates enterprise AI service delivery is becoming a capital-intensive competitive moat, signaling a shift from software licensing to managed AI implementation services that could pressure traditional consulting firms.
newsletters.originalTitle: Axios Pro Rata: Sun Valley

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Wind and solar tax credits are set to expire, with Democrats in Congress pushing to restore them despite uncertain industry support. The newsletter teases a deeper analysis of the political and economic tensions surrounding renewable energy incentives and suggests the industry may have mixed motivations regarding credit renewal.
keyTakeaways: Renewable energy tax credits face expiration with uncertain political backing—investors should monitor Congressional activity closely as credit uncertainty could impact project economics and capital allocation in the wind and solar sectors

Industry opposition to credit restoration signals potential shifts in renewable energy economics; companies may be signaling confidence in standalone profitability or concerns about credit policy volatility affecting long-term business models

The misalignment between Democratic support for credits and industry hesitation suggests the renewable energy market may be transitioning from subsidy-dependent to market-competitive, with significant implications for future funding rounds and infrastructure investment valuations
newsletters.originalTitle:
The Wind and Solar Tax Credits Are About to Expire. Will They Come
 Back?

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Electric vehicle sales are showing unexpected strength in Q2 2026 despite the expiration of federal tax credits, with Tesla reporting 25% year-over-year sales growth driven largely by European demand, while Rivian projects delivering 3,000 more vehicles than expected this year. The newsletter also examines the formal end of solar and wind tax credits on July 4, 2026, and the emerging debate among Democrats about whether to revive these incentives or prioritize permitting reform as a more fiscally efficient path to clean energy deployment.
keyTakeaways: Tesla's 25% quarterly sales surge and Rivian's upward delivery guidance suggest EV demand resilience beyond U.S. tax credit incentives, particularly in Europe where high fuel prices and aggressive pricing strategies are driving adoption—indicating the market may be maturing faster than policy assumptions.

The disconnect between clean energy experts prioritizing permitting reform and Democratic lawmakers' reluctance to engage Republicans on the issue signals that future climate policy may face political gridlock, creating opportunities for private solutions to infrastructure bottlenecks.

The expiration of both EV and renewable energy tax credits by mid-2026 combined with higher power prices and grid congestion reshapes the clean energy investment landscape toward cost-competitive technologies and away from subsidy-dependent models, affecting capital allocation decisions for infrastructure investors.
newsletters.originalTitle: Some Decent News for EVs, Finally

newsletters.title: Maddyness
newsletters.summary: Withings, an 18-year-old French healthtech company, has announced its first year of profitability, establishing France as its third-largest market behind Germany and the US. The newsletter also covers broader European tech developments including regulatory approval for Poppins (an AI-assisted mobile app for dyslexic children), Italy's strengthened presence at VivaTech 2026 with 44 startups, and ongoing challenges to French digital sovereignty despite policy initiatives.
keyTakeaways: Withings' path to profitability after nearly two decades demonstrates the long-term capital requirements for hardware-software hybrid businesses in healthtech, signaling potential exit opportunities in maturing medtech platforms that achieved early European market traction.

Poppins' HAS reimbursement approval represents an exceptional regulatory breakthrough for digital health in France, suggesting increasing willingness to fund health-focused tech solutions—a significant tailwind for edtech and health-adjacent startups seeking institutional validation.

Italy's coordinated ecosystem push at VivaTech 2026 and concerns about French digital sovereignty indicate growing regional competition for tech talent and capital, with implications for cross-border European VC strategy and the viability of decentralized tech ecosystems outside Silicon Valley.
newsletters.originalTitle: 02.07 - 18 ans plus tard, cet acteur phare de la French Tech devient rentable

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: PitchBook released new Q2 2026 dashboards showing a bifurcated private markets recovery: venture capital is experiencing a historic liquidity boom driven by SpaceX's $1.7 trillion IPO and upcoming OpenAI and Anthropic listings, generating nearly $2 trillion in total exit value for the quarter alone. Meanwhile, private equity is diverging negatively, with exits pacing below 2025 levels, weakest dealmaking since Q2 2023, and concentrated value in mega-deals. The SPAC market is resurging with 117 new vehicles in H1 2026 as VC-backed companies in quantum, energy, and AI infrastructure seek alternatives to traditional exit routes.
keyTakeaways: VC liquidity is heavily concentrated: SpaceX's IPO alone surpassed all of 2021's exit value, raising the critical question whether broad LP returns materialize or value remains trapped with a few mega-deal investors through Q3-Q4 2026

PE investors face a liquidity crunch—exits are slowing below prior year while continuation vehicles become the dominant exit mechanism, signaling GPs may need to extend hold periods or accept lower realized returns

SPACs are re-emerging as a viable exit alternative for VC-backed companies in policy-favored sectors (AI, energy, quantum), but new SEC disclosure rules have fundamentally changed risk dynamics compared to the 2020-22 bubble
newsletters.originalTitle: New Q2 data, new dashboard

newsletters.title: Term Sheet
newsletters.summary: Eli Lilly invested $40 million in Absci, an AI drug discovery company, as part of a $100 million funding round to develop ABS-201, an injectable antibody for baldness and endometriosis designed using generative AI. The deal signals Big Pharma's confidence in AI-designed drugs and Absci's thesis of combining AI discovery with China-based clinical trials to reduce development costs from $150 million to $15-20 million, while positioning injectables as the next consumer health category following GLP-1s' mainstream acceptance.
keyTakeaways: AI drug discovery is attracting massive institutional capital—Isomorphic Labs raised $2.1B, Earendil Labs $787M, and Xaira $1B—but only Absci is publicly traded and already in Phase 1 trials, making it a rare publicly accessible play on this trend for investors seeking AI pharma exposure.

The real value play for Absci isn't licensing AI tools (which are becoming commoditized) but owning the final drug asset itself, combined with cost arbitrage through China-based trials; this model could compress timelines and reduce capital requirements, reshaping pharma economics.

The injectable market is shifting from medical necessity to lifestyle/wellness category—GLP-1s normalized self-injection for weight loss, and combining hair regrowth, weight loss, and other biologics into single-shot "total vitality" products represents a $650 billion addressable market where DTC distribution becomes a key competitive advantage.
newsletters.originalTitle: Hair apparent

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: The U.S. Trump administration is preparing to ban foreign-made inverters, particularly Chinese models from Huawei and Sungrow that dominate the global market, citing security concerns over rogue communications devices discovered in Chinese solar panels—a move that could benefit domestic players like Texas-based SolarEdge. Meanwhile, Florida has prohibited municipalities from setting net-zero emissions goals through new legislation, while the Trump administration pressures New York to abandon solar development on prime farmland, reflecting broader political headwinds for renewable energy expansion.
keyTakeaways: Inverter import bans create a protectionist windfall for U.S. manufacturers like SolarEdge but introduce supply chain risk and potential cost increases for solar developers, as the global inverter market is already contracting 2% annually due to policy uncertainty.

State-level climate policy rollback in Florida and federal pressure on New York's solar expansion signal a shift in regulatory environment that could reduce near-term solar project deployment and investor returns in these key markets.

Geopolitical tensions over Chinese solar supply chains are accelerating domestic manufacturing reshoring (SolarEdge Austin factory) but may slow overall solar market growth, particularly as the sector faces simultaneous headwinds from restrictive state policies and import controls.
newsletters.originalTitle: The Zeal of the Inverter

newsletters.title: Fintech Business Weekly
newsletters.summary: A podcast episode examining the collapse of Parker Card, an SMB-focused fintech credit card startup, explores critical lessons for the fintech industry regarding business model sustainability and risk management. The episode also covers emerging concerns around prediction markets (including Polymarket's alleged fake advertising and Meta's Arena launch) and the persistent debanking issue affecting crypto and fintech companies.
keyTakeaways: Fintech credit card startups face structural challenges around unit economics and customer acquisition costs—Parker Card's collapse signals that disruption alone isn't sufficient without sustainable revenue models or adequate capital reserves.

Regulatory scrutiny is intensifying across multiple fintech verticals simultaneously: prediction markets face CFTC rule proposals while debanking continues to threaten crypto and fintech access to traditional banking rails, suggesting capital should focus on regulatory-compliant infrastructure plays.

The combination of fintech failures, platform debanking, and proposed regulations indicates the sector is consolidating toward larger, well-capitalized players with banking relationships and compliance infrastructure rather than lean, growth-at-all-costs startups.
newsletters.originalTitle: Fintech Recap: What Can We Learn From A Fintech Credit Card's Collapse?

newsletters.title: Pro Rata
newsletters.summary: Bending Spoons, an Italian holding company that acquires and revitalizes legacy tech brands like AOL, Eventbrite, and Vimeo, raised $1.7 billion in its IPO at $29 per share, valuing the company at $18.4 billion. CEO Luca Ferrari outlined the company's acquisition strategy, which targets predictable businesses with significant operational improvement potential, acquiring 4-5 companies annually and identifying over 1,000 possible targets globally.
keyTakeaways: Bending Spoons' acquisition model prioritizes operational transformation over portfolio synergies, buying underperforming assets across verticals and rebuilding technology, products, and monetization strategies rather than cross-selling between portfolio companies.

The company's acquisition velocity is constrained by execution capacity rather than deal availability—Ferrari estimates 90% of their 1,000 identified targets could be acquired with sufficient capital, but transformation timelines limit deals to 4-5 per year, suggesting significant dry powder remains underdeployed.

The $18.4 billion IPO valuation validates a new M&A model for distressed digital assets, potentially signaling increased capital allocation toward acquiring and operationally improving legacy tech rather than funding greenfield startups.
newsletters.originalTitle: Axios Pro Rata: AOL IPO

newsletters.title: Latitude Media
newsletters.summary: Oracle is challenging Wisconsin's collateral requirements for its massive 1-GW Stargate data center project, arguing the A-/A3 credit rating threshold requiring over $100 million in annual security is unusually strict. However, analysis shows Wisconsin's terms are comparable to or less stringent than collateral requirements from utilities like Santee Cooper and FPL, and the credit threshold is already established in Indiana and Ohio where Oracle's competitors have accepted similar terms. The case highlights a growing tension between hyperscalers and regulators over cost-sharing mechanisms for grid infrastructure serving large data center loads.
keyTakeaways: Oracle's legal challenge to Wisconsin collateral rules may fail on precedent grounds—Amazon, Google, and Microsoft all accepted A-/A3 thresholds in competing utility territories, potentially weakening any 'unfair treatment' argument.

Collateral sizing methodologies vary significantly by utility: Wisconsin's approach based on power plant net book value ($7B for Oracle) is more capital-intensive than bill-based models elsewhere, creating competitive disadvantages for data center developers in that region.

Data center regulation is becoming a critical battleground for infrastructure investment returns—stricter collateral terms and credit thresholds may steer future mega-load projects toward utilities with more favorable tariff structures, reshaping regional competitive dynamics in AI infrastructure development.
newsletters.originalTitle: Oracle vs. the Wisconsin PSC

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Google and Amazon's latest sustainability reports reveal massive energy consumption surges driven by AI infrastructure buildout, with Google's greenhouse gas emissions up 18% and energy use jumping 37%, while Amazon's emissions increased 16%. A new Groundwork Collaborative report proposes policy solutions including voluntary utility-level electricity rate freezes paired with federal financing and easier permitting, designed to stabilize grid costs during a critical multi-year capacity crunch as power plants come online.
keyTakeaways: Big Tech's AI infrastructure spending is outpacing grid decarbonization, creating both a near-term constraint on electricity supply and long-term risk to corporate sustainability commitments—investors should monitor whether rate pressures force capex reductions in AI buildout plans.

The proposed rate-freeze mechanism with federal financing incentives could become a material policy tool affecting utility profitability and capex planning; utilities that opt in gain permitting advantages and low-cost capital, creating potential winners among regional grid operators and transmission companies.

The grid capacity crunch during 2026-2028 represents a macro inflection point where energy infrastructure, renewables deployment, and data center siting decisions converge—early-stage companies in grid modernization, virtual power plants, and reconductoring technology may see accelerated adoption.
newsletters.originalTitle: Here’s One Way to Freeze Electricity Rates

newsletters.title: Maddyness
newsletters.summary: BlaBlaCar is leveraging AI to expand into 20 new countries across Latin America, Southeast Asia, Europe, and North Africa, effectively doubling its international footprint. The newsletter also highlights France's vibrant startup ecosystem with 1,300 startups in the Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes region, while fintech Defacto pursues European banking credentials to scale PME financing and Pasqal accelerates quantum computing applications with Crédit Agricole CIB.
keyTakeaways: BlaBlaCar's rapid geographic expansion powered by AI represents a maturing European tech company successfully scaling internationally—investors should monitor how AI-driven operational efficiency becomes a competitive moat for ridesharing and marketplace platforms across emerging markets.

Defacto's pursuit of official EU banking credentials signals a critical inflection point for fintech scaling: regulatory approval unlocks pan-European operations and competitive advantages that venture-backed competitors cannot easily replicate, validating the fintech-to-bank conversion thesis.

France's tech ecosystem demonstrates sustained venture capital velocity with significant regional clusters beyond Paris; the quantum computing collaboration between traditional finance (Crédit Agricole) and startups (Pasqal) shows institutional capital increasingly backing deep-tech applications in operational markets rather than pure research.
newsletters.originalTitle: 01.07 - BlaBlaCar débarque dans 20 nouveaux pays !

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: US roofing is emerging as a major PE consolidation opportunity comparable to the HVAC rollup, with QXO's $17 billion TopBuild acquisition at 14.9x EBITDA setting valuation benchmarks. The fragmented $92.5 billion market—dominated by 100,000 small owner-operated businesses with less than 4% concentration among top three players—is experiencing a surge in PE activity (67 deals in 2025 vs. 13 in 2019), driven by a cyclical re-roofing wave as homes from the 2003-2006 housing boom enter their 20-25 year replacement window.
keyTakeaways: Roofing consolidation offers durable, inflation-resistant cash flows with demand driven by replacement cycles rather than economic conditions—80% of 2025 market activity was re-roofing, not new construction. PE sponsors should use QXO's TopBuild deal as the valuation ceiling for platform acquisitions in this space.

Material cost headwinds are real: doubled steel/aluminum tariffs (now 50%) and crude oil price inflation tied to geopolitical tension are pressuring margins. Sponsors must stress-test pricing power assumptions and ensure portfolio companies can pass through cost increases to maintain returns.

The roofing sector mirrors HVAC's pre-consolidation profile—extreme fragmentation, founder succession challenges, and predictable revenue growth at 5% annually. With 100,000 potential acquisition targets mostly under $10 million revenue, this represents a multi-year PE deployment opportunity comparable to previous specialty construction roll-ups.
newsletters.originalTitle: The roof is on fire

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: The Trump administration paid Duke Energy $129 million to abandon its North Carolina offshore wind lease, marking the second major federal buyout of renewable energy projects in two weeks following a $765 million deal with Invenergy. Simultaneously, the Supreme Court stripped independence from FERC and the NRC by granting the president power to fire commissioners without cause, fundamentally altering regulatory oversight of energy infrastructure at a moment when transmission expansion faces mounting landowner challenges.
keyTakeaways: Federal subsidies for abandoning renewable infrastructure signal a structural shift in energy capital allocation—investors should reassess exposure to offshore wind developers and question future project viability in politically contested jurisdictions.

FERC's loss of independence creates regulatory uncertainty for long-term energy investments; decisions previously grounded in technical expertise may now face politicization, affecting the credibility and predictability of grid modernization approvals.

Transmission line development is fragmenting regionally—while North Dakota approved the JETx line, Texas landowner challenges signal that project-level opposition remains a material risk factor that could slow grid expansion needed for renewable integration.
newsletters.originalTitle: Duke Abdicates

newsletters.title: Term Sheet
newsletters.summary: Vicus Ventures, a newly launched VC firm founded by brothers Raj and Sunny Singh Sandhu, has closed a $55 million debut fund with a contrarian thesis that founders need operational support and network access rather than larger capital checks. The firm, backed by prominent LPs including General Catalyst, Kleiner Perkins, Jeff Wilke, Mike Novogratz, and others, focuses on delivering "minimum dilution, maximum impact" through a curated ecosystem approach rather than blank checks.
keyTakeaways: Emerging managers are successfully differentiating on value-add and right-sizing funds rather than capital availability—Vicus deliberately capped their debut at $55M despite interest in larger checks, betting that fund size matters less than operational impact and deal quality

LP quality and brand now matter more than LP quantity for emerging fund credibility; Vicus's roster of recognizable names and operators (not just institutional LPs) signals a shift toward founder-friendly, network-driven capital allocation

The "network-as-a-service" model represents a resurgence of old-school venture capitalism focused on relationship capital and founder support, positioning smaller, specialized funds as alternatives to mega-fund dry powder in an increasingly saturated market
newsletters.originalTitle: The VCs building a village

newsletters.title: Pro Rata
newsletters.summary: Nippon Steel has invested less than $200 million of its promised $11 billion U.S. Steel acquisition commitment over the past year, with projections to reach only $580 million by August despite reaffirming its pledge. The article also covers FlexWork Sports' acquisition of ProCamps amid growing bipartisan scrutiny of PE involvement in youth sports, plus three venture capital rounds including LeapXpert's $180 million funding for external messaging app monitoring.
keyTakeaways: Nippon Steel's leverage has nearly tripled post-acquisition with S&P maintaining a negative outlook, raising credibility questions about fulfilling the remaining $7.8 billion investment promise despite stable operations and strong 2026 profit projections.

PE consolidation in youth sports is accelerating despite regulatory headwinds—FlexWork Sports' ProCamps acquisition signals operators see value in scale, but bipartisan congressional scrutiny could reshape deal economics in this emerging category.

Enterprise software for compliance and monitoring remains well-funded (LeapXpert's $180M round), while deep tech in semiconductors and industrial applications continues attracting institutional capital across geographies.
newsletters.originalTitle: Axios Pro Rata: Steely promises

newsletters.title: Benedict Evans
newsletters.summary: Benedict Evans examines fundamental limitations of AI models, arguing that certain tasks fall outside their effective scope because required knowledge isn't captured in training data or can't be easily articulated by humans. The newsletter also covers recent US government model restrictions on Anthropic and OpenAI, highlighting policy incoherence, and introduces Larridin, a tool measuring enterprise AI spending and ROI as corporate AI investment grows 13x year-over-year.
keyTakeaways: AI automation potential is bounded by what can be documented or inferred from training data—many human workflows involve tacit knowledge that's difficult to explicit or explain, making certain job categories less vulnerable to displacement than headline claims suggest.

Enterprise AI spending is accelerating rapidly (13x YoY growth), but most organizations lack visibility into actual ROI and AI fluency, creating a market opportunity for measurement and attribution tools that can justify continued investment.

Regulatory fragmentation and arbitrary government restrictions on model access are creating unpredictable capital allocation risks for AI companies, punishing safety-focused players like Anthropic despite their compliance efforts, while clarity on export controls and approved use cases remains absent.
newsletters.originalTitle: Benedict's Newsletter: No. 649

newsletters.title: Latitude Media
newsletters.summary: Latitude Media is hosting a discussion one year after the GOP's One Big Beautiful Bill (OBBB) became law, examining what's preventing clean energy projects from moving forward despite record development levels. The conversation will cover specific bottlenecks including permitting lawsuits, tariff impacts on solar and wind economics, unclear FEOC tax credit guidance, and whether data center demand can offset federal headwinds. Jason Clark, founder and CEO of Power Brief, will join to discuss where projects are actually getting stuck in the system.
keyTakeaways: Permitting and regulatory delays are emerging as the primary constraint on clean energy deployment post-OBBB, not capital availability—investors should focus on companies solving permitting acceleration, environmental review optimization, or tariff cost modeling.

Clean energy-specific tariffs are materially reshaping project economics for solar and wind; investors need updated underwriting models and should track companies with supply chain diversification or domestic manufacturing solutions.

Incomplete FEOC (Foreign Entity Ownership Concern) rules create qualification uncertainty for tax credits—a key lever in OBBB economics—making regulatory clarity a critical risk factor for venture and growth-stage cleantech companies relying on federal incentives.
newsletters.originalTitle: Joining us tomorrow?

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: A Supreme Court ruling allowing President Trump to fire independent agency commissioners without cause could fundamentally reshape energy and climate policy governance. The decision affects key regulatory bodies including the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC), Nuclear Regulatory Commission, and SEC, potentially undermining decades of bipartisan consensus and deliberative processes that Congress designed to insulate technical expertise from political pressure.
keyTakeaways: Presidential authority over independent agencies is now dramatically expanded, enabling rapid restructuring of regulatory commissions that oversee power markets, transmission infrastructure, and natural gas—creating significant policy uncertainty for energy sector investors and operators.

FERC's historic culture of bipartisan cooperation and technical deliberation is at risk, which could accelerate or impede energy transition investments depending on administrative priorities—making regulatory risk a primary factor in energy infrastructure and renewable energy project valuations.

The ruling represents a structural shift in how Congress can constrain executive power through agency design, weakening the independent commission model that has governed technical policy domains since the 19th century and potentially destabilizing long-term energy policy frameworks.
newsletters.originalTitle: Congress Never Meant to Design This

newsletters.title: Maddyness
newsletters.summary: French startups raised €706 million last week with an average ticket size of €64 million, driven partly by four AI-focused companies that secured €45 million combined. Major exits and funding rounds including Walmart's acquisition of Vibe, Alan's mega-funding round, and French Slip's upcoming IPO signal momentum in the French tech ecosystem, while the Raise Summit's third edition highlights growing European VC activity centered in Paris.
keyTakeaways: AI-driven startups are capturing disproportionate capital in France, with four companies securing €45 million of the €706 million raised last week, suggesting investor conviction in AI as a core investment thesis despite acknowledged governance and risk management gaps.

Large ticket sizes (€64 million average) indicate institutional capital is flowing robustly into European tech, with prestigious exits (Walmart/Vibe) and IPO pipelines (French Slip) providing exit confidence that may accelerate follow-on funding rounds.

Tech leaders are massively adopting generative AI without equivalent risk governance frameworks in place—this regulatory and operational gap represents both a due diligence risk for investors and a market opportunity for compliance/safety-focused startups.
newsletters.originalTitle: 30.06 - De Wall Street à Versailles, le rêve américain des fondateurs du Raise Summit

newsletters.title: Term Sheet
newsletters.summary: Quantum computing investment reached a record $3.9 billion across 125 deals in 2025, with megafunds like BlackRock ($1.7B), Nvidia ($1.6B), and Temasek leading deployments rather than specialist quantum VCs. High-profile exits emerged in early 2026, including Quantinuum's $1.68B Nasdaq IPO and four Q1 deals totaling $5.7B, though most quantum companies remain years from delivering commercially viable applications and face severe talent constraints.
keyTakeaways: Institutional capital is concentrating in a handful of platform bets—Quantinuum and PsiQuantum alone represent massive valuations ($10B and $7B respectively)—while median deal size remains just $9M, creating a two-tier market where early-stage quantum companies struggle for funding.

Exit velocity is accelerating but still fragile: the quantum sector just experienced its first significant liquidity event, yet most exits have been SPAC-style reverse mergers rather than traditional IPOs, signaling continued market skepticism about near-term commercialization timelines.

Geopolitical competition is reshaping capital flows: China's $17.5B government commitment and ranking quantum as its top future industry priority is forcing U.S. and EU strategic responses, creating both national security imperatives and potential regulatory friction that could fragment the global quantum ecosystem.
newsletters.originalTitle: Quantum leap

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Norway's Equinor has exited its offshore wind division in Japan after eight years without securing any leases, following similar exits by Denmark's Orsted and a Mitsubishi-led consortium citing ballooning project costs. The newsletter examines structural headwinds in Japan's offshore wind sector, including regulatory delays under Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's government, which has pivoted toward restarting nuclear reactors instead. Greenland is rejecting a rare earths miner's license renewal amid debates over resource extraction versus sustainable development alternatives.
keyTakeaways: Offshore wind developers are abandoning high-cost markets: After repeated auction failures and cost overruns, major players like Equinor and Orsted are reallocating capital away from Japan, signaling that not all coastal, high-density markets support viable wind economics—a critical lesson for renewable energy investors evaluating geographic expansion.

Policy risk is a major capital destroyer: Japan's policy shift toward nuclear over offshore wind, combined with postponed funding procedures, demonstrates how government pivot can strand developer investments; investors should scrutinize political stability and commitment to renewable energy roadmaps before committing to long-cycle projects.

Rare earths and critical minerals face scrutiny beyond geology: Greenland's rejection of mining licenses reveals that Indigenous sovereignty, environmental concerns, and alternative economic models (biotech, tourism) are reshaping resource extraction economics; ESG-conscious investors may find better risk-adjusted returns in jurisdictions with clearer stakeholder alignment.
newsletters.originalTitle: Sayonara, Equinor

newsletters.title: Pro Rata
newsletters.summary: Comcast announced a tax-free spinoff splitting its media and entertainment assets (NBCUniversal and Sky) from its telecom business, with executives denying immediate M&A plans despite widespread speculation about follow-on deals. In the space sector, Rocket Lab agreed to acquire Iridium Communications for $8 billion, combining launch capabilities with Iridium's 66-satellite constellation to compete with SpaceX's integrated business model.
keyTakeaways: The Comcast split unlocks significant downstream M&A opportunities: NBCUniversal could acquire content libraries like Lionsgate or become a takeover target itself, while the telecom entity may pursue Charter (post-Cox merger) or smaller wireless/broadband providers—despite management's public denials of M&A plans.

Leadership changes signal execution capability: Cavanagh (JPMorgan, Carlyle Group) will lead NBCUniversal while Angelakis (ex-Comcast CFO, currently at PE firm Atairos) takes the telecom helm, but Roberts' continued involvement and fraught Trump relationship could constrain major acquisition strategies.

The Rocket Lab-Iridium deal validates the space economy consolidation trend by combining launch, manufacturing, and satellite network operations, potentially threatening SpaceX's end-to-end business model and signaling increased competition for space infrastructure dominance.
newsletters.originalTitle: Axios Pro Rata: Comcast splits

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: A Supreme Court decision on Monday significantly expands Trump's executive power over FERC, the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission, an agency that was already experiencing diminished independence. The ruling consolidates presidential control over energy infrastructure decisions and regulatory oversight, potentially reshaping how major energy projects and utilities are governed in the United States.
keyTakeaways: Regulatory risk for energy infrastructure projects has fundamentally shifted—FERC's quasi-independent status is now compromised, meaning executive decisions could override traditional regulatory processes for approvals of pipelines, transmission lines, and power generation facilities.

Utility companies and renewable energy developers should expect faster but more politically volatile project approvals; conversely, regulatory predictability—a key institutional feature of energy markets—has deteriorated, creating valuation and risk assessment challenges for long-term energy infrastructure investments.

The consolidation of executive power over energy regulation signals a major pivot away from the administrative state model that governed energy markets for decades, with implications for climate policy implementation, grid modernization investments, and the competitive positioning of different energy sources.
newsletters.originalTitle: FERC Was Already Losing Its Independence. Now It’s Gone.

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Europe's low air conditioning adoption during deadly heat waves may stem not from cultural resistance but from a structural obstacle: the continent's superior tilt-turn window technology, which provides better insulation and ventilation than American sash windows. This infrastructure incompatibility could create unexpected opportunities for window manufacturers and HVAC companies willing to redesign products for European markets, rather than the assumed wave of AC adoption following climate patterns seen in the Pacific Northwest.
keyTakeaways: Europe's building infrastructure (tilt-turn windows) is fundamentally incompatible with window-unit AC systems, creating a structural barrier that capital allocators have overlooked; companies solving this design challenge could capture significant market share as European heat waves intensify.

The AC adoption narrative assumes rational consumer choice driven by temperature, but actual adoption depends on retrofitting existing building stock—suggesting opportunities in renovation/retrofit companies and integrated HVAC-window solutions rather than standalone AC manufacturers.

Climate change is forcing infrastructure modernization across Europe, but the path forward likely involves building envelope upgrades (windows, insulation) rather than energy-intensive AC—creating a macro shift toward efficiency-focused climate adaptation over cooling-focused solutions.
newsletters.originalTitle: An Alternative Theory of Europe’s AC Problems

newsletters.title: Maddyness
newsletters.summary: French tech ecosystem experiences major momentum with three significant announcements: Walmart's acquisition of Vibe, Alan's mega-funding round, and Le Slip Français's upcoming IPO. The newsletter also highlights infrastructure challenges in AI competitiveness, noting that France's power grid modernization is critical for supporting the computational demands of artificial intelligence development and maintaining technological sovereignty.
keyTakeaways: M&A and public market exits are accelerating in French tech, signaling investor confidence and liquidity events that could reshape capital deployment in the ecosystem

Energy infrastructure is becoming a competitive moat for AI leadership—companies and investors should prioritize opportunities in power grid modernization and sustainable energy solutions supporting data centers
Entrepreneurship is gaining credibility as a career path amid labor market instability, suggesting growing talent availability for startups and reduced friction for recruiting founders

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: Digital marketplaces like iCapital and CAIS have dominated as the 'operating system' connecting wealth advisers to alternative investments, but a new wave of competitors including custodian platforms like Altruist is challenging their grip by offering direct access to private funds from mega-managers like Blackstone and KKR. Meanwhile, Asia-Pacific's private markets are experiencing selective liquidity recovery in 2026, with exit values rising for both PE and VC-backed companies, though improvements remain concentrated among larger, established managers.
keyTakeaways: The competition to control RIA access to private markets is intensifying—if Altruist's custodian-based marketplace model succeeds, it could disrupt iCapital and CAIS's duopoly by removing middleman friction and offering advisers direct deal flow at scale.

Asia-Pacific exit liquidity is improving but unevenly distributed, suggesting capital allocation opportunities favor proven managers with strong track records while smaller or earlier-stage firms may face continued fundraising challenges.

The trend toward integrating private and public assets in advisory portfolios is creating infrastructure battles that will determine which platforms control distribution to the wealth channel—a winner-take-most dynamic worth monitoring for LP and platform investors.
newsletters.originalTitle: Winning the narrow door to alts

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: Investment in AI video generation startups has tripled over five years, reaching $5.6 billion in 2026—a 43% increase from 2025. While competition intensifies among video-generation model developers like Runway and Luma AI, including new Chinese competitors, capital is now flowing toward downstream applications: Google DeepMind's $75 million investment in A24 exemplifies a broader market shift toward studios and creative tools rather than foundational models, suggesting the real value capture is moving beyond the technology layer itself.
keyTakeaways: The AI video market is consolidating around foundational model builders while simultaneously diversifying downstream. Investors should track studio and creative tool partnerships as the next wave of value creation, not just model-building startups.

Commoditization risk is real: as video generation becomes easier to build and competition increases globally, differentiation through technology alone is insufficient. This explains the pivot toward owning creative IP and creator relationships rather than just algorithms.

The macro trend signals a broader pattern in AI investing—early stage funding races toward commodity infrastructure while later-stage capital follows value to application layers and specialized verticals where defensibility exists through content, relationships, or workflows.
newsletters.originalTitle: Why billions are flowing into AI video

newsletters.title: Fintech Business Weekly
newsletters.summary: Coverd, a fintech startup that raised $7.8 million from investors including Andreessen Horowitz's Speedrun accelerator, announced the launch of a credit card offering up to 100% cash back, but its issuing partner Rain has stated the card has never actually gone live. The announcement echoes failed predecessors like Yotta and Bella that made similarly aggressive cashback promises before collapsing, raising questions about the viability of the business model and the due diligence practices of investors backing such ventures.
keyTakeaways: Coverd's claimed product launch appears to be vaporware—the issuing partner explicitly stated the card was never live, indicating either a breakdown in partnership execution or misleading marketing that should concern investors about management credibility and operational competence.

The pattern of failed ultra-high cashback credit card startups (Yotta, Bella, now Coverd) suggests this business model is structurally unsustainable or dependent on unit economics that don't work, yet continues to attract capital, indicating a recurring blind spot in fintech investor due diligence.

Regulatory and partnership risks in fintech are compounding—the newsletter references both the Synapse collapse and ongoing litigation against credit-building platforms, suggesting that bank-fintech partnerships remain fragile and that investors should scrutinize operational dependencies and partner stability before deploying capital.
newsletters.originalTitle: Coverd Is A Late-Stage Capitalism Fever Dream

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: A Heatmap News newsletter covering critical climate and energy infrastructure stories, including data centers' expanding footprint on farmland, 16 gigawatts of residential solar capacity up for acquisition by major players like Sunrun and Tesla, and emerging policy debates around ratepayer protections in the energy transition. The coverage signals intensifying competition in distributed solar and mounting environmental tensions as AI-driven data center demand collides with agricultural land preservation.
keyTakeaways: Data center real estate competition is now directly competing with farmland, creating regulatory and community opposition risks that could impact deployment timelines and costs for hyperscalers and infrastructure investors.

The 16-gigawatt residential solar opportunity represents a significant M&A consolidation play, with established players like Sunrun and Tesla aggressively acquiring distributed energy assets—indicating market maturation and attractive valuations for sellers in the residential solar space.

Ratepayer protection policies are emerging as a critical regulatory variable that could reshape utility economics and distributed energy adoption rates, making policy risk a key due diligence factor for energy infrastructure investors.
newsletters.originalTitle: Data Centers’ Farmland Problem, 16 New Gigawatts, and More

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: SpaceX's June 2026 IPO provides the first public market comparable for valuing frontier AI companies, with implications for Anthropic and OpenAI's upcoming listings. Using an AI Business Quality framework, the analysis suggests Anthropic could be valued at $1.0-$2.8 trillion despite highest business quality scores, while OpenAI ranges $0.5-$1.6 trillion, with the actual outcome dependent on whether the market prices fundamentals or narrative rather than company performance.
keyTakeaways: SpaceX's IPO sets a pricing precedent for AI moonshots, but the comparison is imperfect—Starlink's profitability masks AI segment losses that pure-play labs like Anthropic and OpenAI lack, potentially limiting the scarcity premium transferable to standalone listings

Revenue recognition methodology will be the swing factor at S-1 filing: Anthropic's 20.5x run-rate multiple could expand to 50-60x on recognized revenue, reshaping all subsequent AI company valuations once the filing conversion happens around August

The outcome will determine whether frontier AI IPOs command multiples based on business fundamentals or investor appetite for funding burn—a verdict that sets the template for every AI IPO that follows through 2026-2027
newsletters.originalTitle: How SpaceX prices the AI race

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: Quantum computing has emerged as a record-breaking investment sector with over $1 billion in VC funding across the last three quarters and 60 deals in Q1 2026, driven by hardware breakthroughs and geopolitical competition. Meanwhile, the PE market faces a significant slowdown with large software buyouts frozen following the SaaS sell-off, direct lending LBO volumes down 21% year-to-date, and no mega-deals over $2 billion financed since early March. Investors increasingly believe value will concentrate in quantum infrastructure and enabling technologies rather than complete systems, while Europe's private equity firms now outnumber public companies by a record 2.3x ratio.
keyTakeaways: Quantum computing funding has compressed typical venture timelines with record valuations ($10B pre-money for Quantinuum, $6B for PsiQuantum), signaling capital is betting on infrastructure and pick-and-shovel plays like cryogenic systems and error-correction software rather than integrated quantum computer manufacturers.

PE firms are pulling back from large software acquisitions due to valuation resets, creating a financing drought that has cut direct lending LBO volumes 21% and left private credit markets starved for mega-deals—this shift may force consolidation toward smaller tuck-in acquisitions.

The widening gap between private equity portfolio companies (now 2.3x public firms in Europe) and declining public market liquidity suggests limited exit opportunities, which could pressure deal valuations and returns if the IPO market doesn't recover on macro improvement rather than disclosure policy changes.
newsletters.originalTitle: Quantum leap

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: The U.S. Department of Commerce banned Chinese automaker Polestar from selling new vehicles in the country, marking the first major casualty of Biden-era rules prohibiting Chinese software in internet-connected vehicles due to national security concerns over camera and GPS data exploitation. Texas landowners are challenging a proposed 765-kilovolt transmission line that would span 200 miles, testing whether the state's permitting processes can accommodate large-scale renewable energy infrastructure despite its historically favorable property rights environment.
keyTakeaways: Chinese EV manufacturers face structural barriers to U.S. market entry regardless of product quality, creating opportunities for domestic and allied automakers to capture market share in premium electric vehicles without direct competition from East Asian incumbents.

Texas's renewable energy growth trajectory depends on resolving transmission bottlenecks through regulatory reform, as property rights protections that attract wind and solar developers are now impeding the grid infrastructure necessary to monetize those assets.

Geopolitical technology restrictions are becoming a permanent feature of U.S. industrial policy, signaling that capital allocation in automotive and connected device sectors must account for recurring regulatory intervention on national security grounds.

newsletters.title: Pro Rata
newsletters.summary: General Intuition, an AI lab using gaming content for model training, raised $320 million in Series A at a $2.3 billion valuation led by Khosla Ventures. The company is betting that gameplay video and player inputs can train world models and large action models faster and cheaper than alternative methods, representing the next phase of AI investment expanding into physical economy applications.
keyTakeaways: Gaming-derived training data is emerging as a scalable alternative for AI model development, potentially offering cost and speed advantages over traditional training methods for robotics and physical world applications.

Major institutional investors including Khosla Ventures, General Catalyst, and Bezos Expeditions are betting on gaming-to-AI translation, signaling capital reallocation toward companies bridging digital simulation and physical economy AI.

The shift toward training models on interactive, player-generated data rather than static datasets represents a structural change in AI development methodology, with implications for which startups will dominate embodied AI and robotics applications.

newsletters.title: Latitude Media
newsletters.summary: Latitude Media is hosting a live discussion one year after the GOP's One Big Beautiful Bill became law, examining how clean energy projects are navigating new "foreign entity of concern" provisions, shortened tax credit horizons, and permitting delays. Despite significant regulatory headwinds including tariffs, unfinished Treasury guidance, and looming tax credit sunsets, the industry continues to break deployment records, with data center load growth emerging as a potential market driver.
keyTakeaways: Project deployment is getting stuck in permitting, lawsuits, and appeals rather than collapsing entirely—this suggests regulatory clarity and faster permitting processes could unlock significant capital currently in limbo

The three-decade tax credit regime is ending; investors should model scenarios for post-credit economics and identify which technologies and geographies can sustain deployment without federal subsidies

Data center demand growth is becoming a critical market support mechanism—investors should track whether this load growth can offset federal policy headwinds and which clean energy sub-sectors benefit most from this shift

newsletters.title: Deep Tech Newsletter
newsletters.summary: The Trump administration issued two executive orders to accelerate quantum computing development, directing federal agencies to deploy a research-capable quantum computer by 2028 and prepare security defenses against quantum threats. The move coincides with billions in Commerce Department funding and major private sector investments from IBM, Microsoft, and Google, positioning quantum as a complementary technology to AI advances.
keyTakeaways: Quantum computing has shifted from pure R&D to government-backed commercialization with hard 2028 deployment deadline, signaling investor confidence and reducing technology risk for quantum startups and their backers

The dual focus on capability (research-grade systems) and security (encryption-resistant threats) creates adjacent market opportunities in quantum security infrastructure and post-quantum cryptography solutions across both government and private sector

Quantum is being positioned as an AI-complementary technology rather than a replacement, suggesting a multi-year capital cycle where quantum companies can capture value alongside continued AI infrastructure buildout rather than compete for the same funding

newsletters.title: Term Sheet
newsletters.summary: Framework Ventures closed a $400 million fourth fund, expanding its investment mandate beyond blockchain to include AI, robotics, and energy as founders increasingly pursue these areas. The firm, which manages $1.28 billion in AUM and was an early backer of DeFi protocols like Aave and Chainlink, has already deployed roughly half of the new capital into non-crypto bets including robotics startup Mecka AI. This shift reflects a broader industry trend as crypto-focused VCs like Paradigm and Haun Ventures similarly diversify into AI and adjacent sectors amid Bitcoin's recent downturn and surging valuations in generative AI companies.
keyTakeaways: Established crypto VCs with strong track records are now mainstream diversifiers—Framework's $400M fund demonstrates that institutional LPs (endowments, sovereign wealth funds) will back crypto-native managers pivoting to AI, validating cross-sector thesis investing

The flow of founder talent is driving capital reallocation: crypto VCs are explicitly following their networks into AI rather than chasing hype, suggesting sustained conviction in non-crypto frontier tech over the next 5-7 year fund cycle

Crypto market weakness is accelerating consolidation of VC expertise—as digital assets plateau relative to AI valuations, the best-capitalized crypto funds are becoming generalist frontier tech investors, potentially reducing future liquidity for pure-play blockchain startups

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Europe's air conditioning penetration remains significantly below North America despite being the world's fastest warming continent, with only 19% of European homes having AC compared to 88% in the US and 70% in Canada. The gap presents a substantial market opportunity as AC adoption has more than doubled since 1990, particularly in southern Europe where over 50% of Italian households now have cooling, yet vulnerable populations like the elderly remain largely unprotected—only 3% of UK households with residents over 75 have AC despite their heightened heat mortality risk.
keyTakeaways: Massive addressable market: Europe's 19% AC penetration versus 88% in the US represents billions in potential revenue for HVAC manufacturers and installers as climate-driven demand accelerates, especially in southern European countries where adoption has doubled in less than a decade

Regulatory and infrastructure gaps create investment opportunities in retrofitting, building technology, and grid capacity solutions—current European AC use represents only 0.8% of household energy versus 6% in the US, indicating both adoption headroom and need for electrical grid upgrades

Demographic vulnerability creates policy-driven urgency: Only 3% of elderly UK households have AC despite their acute heat-mortality risk, likely triggering government subsidies and public health spending that could accelerate market penetration through incentive programs and health-focused mandates
newsletters.originalTitle: Heatmap Daily: 6 Facts About Europe and Air Conditioning

newsletters.title: Maddyness
newsletters.summary: Le Slip Français plans to list on Euronext Growth on July 14th, aiming to raise €5 million while enabling existing investors to sell €7 million in shares, despite pandemic-related struggles. Alan, France's health insurance unicorn, raised €480 million in its latest funding round just three months after a €100 million raise, reaching a €5.5 billion valuation. Hightouch, a Silicon Valley martech startup valued at €2.75 billion, made a €1.2 billion acquisition offer for Publicis's LiveRamp Onboarding unit, positioning itself as a competitor to Adobe and Salesforce.
keyTakeaways: Secondary market exits are accelerating for early-stage investors even in struggling companies, as Le Slip Français demonstrates the importance of liquidity events beyond traditional acquisition or IPO paths for VC portfolio companies.

Rapid back-to-back funding rounds at unicorn scale (Alan raised €580 million in 3 months) suggest capital remains abundant for proven business models despite economic headwinds, creating validation for health tech and insurtech sectors.

Martech consolidation is being driven by well-funded startups acquiring established assets to compete against incumbent giants—Hightouch's aggressive move signals that venture-backed scale-ups view strategic acquisitions as faster paths to market dominance than organic growth.

newsletters.summary: Humanoid robotics are experiencing record momentum with Agility Robotics pursuing a $2.5 billion SPAC valuation as Q1 2026 marks the strongest quarter for the sector. Meanwhile, fitness wearables Whoop and Oura are competing aggressively at the World Cup, with Whoop raising $575 million at a $10.1 billion valuation and Oura preparing for a US IPO after securing $907 million. The SEC is intensifying scrutiny of PE continuation funds—an increasingly popular mechanism as exits have declined—potentially forcing sponsors to demonstrate more rigorous price testing, conflict management, and investor disclosure.
keyTakeaways: Robotics and physical AI represent a high-momentum investment opportunity with record deal activity in Q1 2026, signaling strong LP appetite and potential exit pathways through SPAC transactions at substantial valuations.

Consumer hardware companies are leveraging elite sports partnerships as a critical go-to-market strategy; investors should evaluate the durability of athlete endorsements versus actual product superiority when assessing fitness wearable valuations.

PE sponsors using continuation funds face regulatory headwinds that will require more transparent valuation methodologies and conflict-of-interest frameworks, potentially increasing operational costs but also reducing valuation dispute risks at exit.

Century Aluminum, developing America's first new smelter in 50 years, has partnered with a startup for alumina supply, signaling critical infrastructure investments in clean energy and domestic manufacturing.
keyTakeaways: Data center regulation is becoming a serious legislative concern with senior Democratic leadership backing moratoriums, creating near-term uncertainty for AI infrastructure investments and cloud computing expansion plans dependent on grid capacity.

Geopolitical energy shocks disproportionately impact rural and energy-dependent Republican states, creating political pressure that could reshape energy policy and accelerate demand for grid modernization and alternative energy sources.

Domestic aluminum and critical mineral production is consolidating around greenfield projects, signaling investor appetite for supply chain resilience plays and the potential for long-term returns in advanced manufacturing and energy-intensive industries.

newsletters.title: Term Sheet
newsletters.summary: Lux Capital cofounder Josh Wolfe predicts Warren Buffett's death could trigger a Nasdaq crisis and resurgence of value investing, as index investing has artificially inflated mega-cap tech stocks. He also warns of a 10% probability scenario where Iran war-induced fertilizer scarcity creates food crises in peripheral European nations like Macedonia and Bulgaria, potentially destabilizing the EU and empowering far-right political movements.
keyTakeaways: A 10-15% Nasdaq correction concentrated in the Magnificent 7 could create outsized opportunities for active value investors, potentially reversing a decade-long dominance of passive growth strategies that have artificially bolstered the largest tech companies.

Geopolitical fragility, particularly around Iran, poses underestimated tail risks to European stability through commodity supply chains—fertilizer scarcity could cascade into migration crises and currency devaluation, warranting scenario planning for macro-hedge positions.

The shift from passive to active investing and the rise of political instability in Europe represent structural market disruptions that could reshape capital allocation patterns and create both risks and entry points for contrarian investors over the next 18 months.

newsletters.title: Pro Rata
newsletters.summary: Global M&A hit a record $2.7 trillion in the first half of 2026, up 47% year-over-year, driven by mega-deals over $5 billion which now represent over half the volume. However, the actual number of deals dropped 10% globally and 20% in the U.S., signaling a bifurcated market where larger transactions dominate while smaller deals struggle. Private equity bucked the trend with $583 billion deployed across 5,729 deals despite expectations for interest rate cuts that never materialized.
keyTakeaways: Deal count is collapsing while deal size balloons—this suggests consolidation dynamics favor large, well-capitalized sponsors who can move quickly on mega-deals, creating headwinds for mid-market and smaller transactions.

The $2.7 trillion headline masks weak fundamentals when adjusted for inflation, and historical patterns show the market peaked in similar years before declining sharply, suggesting caution on deploying remaining capital into H2 2026.

Private equity's resilience (+54% YoY) despite no Fed rate cuts indicates PE sponsors are confident enough to deploy capital aggressively on valuations, signaling either strong dry powder or competitive pressure to deploy before markets cool.

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Europe is experiencing record-breaking heat waves with France hitting 110°F and warm nights above 79°F in Germany, killing hundreds and highlighting the deadliest impacts of climate change. Oil and gas executives are increasingly concerned about data centers as major energy consumers competing with AI development, drawing parallels to how fracking became politically polarized across red and blue states in the 2010s. This suggests emerging political tensions around infrastructure needed for AI advancement, with potential implications for energy policy and regional investment patterns.
keyTakeaways: Data center energy demand is becoming a primary strategic concern for oil and gas executives, positioning energy infrastructure as critical to AI competitiveness—this creates investment opportunities in power generation and grid modernization companies serving data centers.

The fracking analogy suggests data center infrastructure could become politically polarized by geography, with blue states potentially restricting development while red states embrace it, creating regional capital allocation opportunities and risks.

Climate-driven mortality events in developed economies (France, UK, Germany) are accelerating investment in climate adaptation infrastructure like air conditioning and cooling systems, representing a significant new market alongside traditional decarbonization spending.

Meanwhile, Hightouch, a Silicon Valley-founded martech platform valued at $2.75 billion, made a surprise $1.2 billion acquisition offer for Publicis' LiveRamp Onboarding unit, signaling aggressive consolidation ambitions in the competitive martech sector against Adobe and Salesforce.
keyTakeaways: Alan's rapid consecutive fundraising (€100M + €480M in 3 months) demonstrates strong investor appetite for European healthtech despite macro headwinds, though the company remains secretive on capital deployment specifics—watch for expansion announcements

Hightouch's unsolicited acquisition bid reveals martech consolidation is heating up, with well-funded startups now competing directly with enterprise software giants through strategic M&A rather than organic growth alone

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: US private equity platform LBOs have fallen to 19% of deal activity through May 2026, well below the predicted 25%, as sponsors face tariff uncertainty, wider credit spreads, and heightened scrutiny of portfolio company quality. In response, dealmakers are pivoting toward smaller add-on and growth equity deals, with add-ons climbing to 74.7% of buyout activity—a post-2023 high. Meanwhile, robotics startups set a VC funding record at $16.3 billion in Q1 2026, while SaaS buyers continue targeting financial management systems for acquisition rather than viewing AI as a disruptive threat to enterprise software.
keyTakeaways: Platform LBO weakness signals sponsors are fundamentally reassessing risk appetite, not just being tactical—this could reshape fund deployment strategies despite $1.1 trillion in available dry powder
The shift toward smaller add-on deals may indicate sponsors are conserving capacity and relying on existing credit lines, suggesting capital concentration among largest managers will accelerate

Robotics' record $16.3B VC funding and continued CFO software acquisition appetite point to divergent sector dynamics—physical AI moving to deployment while software automation is seen as complementary rather than cannibalistic to human finance roles
