The event features speakers from utilities (Xcel Energy, ERCOT), energy companies (Octopus Energy, NRG), and emerging flexibility platforms (Base Power, Align Energy Advisors), signaling investor interest in scaling virtual power plants and AI-driven grid solutions.

The convergence of DER proliferation, grid resilience demands, and political affordability pressure is creating a policy tailwind for scaling distributed flexibility—companies addressing all three factors simultaneously have strongest investment thesis.

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: A recent Iran conflict has exposed a fundamental restructuring of global energy markets into two competing regimes: the traditional oil-and-gas system dominated by OPEC+ and North America, versus an emerging electricity-and-batteries system centered in East Asia, particularly China. China's dramatic reduction in oil imports from 11 million to 7.8 million barrels per day during the conflict, driven by domestic stockpiles, EV adoption, and renewable energy capacity, demonstrates that the clean energy transition is no longer theoretical but operationally competitive against traditional fossil fuels.
keyTakeaways: China's EV and battery manufacturing dominance is now proven competitive infrastructure, not aspiration—the country displaced 3+ million barrels of daily oil demand through electrification, suggesting massive capital reallocation opportunities in EV supply chains and battery technology companies serving that ecosystem.

Two rival energy systems are in direct competition for consumer demand; investors should position for winners in the electricity-and-batteries regime (solar manufacturers, battery makers, EV producers) while reassessing exposure to traditional oil-dependent supply chains facing structural demand destruction.

Geopolitical energy security is shifting from oil chokepoints (Strait of Hormuz) to rare earth minerals and battery material supply chains concentrated in East Asia—this represents a multi-trillion dollar reshuffling of global energy infrastructure investment over the next decade.

Space-tech sector valuations are rising sharply post-SpaceX IPO, suggesting early VC investors in the space industry face potential exit upside, while also signaling LP willingness to allocate more capital to the sector—a positive signal for space-focused fund-raising.

newsletters.summary: SpaceX launched on Nasdaq today at $135 per share ($1.75 trillion valuation), significantly below earlier $2 trillion expectations but above Morningstar's $780 billion valuation. The IPO's performance will set the tone for upcoming AI company public offerings including Anthropic and OpenAI, with investors scrutinizing whether the largest IPO ever can maintain its price amid slim float, heavy retail demand, and a rolling lockup structure.
keyTakeaways: SpaceX's IPO valuation ($1.75T) versus Morningstar's assessment ($780B) signals a 2.2x valuation gap that could indicate either market exuberance or the market pricing in Starlink's profitability trajectory—watch Q2 earnings for subscription churn metrics to validate the premium.

The company's $29.1B debt load and massive capex requirements ($10.1B in Q1, with $7.72B in AI capex) mean profitability is likely 2-3 years away; investors should prioritize management guidance on cash burn rates and path to positive operating cash flow over near-term earnings.

SpaceX's successful pricing sets the floor for the 2026 IPO pipeline—Anthropic and OpenAI's valuations now hinge on whether SpaceX holds its $135 opening price, making this a bellwether moment for AI infrastructure and AI model company valuations in a volatile macro environment.
newsletters.originalTitle: It's launch day

newsletters.title: Deep Tech Newsletter
newsletters.summary: A Ukrainian defense official revealed that fully autonomous AI-controlled drones killed soldiers in combat for the first time during a 2024 test involving 10 quadcopter 'Terminator' drones that could operate independently without human oversight or video feed monitoring. The newsletter covers multiple deep tech developments including Avalanche's desktop fusion reactor, NASA's X-59 supersonic aircraft, New York's first-in-nation data center moratorium, and China's investment in large nuclear reactors—signaling accelerating competition in autonomous weapons, energy technology, and AI infrastructure.
keyTakeaways: Autonomous weapons systems have moved from theoretical to operational: investors should monitor regulatory responses and defense contractor pivots toward AI integration, as autonomous lethal capability will likely reshape defense spending and geopolitical dynamics

Infrastructure bottlenecks are emerging as critical capital allocation points—data center moratoria, fusion energy development, and AI-optimized satellite systems represent competing bets on which technologies will enable next-generation AI deployment

The shift toward autonomous systems across defense, energy, and logistics (robotic meal prep, autonomous drones) indicates a broader market inflection where autonomy ROI is becoming demonstrable, attracting capital to specialized hardware and AI software providers

newsletters.title: Term Sheet
newsletters.summary: SpaceX's first employee Tom Mueller believes the company's $1.75 trillion IPO valuation is justified by Elon Musk's vertically-integrated stack combining cheap rockets, Starlink's proven space computing, and AI data assets—a bet on compute and data centers moving to space. Mueller predicts the stock will follow Tesla's pattern: strong opening, a "valley of death" during schedule slips, then recovery, with the key difference being that SpaceX avoids quarterly earnings pressures as a public company.
keyTakeaways: SpaceX's IPO valuation hinges on a thesis that space-based compute and data centers will become critical infrastructure—investors should view this as a multi-decade bet on Musk's ability to execute vertical integration rather than near-term profitability (2025 saw $18.67B revenue but $4.9B net loss).

Public market scrutiny will test SpaceX's timeline execution similar to Tesla's path; companies with ambitious space or satellite ambitions should prepare for volatility when development delays become visible to institutional investors who lack patience for long R&D cycles.

The SpaceX IPO validates the broader space economy thesis and accelerates competition in orbital services and data center infrastructure; this signals venture capital should recalibrate allocation toward space-adjacent companies, particularly satellite operations, orbital logistics (like Mueller's Impulse Space), and earth observation.

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: SpaceX achieved a historic $1.7 trillion IPO valuation on the Nasdaq after raising $75 billion, positioning itself as a leading satellite internet provider with plans to launch an extraterrestrial data center by 2028. The Department of Energy reversed its terminations of 11 clean energy grants to Democratic-leaning states after a federal court ruled the cancellations violated equal protection rights, while Michigan regulators are being advised to reject Consumers Energy's sale of 13 hydroelectric dams to private equity due to public interest concerns. These developments highlight growing institutional capital flowing into climate tech, renewable energy infrastructure, and the emergence of a new "climate tech mafia" around deep tech innovations.
keyTakeaways: SpaceX's mega-IPO validates the venture ecosystem around Elon Musk's ventures, signaling that deep tech and satellite infrastructure are attracting massive institutional capital; investors should monitor the competitive landscape for alternative satellite internet and space-based computing platforms.

The Energy Department's forced reversal of grant cancellations eliminates political risk in clean energy project funding, but highlights that federal support remains subject to legal challenge—deal-makers should stress-test government backing and consider diversified funding sources.

Hydropower is experiencing a capital influx from institutional investors (Google's 3GW deal, Hull Street's First Light acquisition, private equity interest), but regulatory pushback on privatization suggests that public interest safeguards will constrain returns and deal structures in this sector.

newsletters.title: Pro Rata
newsletters.summary: SpaceX completed the largest IPO in history at $75 billion after 24 years and $30 billion in VC funding, now trading on Nasdaq under ticker SPCX. The newsletter explores orbital data centers as SpaceX's next growth frontier, where companies like Starcloud, Cowboy Space Corp, and tech giants are developing space-based compute infrastructure to address terrestrial constraints including energy costs, permitting challenges, and growing AI demand. Key technical hurdles remain around cooling systems and launch economics, with realistic timelines suggesting modest experiments within 5 years and scaled installations within 10 years.
keyTakeaways: SpaceX's $75B IPO validates the commercial space economy thesis after decades of patient capital; investors should monitor whether the company can execute on orbital data center vision as a multi-decade revenue stream beyond launch services.

Space data centers represent a capital-intensive bet on solving two problems: terrestrial real estate/permitting constraints and AI's energy demands. This sector will require breakthrough improvements in launch costs and thermal management before achieving unit economics—making it a high-risk, long-duration investment thesis.

The shift of major listings from London to New York (Flutter Entertainment delisting) signals broader capital market consolidation, suggesting growth-stage companies increasingly prioritize liquidity and valuation multiples over geographic diversification.

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: The Trump administration has filed to dismiss its appeal of a December court ruling that overturned its offshore wind permitting freeze, effectively conceding this regulatory battle. This reversal signals a potential opening for offshore wind development to proceed, despite the administration's broader opposition to the sector.
keyTakeaways: Offshore wind developers and their investors may now face fewer regulatory headwinds, as the administration's legal challenge to permit approvals has been abandoned, potentially unlocking stalled projects.

This retreat suggests political or legal constraints are limiting the administration's ability to block renewable energy infrastructure through executive action, which could shift capital allocation toward offshore wind companies and supply chain participants.

The decision indicates potential instability in energy policy under this administration—regulatory whiplash between permitting freezes and reversals creates uncertainty that may impact long-term investment decisions in offshore wind infrastructure.

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: SpaceX completed its IPO on June 12, 2026, raising $75 billion with shares surging 19% on the first trading day, making Elon Musk the world's first trillionaire. The article examines how Musk built a venture-backed hardware manufacturing model and highlights the critical role of government support—including a $465 million Department of Energy loan to Tesla in 2010 and NASA's 2008 commercial resupply contract for SpaceX—in enabling these companies to survive early scaling phases. The piece also explores how SpaceX and Tesla have created a new generation of climate tech founders and executives who are spreading manufacturing-focused, efficiency-driven management practices across the clean energy sector.
keyTakeaways: The SpaceX IPO demonstrates that large-scale government contracts and loan guarantees remain essential for de-risking hardware-intensive climate and energy startups—addressing the so-called 'missing middle' in deep tech financing that venture capital alone cannot solve.

SpaceX and Tesla have become talent pipelines and management culture incubators for climate tech startups, suggesting that acquiring or partnering with alumni from these companies could accelerate manufacturing execution and operational efficiency in clean energy ventures.

The emergence of trillionaire-scale wealth in hardware/manufacturing (vs. pure software) signals a potential shift in venture and public market preferences toward capital-intensive, hard tech solutions for climate and energy challenges.

The funding reflects growing investor appetite for enterprise AI infrastructure plays that help companies implement AI solutions at scale.
keyTakeaways: European AI infrastructure and adoption platforms are attracting significant capital; Mendo's €12M round demonstrates strong investor confidence in B2B AI enablement solutions addressing the enterprise adoption gap.

The funding targets geographic expansion across Europe and headcount doubling, suggesting venture capital is actively backing European tech companies competing in the global AI infrastructure race rather than consolidating into US-dominated markets.

This mirrors broader M&A activity in the AI/commerce tech stack (Akeneo acquiring Pricing Hub), indicating a wave of consolidation and growth in vertical AI solutions as enterprises move beyond AI experimentation toward production deployment.

newsletters.summary: Solar energy surpassed coal for the first time in U.S. history in May 2026, generating 12.8% of electricity versus coal's 12.2%, with solar hitting an all-time high of 45.5 terawatt-hours. Meanwhile, Texas Governor Greg Abbott is pushing for major data center restrictions including mandatory power generation contributions, closed-loop water systems, and elimination of sales tax exemptions—a significant shift in the state's business-friendly stance that signals growing tension between energy infrastructure demands and grid stability.
keyTakeaways: Solar's historical eclipse of coal validates long-term clean energy thesis and suggests accelerating market share gains; South Korean manufacturer Qcells' Georgia facility operating near full capacity signals manufacturing capacity constraints may limit growth

Texas data center restrictions threaten the economic model for hyperscaler expansion in a historically favorable jurisdiction—investors should model increased capex requirements for power generation and water infrastructure in future data center deployments

newsletters.summary: SpaceX's impending IPO at a $1.5-2 trillion valuation, alongside confidential filings from OpenAI and Anthropic, is poised to create a major liquidity event, but PitchBook data shows approximately 80% of active venture investors have no exposure to these companies. These three firms represent anomalies requiring capital far exceeding private market capacity, while concentrated LP hierarchies will likely consolidate power among top-tier investors with direct access, potentially triggering a multi-year shakeout across venture, buyout, and LP markets.
keyTakeaways: Only 700 of approximately 3,500-4,000 active VCs doing 2+ deals annually are invested in SpaceX, OpenAI, or Anthropic—meaning the majority of the venture industry will miss out on what could be the largest liquidity event since 2015, when $1.5 trillion in total venture value reached public markets

Top-tier LPs with direct exposure to these mega-winners will command disproportionate returns and leverage to access the next wave of AI opportunities, creating a widening gap between mega-funds and mid-market investors competing for deal flow

The ripple effects will extend beyond venture into buyout and LP markets over the next 5-10 years, as the AI impact forces systematic repricing of capital allocation and may trigger consolidation among underperforming funds lacking exposure to transformative technologies

newsletters.title: Pro Rata
newsletters.summary: Jeff Bezos-backed Prometheus, an industrial AI startup led by former Google exec Vik Bajaj, raised $12 billion in Series B funding at a $41 billion valuation, bringing total funding to over $18 billion. The company aims to compress manufacturing development cycles by using AI to optimize pre-production processes and design, with Bezos positioning it as an 'artificial general engineer' for industries like aerospace and medical devices. Meanwhile, OpenAI is reportedly considering price cuts on tokens to compete with Anthropic, potentially triggering a pricing war that could compress margins despite both companies preparing for IPOs.
keyTakeaways: Prometheus represents a massive capital concentration bet on industrial AI infrastructure; at $41 billion post-money valuation with backing from JPMorgan, BlackRock, and Goldman Sachs, it signals institutional investors believe AI-driven manufacturing optimization is a multi-hundred-billion-dollar opportunity.

The reported $100 billion fundraising for an affiliated holding company to acquire legacy industrial companies creates a vertically integrated moat strategy—these acquisitions would both provide training data and become customers, which is more defensible than pure software plays.

The emerging price war between OpenAI and Anthropic in token pricing, driven by corporate cost concerns despite upcoming IPOs, suggests that foundational AI model providers may face structural margin pressure that could reshape the economics of AI infrastructure investments.

newsletters.summary: Neobank Current announced an $80 million Series E funding round led by Springcoast Partners, marking the company's third consecutive year of 70% growth. The round reflects investor confidence in Current's expanded product suite, improved unit economics through AI implementation, and strengthened banking partnerships including an expanded financing facility with Cross River and General Catalyst backing.
keyTakeaways: Current's sustained 70% annual growth for three consecutive years demonstrates that neobanks targeting everyday consumers can achieve scale profitably, signaling a maturing market segment beyond early-stage hype

The company's focus on cost discipline and 'return on tokens' suggests fintech leaders are moving away from growth-at-all-costs models toward efficiency metrics that align with public market expectations, relevant for Series D+ investors planning exit strategies

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Lee Raymond, former ExxonMobil CEO who died in June 2026, is remembered as a world-historic figure in both oil and gas business and climate denial. Under his leadership from 1993-2005, Raymond transformed ExxonMobil into the world's most profitable publicly traded company through the merger with Mobil, while simultaneously orchestrating a multimillion-dollar campaign to fund think tanks and pseudo-scientific groups designed to cast doubt on climate science, despite the company's own internal research confirming fossil fuels' role in climate change.
keyTakeaways: Corporate-funded disinformation campaigns on climate science persisted at scale for decades despite internal knowledge of climate risks, suggesting systemic regulatory and reputational failures that create ongoing liability exposure for energy majors.

ExxonMobil's transformation under Raymond from diversified conglomerate to ruthlessly focused oil and gas supermajor proved extraordinarily profitable in the short term but created long-term stranded asset and litigation risks that investors should factor into energy sector valuations.

The historical pattern of major corporations funding doubt campaigns to protect profitable business models reflects a structural incentive problem in fossil fuel industries that suggests carbon transition investments and clean energy alternatives represent compelling risk-adjusted opportunities.

newsletters.summary: Morpho, a European crypto lending and borrowing platform, has secured a $175 million funding round led by prominent investors including Andreessen Horowitz, Ledger, and Xavier Niel, positioning itself as infrastructure for banks and asset managers to distribute credit on blockchain. The newsletter also covers Europe's expanding tech ecosystem, including Swedish legaltech unicorn Legora's Paris expansion, SoftBank's €45 billion commitment to AI data centers in France's Hauts-de-France region, and guidance for French startups entering European markets.
keyTakeaways: Morpho's $175M raise signals institutional validation of crypto infrastructure as enterprise-grade technology, with traditional VCs and industry players (Ledger) co-investing, indicating potential mainstream adoption pathway for blockchain lending among regulated financial institutions

European tech expansion is accelerating across verticals—legaltech, AI, and crypto—with both foreign capital (SoftBank) and local investors backing regional hubs, suggesting a shift from Paris-centricity to distributed innovation clusters and cross-border European plays

The increasing focus on European expansion challenges and regulatory complexity (highlighted in the Galion Project guide) suggests that scaling across EU borders requires sophisticated localization strategies beyond simple geographic replication, favoring well-capitalized, operationally mature startups

newsletters.title: Latitude Media
newsletters.summary: A new utilities report reveals that 80% of utilities now view onsite power as permanent infrastructure rather than a temporary solution, driven by massive load growth from data centers and AI deployments that are outpacing grid capacity. The research shows that 61% of data center developers have shifted to onsite power as their primary solution due to grid interconnection delays and equipment constraints, signaling a fundamental restructuring of how large loads will be served going forward.
keyTakeaways: Onsite power is transitioning from a stopgap measure to core utility strategy—this represents a structural shift in infrastructure investment priorities and creates opportunities in distributed energy and power generation technologies serving enterprise customers.

Data center demand is driving a decentralized power model: with 61% of developers now relying on onsite solutions and grid timelines slipping, capital should flow toward companies enabling on-premise generation, microgrids, and hybrid power systems rather than traditional grid expansion.

The 80% utility adoption signals policy and regulatory acceptance of distributed power infrastructure, reducing execution risk for companies in the onsite generation space (like Bloom Energy, mentioned as report sponsor) and indicating a multi-year secular shift away from centralized grid dependency.

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: Private equity is deploying record capital into the legal sector through a proven buy-and-build playbook, with PE investment reaching $5.1 billion in 2025—up from $3.6 billion in 2023—as Morgan & Morgan explores a $1 billion minority stake sale. The highly fragmented legal market, particularly in the US with potentially 400,000 law firms, presents a significant consolidation opportunity, though US adoption has lagged due to professional ethics rules that prohibit non-lawyer ownership, forcing PE to use alternative practice structures (similar to those deployed in accounting and healthcare).
keyTakeaways: Morgan & Morgan's exploration of a $1 billion minority stake signals a major inflection point: law firms, the last holdout among professional services, are now opening to outside PE capital at scale, creating a multi-year consolidation cycle opportunity.

The fragmented US legal market—potentially 400,000 firms including many solo practitioners—mirrors the pre-consolidation state of accounting and healthcare, suggesting significant M&A activity and platform building opportunities ahead for PE operators willing to navigate regulatory structures.

Alternative practice structures that separate law firm operations from legal practice are becoming the PE playbook for legal services, replicating the proven model from accounting; expect this structure to accelerate deal flow and regulatory acceptance across US jurisdictions.

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: Anthropic's upcoming IPO prospectus will disclose the first audited gross margin from a frontier AI lab, a metric expected to reset valuations across the $1.8 trillion AI infrastructure debate and directly impact OpenAI's eventual pricing. With ARR rocketing to $47 billion by mid-2026 and gross margins tracking toward 44%, Anthropic's $965 billion valuation hinges on whether margins sustain above 35%—a miss would compress valuation by 70-81% and hit OpenAI harder given its worse margin profile and Microsoft revenue-sharing obligations.
keyTakeaways: Anthropic's gross margin disclosure (expected to be ~44%) will be the single most important benchmark for AI infrastructure multiples and will immediately recalibrate OpenAI's IPO pricing, as it's the one hard metric no investor has previously seen from a frontier AI lab.

A gross margin print below 35% would trigger a 70-81% valuation compression on Anthropic and disproportionately damage OpenAI's valuation due to its weaker margin profile and 20% revenue share owed to Microsoft, making this the key risk factor for AI infrastructure investors.

The $965 billion valuation assumes 40-50% gross margins and $345-450 billion 2030 revenue; while the prospectus can validate margins, it cannot confirm the revenue projection, leaving significant pricing uncertainty despite strong unit economics (eight Fortune 10 companies, 1,000+ enterprise customers paying $1M+ annually).

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: South Korea's Qcells has begun solar cell production at its new Georgia factory, marking progress toward completing America's only vertically integrated solar manufacturing plant with expected full capacity by Q3 2026. Meanwhile, global grid investments are projected to exceed $650 billion in 2026, up 5% year-over-year, driven by the need to integrate rapidly growing wind and solar capacity that is expected to comprise nearly half of global generation by 2040.
keyTakeaways: Qcells' Cartersville facility represents a significant bet on U.S. solar manufacturing reshoring, with the factory now producing 16,700 panels daily and approaching full capacity—signaling potential for domestic supply chain consolidation and reduced tariff exposure for manufacturers betting on American production.

The $650 billion global grid investment opportunity reflects a structural, multi-year capital requirement as utilities upgrade infrastructure to handle distributed renewable generation, creating sustained demand for transformer and switchgear manufacturers alongside grid modernization technology providers.

The combination of tariff protection, manufacturing incentives, and grid upgrade requirements is creating a favorable market environment for solar and renewable energy infrastructure investors, with wind and solar expected to grow from 2% of global generation in 2010 to nearly 50% by 2040.

newsletters.title: Term Sheet
newsletters.summary: U.S. government funding cuts to health research are creating a competitive disadvantage as AI transforms drug discovery, according to leaders from Lila Sciences and NVIDIA speaking at Fortune Brainstorm Tech. The AI drug discovery market is valued at $3.25 billion and growing 26% annually toward a projected $10 billion by 2031, with major companies like Isomorphic Labs raising substantial capital while international competitors in Europe and Asia accelerate their investments.
keyTakeaways: AI drug discovery is entering a critical inflection point where government R&D funding decisions made now will determine whether U.S. biotech maintains technological leadership, suggesting investors should monitor policy shifts as a key risk factor for portfolio companies in this space.

The market dynamics favor well-capitalized AI-first biotech platforms (like Lila Sciences' $550M raise and Isomorphic Labs' $2.1B Series B) that can run continuous AI agents across scientific workflows, indicating consolidation risk for smaller competitors and opportunity in foundation model infrastructure like NVIDIA's biology models.

Regulatory approval pathways for AI-designed molecules remain unresolved, with FDA acceptance potentially hinging on digital twin biology models achieving sufficient precision—this represents both a technical bottleneck and a significant moat once solved, affecting time-to-market for early-stage drug discovery ventures.

The solution addresses a massive installed base of century-old heating infrastructure across New York City by improving efficiency without requiring full replacement, highlighting a viable business model in the climate tech sector.

The business model targets existing infrastructure rather than requiring capital-intensive replacement, suggesting lower customer acquisition friction and faster payback periods—attractive unit economics for scaling

newsletters.summary: A viral Twitter discourse reveals a power shift in venture capital, with founders now holding reputational leverage over investors—exemplified by a GP falling asleep during a pitch and a dispute between Vinod Khosla and Cloudflare CEO Matthew Prince over equity demands. Meanwhile, Apollo, Blackstone, and Broadcom are launching a $35 billion AI infrastructure platform to lease compute capacity to companies like Anthropic, representing major commoditization of AI hardware and one of the largest private credit deals ever.
keyTakeaways: Founder reputation now trumps traditional VC authority: investors who lack diligence or courtesy face public shaming and lost deal flow, signaling a fundamental market reversal where capital seekers have become capital allocators' judges.

Off-balance-sheet compute financing is reshaping AI infrastructure strategy—using SPVs to keep hardware liabilities off corporate balance sheets helps pre-IPO companies avoid investor penalties for high debt loads, but is drawing regulatory scrutiny.

The commoditization of AI compute through syndicated mega-deals ($35B+) threatens to undermine venture returns in infrastructure plays; hardware-heavy positions face margin compression as private credit and large-scale leasing models scale.

This collaboration between utilities and tech giants is reshaping infrastructure investment priorities, with Entergy's capital plan doubling from $33 billion to $67 billion and retail sales growth outlook nearly doubling from 4-5% to 9% through 2030.
keyTakeaways: Hyperscaler demand is fundamentally reshaping utility business models and growth trajectories—Entergy's five-year capex doubled primarily due to Meta and AWS infrastructure needs, signaling this is not cyclical but structural.

Meta and AWS are effectively de-risking their infrastructure investments by funding 'meaningful portions' of utility capex through construction contributions and long-term service agreements, creating a new precedent for corporate-utility partnerships that could accelerate similar deals across other regions.

This trend raises material questions about utility rate design and equity—as hyperscalers drive capex growth while benefiting from infrastructure cost-sharing arrangements, traditional ratepayers may bear disproportionate costs, potentially creating regulatory and political friction in rate-setting decisions.

Municipal climate resilience is an emerging investment theme: Failures in public heat response planning will likely drive municipal spending on cooling centers, early warning systems, grid modernization, and workforce training—attractive segments for climate tech investors.

newsletters.title: Pro Rata
newsletters.summary: SpaceX is set to raise at least $85 billion in its IPO with pricing Thursday and trading Friday, with valuations potentially reaching $1.75 trillion if the company achieves $200 billion in revenue by 2030. The bull case centers on Starlink's direct-to-cell expansion, AI compute revenue ($2 billion monthly from Anthropic and Google agreements), and orbital data centers, while bears worry about execution risks on Starship, declining Starlink ARPU, compute commoditization, and Elon Musk's key-person risk. Meanwhile, OpenAI has filed confidential IPO registration papers and plans another tender offer at its $852 billion valuation before a potential late-summer public offering.
keyTakeaways: SpaceX's IPO valuation hinges on diversification beyond launch services—Starlink's direct-to-cell business and AI compute deals are now material revenue drivers worth $2+ billion monthly, representing fundamental business model shift from pure aerospace.

Musk remains a critical valuation variable with asymmetric risk: investors are betting on pre-iPhone/App Store phase where founder vision is essential, but departure would erase premium; evaluate SpaceX's institutional depth and management bench independently of founder narrative.

AI compute is emerging as a high-margin near-term cash generator ($2B/month) but faces long-term commoditization risk as data center capacity expands and inference efficiency improves—position sizing should reflect this temporary moat rather than permanent competitive advantage.
newsletters.originalTitle: Axios Pro Rata: SpaceX futures

newsletters.title: Benedict Evans
newsletters.summary: Benedict Evans analyzes the upcoming IPO wave featuring SpaceX, Anthropic, and potentially OpenAI, while examining debates over stock index inclusion rules that could impact pension fund exposure to AI companies. The newsletter explores how enterprise software adoption follows a spectrum from legacy systems like SAP to improvised solutions using Excel and Google Sheets, with implications for how companies decide when to build, buy, or improvise software infrastructure.
keyTakeaways: Major AI unicorns (SpaceX, Anthropic, OpenAI) entering public markets will force index rule changes—the S&P 500's decision to exclude them versus Nasdaq's inclusion creates fragmented capital allocation and determines whether passive index funds (controlling >50% of US market) gain exposure to AI infrastructure leaders.

The 'improvised software' layer (Excel, Google Sheets, Notion, email) represents a massive arbitrage opportunity for SaaS founders: companies lack standardized solutions for workflows between legacy systems and vertical apps, creating a predictable path from spreadsheet workarounds to dedicated software purchases as operational scale increases.

Index rule debates highlight structural market shifts where traditional gatekeeping (profitability requirements, float size thresholds) may exclude transformative infrastructure companies, potentially misallocating trillions in passive capital away from AI capex leaders during their critical growth phase.
newsletters.originalTitle: Benedict's Newsletter: No. 646

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Tom Steyer's failed California gubernatorial campaign, despite spending $216 million primarily on advertising, highlights the diminishing returns on climate activism funding. Xavier Becerra's likely primary victory despite accepting Chevron donations signals that even in progressive states, voters may not prioritize climate policy positions or penalize candidates for oil industry support, potentially reshaping Democratic approaches to energy policy.
keyTakeaways: Massive political spending ($216M on governor race, $342M on 2020 presidential bid) has failed to deliver electoral wins for climate-focused candidates, suggesting campaign finance efficiency problems for climate investors and activist billionaires

Voter indifference to fossil fuel funding and climate policy ambition in a deeply liberal state indicates climate activism may face headwinds as a political organizing principle, affecting the viability of climate-focused political investment strategies

A potential Democratic shift toward 'conciliatory approach to fossil fuels' even in coastal progressive states suggests regulatory risk for clean energy sectors and opportunity reassessment for climate tech ventures relying on aggressive state-level policy support
newsletters.originalTitle: Tom Steyer’s Win-Loss Record

newsletters.title: Maddyness
newsletters.summary: Back Market, a leading European secondhand electronics marketplace, is undergoing its first CEO transition in 12 years as founder Thibaud Hug de Larauze steps down on September 1st, handing control to CFO Clément Petit. The newsletter also highlights Superprof's continued international expansion through the acquisition of Brazil-based Profes.com.br, and Finovox's €8.2 million Series A funding round led by TX Ventures to scale anti-fraud solutions across European financial institutions.
keyTakeaways: Back Market's leadership transition signals maturation of the unicorn, with the CFO's promotion suggesting a shift toward operational efficiency and financial discipline—a critical signal for investors evaluating European marketplace exits and governance structures.

Superprof's acquisition strategy in high-growth markets (Italy, Brazil) demonstrates how European edtech platforms are building defensible positions in emerging regions, relevant for portfolio diversification and international scaling playbooks.

The €8.2M Series A for Finovox reveals strong investor appetite for compliance and anti-fraud tech in financial services across Europe, indicating a macro trend toward regulatory-driven software investment following stricter KYC/AML frameworks.
newsletters.originalTitle: 09.06 - Back Market va changer de PDG pour la première fois en 12 ans

newsletters.title: Term Sheet
newsletters.summary: A, an AI-powered offensive security startup, raised $37 million from Lightspeed Venture Partners and others to autonomously conduct continuous cyberattacks against its own customers' systems to identify vulnerabilities before real attackers can exploit them. The funding reflects growing urgency around AI-enabled threats following Anthropic's Mythos model release, which exposed zero-day vulnerabilities, and targets the continuous threat exposure management market projected to grow from $2.7 billion in 2025 to $7 billion by 2033.
keyTakeaways: The traditional manual penetration testing model (costing $30K, taking 2 weeks, annual/biannual cadence) is becoming obsolete as AI-powered attackers operate at scale; investors should expect consolidation around continuous, AI-native security solutions rather than legacy point products

A six-month gap between vulnerability discovery and exploitation is now measured in minutes per VCs quoted in the article, creating urgent demand and justifying premium valuations for companies that can compress detection-to-remediation timelines from months to days

The competitive landscape is heating up with well-funded players like XBOW ($120M Series B at $1B+ valuation in March 2026), signaling this is a high-conviction sector for institutional capital and suggesting A's $37M raise may be relatively modest for market share capture
newsletters.originalTitle: Ctrl-Alt-Defeat

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: A federal court overturned Trump administration restrictions on solar and wind tax credits (45Y and 48E), restoring the original 5% investment threshold just weeks before the credits expire on July 4, 2026. The ruling found the IRS lacked adequate justification for eliminating the provision and that the policy change may have been discriminatorily motivated. Separately, New York passed a one-year moratorium on new data center construction to establish clearer energy and labor standards, while Illinois Governor Pritzker signaled similar moves to temporarily halt data center tax breaks.
keyTakeaways: The court's decision immediately reinstates more favorable tax credit eligibility rules for solar and wind developers, but the 26-day window before credits expire limits practical impact—investors should focus on which projects can mobilize fastest to capture remaining incentives.

Data center regulatory headwinds are intensifying in major tech hubs; companies in this sector face tightening energy standards and labor requirements, signaling potential margin compression and higher compliance costs ahead.

Clean energy tax credit policy remains vulnerable to political reversal, creating uncertainty for long-term project financing—investors should model multiple policy scenarios and consider exposure to renewables sensitive to federal incentive changes.
newsletters.originalTitle: A Safer Harbor

newsletters.title: Pro Rata
newsletters.summary: The Trump administration is pursuing an unconventional strategy of acquiring equity stakes in AI giants like OpenAI and Anthropic as part of broader government investment in strategic industries, a departure from free-market principles that could reduce AI regulation while creating financial incentives for government support. Meanwhile, Morgan & Morgan, the country's largest personal injury law firm with $2 billion+ in revenue, is exploring a $1 billion+ minority investment that could accelerate the controversial use of Management Services Organizations to sidestep traditional restrictions on non-lawyer ownership of law firms.
keyTakeaways: Government equity stakes in AI companies fundamentally alter regulatory incentives—expect lighter-touch AI policy going forward as the U.S. government becomes a financial stakeholder in the success of OpenAI and Anthropic, not a neutral regulator.

The Morgan & Morgan deal signals growing PE interest in legal services through MSO structures, which exploit regulatory gaps; this trend will likely trigger state-level regulatory responses and potentially reshape the legal industry's capital structure.

Macro shift: Trump's 'dealmaker' approach to industrial policy (stakes in chipmakers, miners, quantum, and now AI) suggests strategic government ownership is becoming normalized across critical sectors, blurring lines between private capitalism and state capitalism.
newsletters.originalTitle: Axios Pro Rata: USAI Inc.

newsletters.title: Latitude Media
newsletters.summary: The Power Resilience Forum 2027, scheduled for February 3-5 in San Diego, is expanding its focus to address critical infrastructure challenges including extreme weather, wildfires, cyber threats, and surging power demand. Building on PRF26's 500 attendees, the 2027 edition will convene utilities, regulators, technology providers, investors, insurers, and policymakers to discuss resilience strategies, infrastructure investment, cybersecurity, and regulatory frameworks.
keyTakeaways: Growing market opportunity for investors in climate resilience, cyber security, and critical infrastructure—the convergence of extreme weather, cyber threats, and grid stress is driving utilities to increase capital allocation for resilience projects and technology solutions.

Industry consolidation signal: the expansion from 500 to an anticipated larger audience of decision-makers (utilities, insurers, rating agencies, policymakers) indicates institutional recognition that resilience is becoming a core business driver, not a peripheral risk—PE and VC investors should track portfolio companies addressing grid hardening, physical security, and cyber defense.

Regulatory momentum ahead: emphasis on 'resilience policy and regulatory frameworks' suggests anticipated changes in utility regulation and grid standards that will create compliance-driven capex spending and favor established players with resilience capabilities over the next 2-3 years.
newsletters.originalTitle: Save the date: Power Resilience Forum 2027

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Hawaii's electricity bills surged 22% in May 2026, marking a significant price spike that aligns with regulatory predictions. The increase reflects ongoing challenges in the state's energy market and has been documented in the Electricity Price Hub, a tracking mechanism for power pricing trends.
keyTakeaways: Hawaii's utility market is experiencing material cost pressures that regulators anticipated, suggesting predictable rate trajectory for investors evaluating regional utility stocks or renewable energy investments in the Pacific region

The 22% spike signals strong demand for alternative energy solutions and grid modernization in Hawaii, creating potential investment opportunities in solar, battery storage, and distributed energy resources

Hawaii's high electricity costs relative to the mainland create competitive disadvantages for residents and businesses, likely accelerating policy push toward renewable energy adoption and creating tailwinds for cleantech companies operating in the state
newsletters.originalTitle: Hawaii’s Electricity Bills Spiked 22% in May

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Two major U.S. transmission projects—the Champlain Hudson Power Express (CHPE), a 339-mile line delivering 1,250 megawatts of Quebec hydroelectric power to New York City, and the SunZia Wind and Transmission Project, a 550-mile line connecting a 3.5-gigawatt New Mexico wind farm to Arizona and California—have recently begun operations and are now receiving strong support from environmental advocates who previously opposed them during their lengthy permitting battles.
keyTakeaways: Large-scale transmission infrastructure projects face significant permitting and advocacy headwinds during development but become materially valuable once operational, suggesting investors should evaluate transmission projects on long-term impact rather than near-term opposition sentiment.

The shift from environmental opposition to support for CHPE and SunZia demonstrates that decarbonization infrastructure requires transmission buildout as a critical bottleneck; capital deployed to transmission companies and developers solving this chokepoint addresses a genuine market gap.

The U.S. electricity sector remains fundamentally constrained by transmission capacity, creating a multi-decade opportunity in grid modernization and long-distance power line development—particularly for projects linking abundant renewable resources in remote areas to high-demand urban centers.
newsletters.originalTitle: Build It and They Will Come Around

newsletters.title: Maddyness
newsletters.summary: French startups raised €202 million this week, more than four times the previous week's total, with an average ticket size of €22 million, reflecting momentum in the European VC market. Key developments include a new accelerator partnership between the TV show platform "Qui veut être mon associé?" and Tomcat focused on B2B Tech startups, H Company joining Nvidia's Nemotron coalition for open-source AI models, and Innovafeed securing €51 million despite sector headwinds and planned workforce reductions.
keyTakeaways: French fundraising volatility is notable—€202M this week vs €46.5M the prior week—suggesting capital is concentrating around larger rounds rather than consistent deployment across a broad startup base.

The new B2B Tech accelerator signals demand for infrastructure supporting overlooked startups outside mainstream media attention, while Nvidia's recruitment of French AI startups like H Company indicates Europe's emerging strength in enterprise AI applications.

The insect-farming sector faces structural challenges despite capital availability (Innovafeed's €51M raise alongside 60 job cuts), highlighting how funding alone cannot solve market fundamentals—relevant for sector-focused investors evaluating deep tech agriculture bets.
newsletters.originalTitle: 08.06 - «Qui veut être mon associé ?» et Tomcat lancent leur accélérateur de startups

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: Trump's $100,000 H-1B visa fee, intended to reduce foreign tech hiring, has had a bifurcated effect: top-funded AI startups like OpenAI and Stripe are hiring more H-1B workers than before by absorbing the cost, while mid-market and smaller companies are sharply curtailing applications. The policy has widened the competitive moat for well-capitalized unicorns in the race for AI talent, with some firms shifting strategies to poach visa holders from competitors rather than sponsor new approvals.
keyTakeaways: The H-1B fee is functioning as a capital-based competitive filter—only the best-funded AI labs can afford it, making foreign tech talent acquisition a liquidity advantage rather than a deterrent to hiring

Smaller startups and mid-market companies are losing access to global talent pools, forcing them to compete on domestic hiring alone and potentially widening the valuation gap between tier-1 and tier-2 VC-backed companies

The policy's real-world impact contradicts its stated intent: instead of reducing foreign hiring, it's concentrated that hiring among megafunded labs while creating secondary effects like visa-holder poaching from other companies
newsletters.originalTitle: Who's paying Trump's H-1B fee?

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: European PE firms are capitalizing on AI-driven cybersecurity threats to critical infrastructure, with investors like Eurazeo and PSG Equity backing specialized companies including Nextron Systems and Glasswall. AI has accelerated the cybersecurity threat landscape, enabling sophisticated attacks in hours rather than weeks and putting advanced capabilities in reach of low-skilled actors, driving a structural shift in investor demand for defense-focused cyber solutions.
keyTakeaways: PE investors are deploying capital into cybersecurity companies with 60%+ revenue concentration in critical infrastructure and defense sectors, signaling conviction that government spending and regulatory mandates will sustain demand growth

AI has compressed attack timelines and democratized advanced cyber capabilities, creating a durable investment thesis for companies offering proactive threat prevention rather than reactive incident response

Business email compromise and vendor payment fraud losses are creating secondary investment opportunities in PE portfolios themselves, suggesting operational value-add potential for cyber-focused PE firms managing existing portfolio companies
newsletters.originalTitle: Could AI cyber threats spark an investment wave?

newsletters.title: Fintech Business Weekly
newsletters.summary: Fintech startup Bilt is facing regulatory pressure from Senator Warren and the CFPB to compensate users impacted by a problematic transition to its Bilt 2.0 platform in February-March, which resulted in widespread complaints including declined card applications, frozen accounts, unauthorized balance transfers from Wells Fargo, and missing account statements. The newsletter also highlights broader fintech regulatory challenges including an Aspiration cofounder receiving 14 years in prison, GreenSky settling an investigation, and Wise facing new AML scrutiny, signaling intensifying regulatory scrutiny across the fintech sector.
keyTakeaways: Consumer-facing fintech platforms face significant regulatory and reputational risk during product migrations; Bilt's technical and operational failures during its transition generated enough user friction to trigger CFPB and congressional attention, potentially requiring customer compensation and establishing precedent for similar cases.

Regulatory enforcement is expanding across fintech verticals—from lending (GreenSky) to payments (Wise) to rewards platforms (Bilt)—suggesting that compliance and AML infrastructure are becoming critical competitive differentiators and potential deal-breakers for institutional partnerships.

The sector is experiencing a maturation phase where operational excellence and regulatory compliance are as important as product innovation; companies with weak transition management or compliance postures face existential risks from fines, forced compensation, and loss of banking partnerships.
newsletters.originalTitle:
Sen. Warren, CFPB Pressure Bilt to Compensate Users Impacted by Rocky
 Transition

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: This newsletter highlights growing public concern about data centers' energy consumption and costs, with polling showing most Americans support a national data center moratorium and blame them for rising power bills. Meanwhile, fusion energy company Helion has tripled its valuation, signaling investor optimism in alternative power sources that could address the grid strain from AI and data center expansion.
keyTakeaways: Data center energy demand is becoming a political liability—majority public support for a moratorium creates regulatory risk for hyperscalers and opportunity for alternative energy providers like fusion startups to capture infrastructure investment.

Helion's tripled valuation reflects capital flight toward fusion as a solution to the data center power crisis, suggesting VCs are hedging against regulatory constraints on traditional grid infrastructure by backing next-gen energy.

The carbon removal sector remains underdeveloped despite climate urgency, creating a structural gap between AI/data center growth and decarbonization solutions—indicating underinvestment in carbon tech and potential M&A consolidation opportunities.
newsletters.originalTitle: Data Centers’ Eye-popping Polling, a Fusion Boom, and More

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: OpenAI is valued at a significant premium to peers despite showing weaker unit economics according to PitchBook's analysis, suggesting a valuation disconnect from fundamentals ahead of its IPO. The venture market remains constrained with only $274.1 billion raised across 619 funds in Q1 2026, though secondaries and top-tier VC funds bucked broader trends, indicating LP capital is concentrating among elite managers while Big Tech companies have committed nearly $600 billion to AI infrastructure but remain conservative on acquisitions.
keyTakeaways: OpenAI's valuation premium to peers does not reflect comparable business quality metrics, signaling potential overvaluation risk for pre-IPO investors entering at current terms.

Capital concentration accelerating: only experienced VC managers growing their funds while secondaries hit record fundraising, forcing LPs to choose between illiquidity and betting on a shrinking group of proven performers.

The $600 billion AI infrastructure spend by Big Tech without matching M&A activity suggests these companies are building rather than buying competitive advantage, reducing exit optionality for AI startups and extending their private capital dependency.
newsletters.originalTitle: OpenAI's value paradox

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: SpaceX set an unusual fixed IPO price of $135 per share ($75 billion gross proceeds) without traditional price discovery, signaling confidence in oversubscribed demand and limiting downside risk for underwriters. Meanwhile, Big Tech's four major players (Amazon, Google, Meta, Microsoft) are projected to spend nearly $600 billion on AI infrastructure in 2026, prioritizing minority stakes and infrastructure deals over traditional acquisitions, which hit a decade low of seven VC-backed deals in 2024.
keyTakeaways: SpaceX's fixed pricing strategy removes traditional price discovery risk but signals exceptional founder confidence—any downward adjustment would now appear as a stumble rather than routine calibration, creating psychological pressure on underwriters to deliver on demand.

Big Tech's capital reallocation from M&A to infrastructure (Microsoft's 30% OpenAI stake, Meta's $14.3B Scale AI investment) reflects a strategic pivot toward securing AI positioning through compute commitments rather than traditional venture acquisitions, limiting exit opportunities for VC-backed AI startups.

The combination of mega-liquidity events (SpaceX IPO, record $47.3B in co-investment funds raised) with depressed acquisition activity suggests a bifurcated market where scale-stage deals command premium valuations while early-stage exit pathways narrow significantly.
newsletters.originalTitle: Elon: Are you in or are you out?

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: The Trump administration announced nearly $1 billion in coal sector funding across three initiatives: $425 million for coal plant supply chains and capacity expansion, $75 million for a new coal export facility in Oakland, and $350 million for new coal plant construction in Alaska and West Virginia plus infrastructure upgrades in Puerto Rico and Maryland. Meanwhile, the EU is positioning itself as a global leader in ocean monitoring technology with plans to capture 35% of the market by 2035, as the U.S. dismantles its decade-old coastal monitoring program.
keyTakeaways: The U.S. coal sector is receiving direct federal capital injection totaling ~$850 million for new construction and expansion, creating near-term opportunities in coal plant construction, supply chain infrastructure, and coal export logistics — but investors should note utilities are already challenging these cost burdens in court.

The EU's OceanEye initiative represents a $1+ billion opportunity to capture ocean observation technology markets, suggesting European cleantech and maritime monitoring companies will be positioned as preferred vendors over U.S. competitors as the administration withdraws from ocean monitoring.

Policy reversal risk is acute: coal plant investments depend on sustained government support that faces legal challenges and inherent asset deterioration (plants keep breaking down), while the simultaneous EU pivot to ocean intelligence suggests climate/environmental tech investment is bifurcating geographically away from the U.S.
newsletters.originalTitle: Trump’s Billion-Dollar Coal Gamble

newsletters.title: Term Sheet
newsletters.summary: Anduril co-founder Trae Stephens warns of an impending defense tech shakeout, arguing that mid-stage companies trade at 50-200x multiples while the underlying defense industry trades at 2-2.5x revenue, creating an unsustainable disconnect. With defense tech equity funding hitting $17.9 billion in 2025 and doubling year-over-year, Stephens contends that venture capital is overpricing the sector as if there will be multiple "Andurils," when realistically only a couple of credible new players will emerge and the rest will fail.
keyTakeaways: Mid-stage defense tech companies are trading at 50-200x revenue multiples while established defense contractors trade at 2-2.5x, signaling massive valuation disconnects that will force most startups into acquisition failures or bankruptcy rather than IPO exits.

The $17.9 billion in 2025 defense tech funding represents poorly-calibrated capital allocation; founders refusing discounted acquisitions due to ego will drag down ecosystem returns, suggesting VCs should tighten diligence on defense tech unit economics and exit paths.

The defense tech bubble risks sector credibility if government procurement advantages (OTA agreements, $20B+ OSC funding) cannot overcome structural overvaluation—the shakeout may consolidate winners but damage VC returns broadly across defense innovation.
newsletters.originalTitle: Casualties of war

newsletters.title: Pro Rata
newsletters.summary: Kevin Mayer's Smash Capital led a $200 million Series B for autonomous defense-tech company Allen Control Systems at a $2.2 billion valuation, marking the venture firm's expansion into defense while maintaining its core software and media portfolio. The newsletter also reports that Trump administration officials are exploring voluntary equity stakes in major AI companies like OpenAI, with potential returns directed to public purposes, alongside notable funding rounds for Supabase ($500M at $10.5B valuation) and Helion fusion ($465M at $15.5B valuation).
keyTakeaways: Defense-tech is attracting established brand-name VCs: Smash Capital's move signals that top-tier venture firms previously focused on consumer tech are now aggressively deploying capital into autonomous weapons and defense platforms, particularly those with existing government contracts exceeding $120M.

Government equity acquisition in AI companies represents unprecedented policy risk: if the Trump administration successfully acquires voluntary stakes in OpenAI and other major AI firms, it could fundamentally reshape cap table dynamics and governance for the sector's most valuable companies.

Defense and AI sectors are consolidating investor attention: the crowding in defense-tech investing combined with continued mega-rounds in frontier AI infrastructure (Supabase, Helion) suggests capital is bifurcating toward high-conviction bets in geopolitically sensitive sectors rather than dispersing across traditional software categories.
newsletters.originalTitle: Axios Pro Rata: AI USA Inc.

newsletters.title: Deep Tech Newsletter
newsletters.summary: Antares Nuclear became the first company in the U.S. Department of Energy's accelerated small modular reactor program to reach criticality, a major milestone for commercial nuclear development. The newsletter covers a broad range of deep tech investments including agtech boom, SpaceX's Starfall reentry vehicles, Muon Space's Condor-Ultra satellite platform targeting orbital data centers, quantum computing advances, and regulatory developments around AI and synthetic biology.
keyTakeaways: Antares Nuclear's criticality achievement signals the commercial viability of small modular reactors; with nine other companies in the DOE pilot program racing to hit the July 4 deadline, this is a critical inflection point for nuclear energy infrastructure investment.

Multiple emerging deep tech verticals are simultaneously maturing—orbital platforms (Muon), quantum computing, fusion energy (Xcimer), and advanced biomanufacturing—suggesting capital is flowing toward infrastructure plays that enable downstream applications rather than end-consumer products.

Regulatory tailwinds (Trump administration nuclear support, NSA's central role in AI governance, China's brain-computer interface approval) are accelerating commercialization timelines across regulated sectors, creating first-mover advantages for companies navigating approval pathways.
newsletters.originalTitle: DTN 165: Antares Nuclear Goes Critical

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: American public sentiment has sharply turned against data center development, with 70% now opposing local data centers compared to a 50-50 split in August 2025. Simultaneously, 53% of Americans blame data centers for rising electricity costs (up from 28%), and 45% express pessimism about AI's societal impact, with a majority supporting a nationwide data center moratorium. The backlash appears driven by viewing data centers as symbols of AI and Big Tech rather than as neutral infrastructure, creating significant policy and investment risks.
keyTakeaways: Data center developers and AI infrastructure investors face rapidly deteriorating public support that will likely translate into regulatory hurdles—a majority of Americans now support moratorium policies, signaling that local zoning battles will intensify and expand nationally.

The public attribution of rising electricity costs to data centers (jumping from 28% to 53% in nine months) creates a political opening for energy regulation that could directly impact operating costs and land acquisition strategies for data center operators and cloud infrastructure companies.

Demographic and political divides suggest this is not a partisan issue but rather a generational and class-based concern, making it a durable macro headwind rather than a temporary sentiment swing—capital should anticipate sustained regulatory pressure on AI infrastructure buildout across the U.S.
newsletters.originalTitle: The Backlash to the Boom

newsletters.title: Maddyness
newsletters.summary: Gojob, a French HR tech scale-up, has voluntarily exited the Next40 program just days before a new cohort announcement, signaling its transition to profitability and international maturity. Separately, Innovorder, a restaurant digitalization platform serving major clients like Elior and Sodexo, has raised €20 million from UL Invest while already profitable, targeting European expansion and acquisition opportunities.
keyTakeaways: French scale-ups are reaching maturity faster, with profitable companies self-selecting out of government support programs to make room for earlier-stage ventures—a sign of healthy ecosystem progression rather than failure.

B2B SaaS companies in the hospitality sector are attracting significant growth capital even at profitability, suggesting strong unit economics and market demand for enterprise digitalization solutions across Europe.

The shift toward agentic AI and data-driven governance is reshaping how enterprises operate, creating new strategic priorities around implementation and risk management that could drive demand for specialized consulting and software solutions.
newsletters.originalTitle: 05.06 - Gojob quitte volontairement le Next40, à quelques jours de la nouvelle promotion

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: U.S. crude oil stocks have fallen to their lowest level since 2004 amid geopolitical tensions, with analysts warning prices could reach $200 per barrel by summer if the Strait of Hormuz remains closed. Oklahoma's attorney general is suing to block a new aluminum smelter backed by UAE investment after the Trump administration imposed 50% tariffs on aluminum, and Texas's grid operator has approved new rules governing data center connectivity that could reshape the state's energy infrastructure.
keyTakeaways: Oil supply constraints driven by Iran tensions present both macro risk and potential upside for energy infrastructure investors, with strategic petroleum reserve depletion limiting government tools to manage price spikes.

The aluminum smelter dispute signals protectionist trade policy risk for foreign direct investment in U.S. industrial projects, even from traditionally allied nations, affecting capital flows into manufacturing.

Texas grid operator rules on data center load management suggest regulatory tightening around AI/crypto infrastructure that could redirect investment away from Texas or increase compliance costs for grid-intensive operations.
newsletters.originalTitle: Oklahoma!

newsletters.title: Term Sheet
newsletters.summary: Honeycomb Insurance, an AI-native insurer for multifamily residential properties, raised $40 million led by Zeev Ventures, bringing total funding to $95 million. The company uses machine learning on geospatial data and aerial imagery to price risk without physical inspections, offering coverage up to 40% cheaper than traditional carriers while achieving profitability with $275 million in gross written premium across 22 states. The market is shifting from a hard insurance market (2023 peak of 20% annual premium increases) to a soft market in 2025, with apartment building coverage rates declining 5-15% as reinsurance capacity floods back.
keyTakeaways: Honeycomb demonstrates a viable alternative to legacy insurance underwriting: AI-driven risk assessment eliminates the economics barrier that prevented insurers from serving fragmented multifamily markets, creating a defensible moat around data accumulation and model refinement.

The company's profitability while still investing growth capital signals that AI-native underwriting has achieved unit economics advantages over inspection-based models, making this a durable business model rather than a temporary market arbitrage play.

The reversal from hard to soft insurance markets (rates down 5-15% in 2025) represents a critical inflection point where incumbent carriers regain capacity and price competitively, forcing tech-enabled players to compete on non-price factors like speed, accuracy, and customer experience rather than pure cost advantage.
newsletters.originalTitle: Protect the hive

newsletters.title: Pro Rata
newsletters.summary: Andreessen Horowitz has hired Anne Neuberger, former U.S. deputy national security advisor, as a full-time partner and head of global affairs, signaling the firm's strategic pivot toward geopolitics and international expansion. The move reflects how defense-tech, sovereign wealth fund partnerships, and government relationships are becoming critical to venture success as a16z expands globally with offices like its recent Tokyo location. Meanwhile, Bill Ackman's Pershing Square is exiting its five-year UMG investment with over $600 million in profits after the music label rejected a $63 billion acquisition offer, highlighting challenges in the streaming and music industry from AI competition.
keyTakeaways: Top-tier VC firms are now actively recruiting high-level government talent to navigate geopolitical complexities and build sovereign relationships—expect this trend to accelerate as defense-tech and AI regulation become portfolio differentiators.

The hiring of Neuberger despite a16z's public friction with the Biden administration over AI policy suggests that accessing government relationships matters more than ideological alignment, reshaping how LP networks and deal access work.

Music industry consolidation and catalog valuations are plateauing amid streaming saturation and AI disruption, signaling that tech investors should watch for similar margin compression in other content-dependent sectors with high regulatory/IP exposure.
newsletters.originalTitle: Axios Pro Rata: a16z goes global

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Heatmap News and Orennia are hosting a virtual event on June 24, 2026, examining how accelerating AI-driven data center demand is reshaping U.S. power markets and creating investment opportunities across generation, transmission, and project development. The discussion will focus on siting, permitting challenges, local opposition, and power market dynamics influencing where data center projects advance or face constraints.
keyTakeaways: Data center siting and permitting risk are emerging as critical investment variables—investors need intelligence on which projects face local opposition and regulatory headwinds versus those with clear infrastructure pathways

Behind-the-meter power solutions and distributed generation opportunities are gaining prominence as developers race to secure reliable power; this signals capital allocation shifts toward resilient, localized energy infrastructure

Power market constraints and transmission bottlenecks are becoming deal-limiting factors for hyperscale data center development, creating opportunities for investors in grid modernization and power infrastructure projects
newsletters.originalTitle:
Upcoming Virtual Event: The 2026 Data Center and Power Market
 Outlook

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: A New World screwworm, a flesh-eating fly that devastates livestock, has breached the Panama biocontrol barrier established by the U.S. government and crossed into Texas, threatening to drive already record-high beef prices even higher. The failure exposes critical gaps in state capacity and long-term infrastructure maintenance, as the U.S. must now build a larger sterile screwworm production facility in Texas capable of producing 300 million insects annually—a far more expensive undertaking than maintaining the original Panama containment strategy.
keyTakeaways: Agribusiness and meat production face immediate cost pressures from screwworm infestation; companies in livestock management, veterinary services, and beef processing should model supply chain disruptions and price volatility as the government's re-eradication efforts take years to stabilize.

The $300 million+ investment in new screwworm production infrastructure signals rising U.S. government spending on biosecurity and agricultural risk management—creating opportunities in biotech manufacturing, pest control, and agricultural technology sectors focused on disease prevention.

The screwworm case exemplifies a broader governance crisis: critical infrastructure and pest control systems lack sufficient political incentives for maintenance, suggesting underinvestment across climate resilience, food security, and disease eradication—a macro risk for food-dependent economies and an opportunity for private firms filling government capacity gaps.
newsletters.originalTitle: The Screwworm Screw-Up

newsletters.title: Maddyness
newsletters.summary: French quantum computing startup Quobly raised €115 million in Series A funding backed by industrial giants STMicroelectronics and Air Liquide, marking a resurgence of investor interest in quantum computing technology for semiconductor-based systems. The funding round signals growing momentum in European quantum tech as enterprises like EDF explore applications for energy optimization and numerical simulation, while the EU simultaneously moves to reserve sensitive cloud and AI contracts for European suppliers.
keyTakeaways: Quantum computing is experiencing renewed investor attention with substantial industrial backing—€115M Series A for Quobly demonstrates that deep-tech quantum plays can attract major corporate strategics, not just traditional VCs, signaling confidence in near-term commercialization paths.

European tech champions are consolidating competitive advantage through strategic acquisitions and funding rounds; combined with EU procurement protectionism for AI/cloud, this suggests capital will increasingly concentrate in European-backed quantum and AI infrastructure plays rather than fragmenting globally.

Energy sector applications (EDF's interest in optimization and simulation) represent a concrete use case driving quantum investment beyond hype—investors should monitor enterprise adoption timelines in utilities, logistics, and financial services as the next validation milestone for quantum computing ROI.

newsletters.title: Latitude Media
newsletters.summary: Critical Capital is a new podcast from Crux and Latitude Studios exploring the business dynamics of clean energy and critical infrastructure through markets and capital flows. The first three episodes cover AI's massive physical infrastructure demands and the trillion-dollar manufacturing opportunity, the resilience of U.S. industrial trade policy amid political volatility, and the grid's hardware bottleneck in power electronics and transformers rather than generation capacity.
keyTakeaways: The intersection of AI and physical infrastructure is creating a trillion-dollar manufacturing opportunity, with geopolitical implications as China's industrial capabilities compete directly with U.S. buildout.
U.S. clean energy momentum depends on sustained bipartisan industrial policy support—political volatility poses real execution risk to manufacturing-heavy capital plans.

Grid modernization bottlenecks lie in aging power electronics and transformers, not renewable generation—this shift creates targeted hardware investment opportunities in less-discussed but critical infrastructure segments.

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: SpaceX is targeting a $75 billion raise at a $1.78 trillion valuation in its upcoming IPO. Meanwhile, Partners Group's decision to cap redemptions at 5% on an $8.6 billion evergreen PE fund has sparked broader concerns about stress in private markets, with PE stock prices tumbling 16% or more across major European firms like EQT and CVC Capital Partners. The market volatility reflects growing investor anxiety about corporate distress and asset quality in private equity vehicles similar to ongoing troubles in private credit.
keyTakeaways: Evergreen PE fund redemption restrictions signal potential liquidity and valuation stress in private equity portfolios, not just private credit—this could trigger a broader repricing of illiquid private assets
The Partners Group selloff contagion across EQT, CVC, and Bridgepoint suggests institutional LPs may be reassessing exposure to legacy portfolio companies amid rising bankruptcy rates since 2022

Emerging opportunities are shifting to high-growth geographies: India is attracting strong LP interest for diversification, and European quantum startups are outpacing North America in VC funding for the first time since 2023

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: New York and six Northeast states are suing the Trump administration over a $1 billion payment to TotalEnergies to abandon offshore wind leases, claiming it violates federal law and threatens over 1,000 union jobs. The House passed a bipartisan geothermal energy package aimed at accelerating permitting and deployment timelines for the technology, while the Trump administration is simultaneously eliminating a $368 million deep-ocean observation system used to monitor coastal environments.
keyTakeaways: Offshore wind faces significant political headwinds with the Trump administration actively paying foreign energy companies to exit renewable projects, creating litigation risk and uncertainty for utilities and developers betting on coastal wind capacity.

Geothermal energy is emerging as a bipartisan priority with new legislation designed to streamline permitting—companies like Fervo Energy could benefit from faster deployment timelines, potentially making geothermal an attractive alternative to offshore wind in the current political climate.

The administration's simultaneous elimination of ocean monitoring infrastructure while blocking offshore wind suggests a coordinated rollback of climate infrastructure investment, signaling that renewable energy investors should prepare for regulatory reversals and reduced federal support over the next 18 months.
