SpaceX has filed for IPO while OpenAI and Anthropic approach their own public offerings, with both AI firms' quarterly revenue numbers leaking as they move toward institutional disclosure.
keyTakeaways: Google's transformation of search from 10 blue links to AI-generated synthesis directly threatens publisher traffic monetization, creating an existential challenge to the web's economic model and forcing every content company to reconsider dependency on search referrals.

The competitive pressure between Google and OpenAI to capture AI-driven search behavior means this disruptive shift is likely inevitable regardless of publisher concerns—capital should be deployed toward companies that can survive or thrive in 'Google Zero' scenarios.

Approaching IPOs for SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic signal a major inflection point in tech infrastructure and AI commercialization; leaked quarterly numbers suggest OpenAI's revenue scaling significantly, indicating both growth opportunity and valuation benchmarks for the AI infrastructure investment thesis.

newsletters.summary: Nvidia's head of sustainability Josh Parker discusses how artificial intelligence could reduce carbon emissions despite massive electricity demands from AI data centers. Tech companies are building new solar, battery, and gas infrastructure to power chip warehouses dominated by Nvidia's hardware, raising questions about whether AI will ultimately deliver on its climate promise.
keyTakeaways: Nvidia dominates the AI chip market serving data center buildouts by major tech firms, positioning the company as a critical infrastructure play in the clean energy transition and creating long-term revenue visibility from climate-conscious capital expenditures.

AI's net climate impact remains unproven—while proponents argue AI can optimize energy systems and reduce emissions, the massive electricity consumption of current data centers presents a near-term carbon liability that investors should scrutinize against long-term efficiency claims.

The convergence of tech infrastructure spending, renewable energy deployment, and fossil fuel integration creates multiple investment vectors: renewable energy providers, grid modernization companies, and semiconductor manufacturers are now intertwined in climate outcomes.

keyTakeaways: State-level climate policy is less resilient than previously assumed; even ambitious legislation like New York's CLCPA can be materially weakened when energy affordability concerns gain political traction, creating uncertainty for clean energy investors betting on state-led transition momentum.

The delay of carbon regulation to 2028 and accounting method changes suggest compliance timelines and measurement standards for climate commitments are becoming negotiable—investors should reassess the enforceability and credibility of state-level climate targets.

Trump's return to the White House is shifting the climate policy burden from federal to state level more slowly than in his first term due to cost-of-living pressures; this macro dynamic may reduce near-term demand for emissions reduction technologies and delay capital deployment in climate solutions.

keyTakeaways: High-net-worth retail investors are now behaving like institutional investors with comparable time horizons, risk appetite, and capital bases—creating a new market segment that wealth advisers and consultants are racing to serve with institutional-grade products and research.

US private credit spreads have diverged sharply from Europe in 2026, with US lenders able to demand 50-100 bps more from borrowers amid broader market volatility, while European spreads remain unchanged—this narrows or potentially eliminates the historical 25-150 bps European premium, reshaping cross-Atlantic capital allocation decisions.

keyTakeaways: Anthropic's valuation flip above OpenAI reflects real market divergence in investor confidence and enterprise traction—this signals a competitive reshuffling in frontier AI that could reshape downstream capital allocation away from OpenAI ecosystem bets.

xAI's $1B+ monthly burn represents a high-risk, high-reward bet on execution speed rather than recklessness; if autonomous agents materialize before competitors scale GPU capacity, losses become a bargain—otherwise it becomes the largest venture correction in AI history.

The enforcement action highlights regulatory risks in the fintech banking-as-a-service model, particularly around compliance infrastructure for high-velocity, international transaction volumes.

Multi-currency and cross-border payment fintechs using bank partnerships face heightened scrutiny; investors backing platforms in this space should expect their banking partners to face increased regulatory costs and operational friction, potentially impacting unit economics and growth timelines.

The BSA/AML enforcement trend signals a broader regulatory shift toward holding small and mid-sized banks accountable for their fintech ecosystems—expect more enforcement actions and potential de-risking by banks of marginal fintech partnerships, constraining growth for smaller payment platforms.

newsletters.summary: A federal jury rejected Elon Musk's lawsuit against OpenAI, clearing a major legal overhang ahead of the company's anticipated IPO. Both OpenAI and Anthropic have adopted public benefit corporation structures, which legally require balancing profits with public good missions, though enforcement remains largely discretionary under Delaware law and the 'business judgment rule.' The ruling raises questions about how investors will value AI companies with dual profit and societal benefit mandates when they enter public markets.
keyTakeaways: OpenAI's path to IPO is now clearer after the Musk lawsuit dismissal, but its public benefit corporation status will test whether institutional investors accept corporate structures that prioritize AGI safety alongside shareholder returns.

The legal enforceability of PBC missions is weak—Delaware law gives boards wide discretion, meaning OpenAI and Anthropic could justify nearly any business decision as advancing their stated public benefit goals, reducing actual accountability.

Anthropic's recent valuation milestone (reportedly exceeding OpenAI's) suggests the market may not yet be pricing in the governance complexity of AI companies structured as PBCs, presenting both valuation risk and opportunity for sophisticated investors.

keyTakeaways: SpaceX's infrastructure economics are dramatically better than disclosed: the Anthropic deal alone recoups AI capex in under one month, validating compute as a standalone asset independent of proprietary AI models like Grok.

The filing shows a major narrative-to-economics gap for IPO investors: AI accounts for 47% of filing language but only 6.7% of revenue and a $14 billion free cash flow loss, while Starlink delivers 61% of revenue and nearly all free cash flow with 63% EBITDA margins.

The prevalence of earnout provisions (1 in 5 VC deals) indicates seller concessions remain structural; buyers retain leverage despite improved exit activity, limiting full exit valuations

The feeding frenzy has created such frothy conditions that investors are wiring hundreds of thousands of dollars to brokers for share blocks without verification, driven by pent-up demand and limited access paths to the company's equity. This secondary market scramble reflects the extreme investor appetite for exposure to one of AI's leading companies before its public debut.
keyTakeaways: Secondary markets for pre-IPO shares are experiencing extreme price discovery mechanisms and verification gaps—investors are transacting without confirming share authenticity, signaling both desperation for allocation and potential fraud risks in unregulated secondary trading.

The 48-hour allocation window Anthropic imposed created artificial scarcity that supercharged demand, suggesting companies can use time-constraint mechanisms to generate frenzied bidding in capital raises, though this raises questions about market efficiency.

The gap between annualized revenue run rates ($45 billion claimed) and actual realized performance, combined with valuations exceeding $900 billion pre-IPO, indicates the AI sector is pricing in aggressive growth assumptions that may not materialize, potentially creating significant downside risk for late-stage secondary buyers.

Biomanufacturing, grid batteries, and quantum computing are reaching inflection points where technical breakthroughs are translating into commercial deployment windows, suggesting investor focus should shift from R&D-stage companies toward those positioned for near-term commercialization and government procurement cycles.

Defense and space infrastructure modernization is becoming a consistent capital theme, evidenced by in-space refueling demos, autonomous ground robots in active conflict, and satellite servicing capabilities, indicating sustained government spending that extends traditional defense tech investment horizons.

The disconnect between energy consumption and billing creates investment opportunities in energy efficiency solutions, distributed solar (as seen in Southern California Edison's 24% usage reduction), and demand management technologies that utilities must adopt to manage this dynamic

keyTakeaways: SpaceX's massive valuation relative to current profitability suggests investor appetite is driven by growth potential and optionality rather than fundamentals, which could signal either a reopening IPO window or unsustainable market exuberance for capital-intensive tech.

The xAI acquisition and resulting losses demonstrate the competitive pressure in AI infrastructure, where companies must spend aggressively to remain viable—a key consideration for PE/VC firms evaluating AI-related investments and exit timing.

The SpaceX IPO reaction, particularly toward xAI, will likely set the tone for other pending IPOs like OpenAI's, making this filing a crucial market test for whether late-stage high-burn AI and deeptech companies can access public capital markets.

newsletters.summary: The U.S. solar manufacturing sector is experiencing explosive growth with nearly 30 new utility-scale factories launched last year, creating capacity to produce over 60 gigawatts of panels annually—more than double the projected 35 gigawatt demand through 2030. However, upstream solar cell manufacturing remains a bottleneck with only three active factories, while battery storage is approaching domestic self-sufficiency with five new anode material plants expected online by year-end. Meanwhile, global oil markets are showing signs of easing as major tankers successfully navigate the Strait of Hormuz, potentially softening the energy crisis that has defined markets since early 2026.
keyTakeaways: Solar module manufacturing capacity has surged 1,600% over five years and now vastly exceeds demand, signaling a potential overcapacity problem that could compress margins and trigger consolidation in the sector despite protectionist tariffs and tax credit policies.

The critical bottleneck shifting upstream to solar cell production—with only three active U.S. factories against pipeline demand—creates a targeted investment opportunity in cell manufacturing before 2030 and suggests current module capacity growth may prove stranded without corresponding domestic cell production.

The convergence of domestic battery self-sufficiency by year-end and potential oil market softening could trigger a major rotation in energy infrastructure investment toward electrification, making integrated solar-storage-cell producers the likely winners versus pure module manufacturers.

The proposed Cursor acquisition signals SpaceX's pivot toward AI infrastructure and coding tools as a core business pillar rather than a satellite-launch company, requiring investors to reassess valuation multiples and competitive positioning against pure-play AI firms.

X's sustained advertising revenue decline despite Musk's ownership raises questions about the platform's long-term viability as a profitable business unit within the SpaceX IPO structure, making the overall investment thesis dependent on whether Starlink and AI ventures can compensate for this drag.

Government policy risk—particularly loan cancellations—remains a critical threat to solar companies reliant on federal financing, making investors wary of overlevered solar platforms dependent on favorable policy regimes.

newsletters.summary: U.S. clean energy investment declined 3% in Q1 2026 to $61 billion, marking the second consecutive quarterly drop and ending a seven-year growth streak, with clean manufacturing investments plummeting 34% year-over-year as federal EV tax credits were eliminated. Despite federal headwinds, six states including Virginia, Colorado, and New York posted double-digit increases in clean electricity investments, while global EV sales are projected to reach 30% of new car markets in 2026.
keyTakeaways: Federal policy reversal is crushing domestic clean tech manufacturing (down 34% YoY) and battery production (down 47% YoY), signaling a dangerous retreat in U.S. competitiveness in critical green infrastructure sectors

State-level divergence presents a fragmented investment landscape where Virginia, Colorado, New Mexico, Oklahoma, Michigan, and New York are becoming de facto safe havens for clean energy capital, creating potential M&A and expansion opportunities in these jurisdictions

The global EV market acceleration (30% projected share in 2026) contrasts sharply with U.S. domestic investment declines, suggesting American investors and manufacturers risk ceding market share to Chinese and European competitors while domestic supply chains deteriorate
newsletters.originalTitle: A Broken Streak

newsletters.title: Term Sheet
newsletters.summary: Metropolis, an AI computer vision infrastructure company, has built a $5 billion valuation by automating parking and vehicle recognition across 4,200+ U.S. locations, processing $5 billion in annual payment volume. The company completed a $1.5 billion take-private of SP+ in 2024 (largest venture-backed M&A that year), raised $1.6 billion in November 2025 including a $1.1 billion Term Loan B, and is expanding into autonomous vehicle management and urban air mobility through partnerships with Joby Aviation, signaling a shift from legacy parking operations into next-generation mobility infrastructure.
keyTakeaways: The 'growth buyout' hybrid model—combining AI software with traditional infrastructure assets—is proving highly fundable across the capital stack: UP Partners and Slow Ventures at early stage, through Vista and Silver Lake as growth investors, to private equity (BDT, MSD, Eldridge) and debt markets (JPMorgan's TLB). This structure may represent a replicable playbook for modernizing legacy industries.

Legacy liabilities matter: an $8.75 million Tennessee AG settlement for deceptive pricing and ongoing DPPA class action litigation reveal that acquiring distressed parking operators comes with regulatory and consumer trust baggage that can undermine valuation multiples and require significant capital allocation to remediate.

The convergence of autonomous vehicles, electric vehicle charging, urban air mobility (vertiports), and biometric recognition into parking infrastructure suggests physical real estate may become the critical chokepoint and platform for next-gen mobility—not the vehicles or software themselves—creating a rare opportunity for infrastructure-first investors.
newsletters.originalTitle: Where Waymo sleeps at night

newsletters.title: Pro Rata
newsletters.summary: Tech buyouts have collapsed to just $9.3 billion globally across April-May 2026, down 82% from March's $52.6 billion, as AI breakthroughs from Claude and Codex have paralyzed the buyside while private credit liquidity has drained. Meanwhile, healthcare consolidation is accelerating with GHO Capital and CBC Group merging to form a $21 billion AUM firm, signaling smaller PE players must scale to compete against generalist giants crowding the sector.
keyTakeaways: Tech M&A is in severe freeze mode: buyers are paralyzed by AI disruption uncertainty and sellers are walking away from deeply discounted offers, meaning deal flow will likely remain depressed until 2-3 more quarters of AI impact data emerges.

Private equity is adapting through alternative structures: continuation vehicles and convertible preferred rounds are being used to preserve valuations and manage portfolio risk during the liquidity crunch—a sign the market is under real stress.

Healthcare consolidation accelerating: smaller PE firms must merge to reach scale and compete with generalists; future healthcare exits may depend on whether AI-driven drug discovery startups can actually deliver on transformation promises still unfulfilled.
newsletters.originalTitle: Axios Pro Rata: Frozen

newsletters.title: Latitude Media
newsletters.summary: NextEra Energy and Dominion Energy announced a $67 billion all-stock merger creating the world's largest regulated electric utility, strategically combining NextEra's renewable energy pipeline and battery storage capabilities with Dominion's dominance in data center connections—particularly in Northern Virginia where Dominion has contracted 51 GW of large-load capacity. The merged entity claims a 130 GW large-load position, nearly matching NextEra's entire current 110 GW generation portfolio, positioning it to serve hyperscaler AI demands through co-located generation, storage, and load hubs.
keyTakeaways: The merger creates a utility-scale platform specifically designed to capture AI infrastructure buildout: NextEra gains access to Dominion's densest hyperscaler real estate in Northern Virginia while contributing renewable generation and battery storage to meet 130 GW of contracted capacity.

Battery storage emerges as undervalued deal leverage—NextEra contracted 1.3 GW in Q1 2026 alone with strong supply chains, while Virginia targets 20 GW by 2045, creating a critical moat in the data center hub model of co-locating generation with AI load.

Near-term buildout to 130 GW capacity must rely on renewables and storage by default since new gas-fired generation won't scale until 2030, signaling accelerated capital deployment into renewable and battery assets rather than fossil fuel infrastructure despite recent gas partnerships.
newsletters.originalTitle: What does NextEra-Dominion mean for AI?

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: The Trump administration justified a nearly $1 billion settlement with TotalEnergies to buy back the French company's U.S. offshore wind leases by citing national security concerns from the Department of War. However, emails obtained by House Democrats suggest the administration's timeline for these security concerns may have been fabricated, raising questions about the true motivations behind the deal.
keyTakeaways: Major government-backed buyout deals in renewable energy may face political scrutiny and potential legal challenges if the stated rationales don't align with documented timelines, creating deal risk for energy companies with U.S. offshore assets.

TotalEnergies faced significant capital loss on its U.S. offshore wind leases, signaling regulatory and political risks in the American offshore wind sector that investors should price into future project valuations.
The politicization of offshore wind development and national security justifications for killing renewable projects may deter foreign energy capital investment in U.S. clean energy infrastructure.
newsletters.originalTitle: Trump ‘Fabricated’ Timeline in Offshore Wind Deal, House Democrat Says

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Western states have formed the Mountain West Geothermal Consortium to streamline financing and permitting for geothermal energy development across state lines, addressing regulatory fragmentation that hinders project advancement. The initiative arrives as geothermal gains bipartisan political support and next-generation developer Fervo Energy recently raised nearly $2 billion in its initial public offering, signaling substantial capital appetite for the sector.
keyTakeaways: Geothermal energy has become a rare bipartisan priority in U.S. politics due to its carbon-free profile and compatibility with existing oil and gas infrastructure, creating favorable conditions for policy support and capital deployment.

Regulatory coordination across state lines is now emerging as a critical bottleneck for geothermal scaling; consortium formation suggests market recognition that streamlined permitting and financing frameworks will unlock project development and investor returns.

The $2 billion Fervo IPO demonstrates institutional confidence in next-generation geothermal technology viability, positioning the sector as a growth opportunity within the broader energy transition despite historical underinvestment relative to solar and wind.
newsletters.originalTitle: Geothermal Rises in the West

newsletters.title: Maddyness
newsletters.summary: Mistral AI continues its acquisition strategy by acquiring Austrian startup Emmi AI, marking its second major acquisition after Koyeb earlier this year. The European tech ecosystem shows strong momentum across multiple sectors, with French startup Leadbay raising $4.3M post-Y Combinator to identify businesses invisible to LinkedIn, while a French-German merger between Singulier and Ommax aims to create a 400+ consultant AI consulting powerhouse targeting €80-100M in revenue.
keyTakeaways: Mistral AI's acquisition strategy signals confidence in bolt-on growth for an AI foundation model company, expanding beyond core model development into complementary infrastructure and services businesses.

European medtech and healthtech are attracting significant capital allocation, with Lauxera oversubscribing its €520M fund to help European medical device companies scale into US markets, indicating a clear arbitrage opportunity between European and American valuations.

Horizontal consolidation is accelerating in European AI consulting and B2B software (Singulier-Ommax merger, Indy's legaltech acquisition), suggesting early-stage founders should consider whether their niches will face margin compression as larger platforms integrate competing functionality.
newsletters.originalTitle: 20.05 - Mistral AI boucle une nouvelle acquisition !

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: Nasdaq Private Market and Hiive are competing for patent control over technology that standardizes venture secondary deals, signaling the growing importance of this market segment. NextEra Energy's $66.8 billion acquisition of Dominion Energy reflects accelerating consolidation in the utilities sector driven by massive power demand from AI data centers, with NextEra strategically gaining access to Northern Virginia's 'data center alley' and positioning itself to supply 15 gigawatts of power to data center hubs by 2035.
keyTakeaways: The venture secondaries patent battle indicates this market is maturing rapidly enough to warrant intellectual property disputes—investors should monitor which platform wins as it could set industry standards for deal execution and pricing

PE investors are increasingly targeting fragmented utility and energy infrastructure companies as a direct play on AI infrastructure buildout, with regulatory fragmentation across states creating acquisition opportunities similar to roll-up strategies in other sectors

The NextEra-Dominion deal and partnerships with tech giants like Google signal that energy infrastructure is now a core component of the AI supply chain, potentially creating a new asset class for infrastructure-focused PE funds and long-term power purchase agreements
newsletters.originalTitle: Patent fight in VC secondaries

newsletters.title: Term Sheet
newsletters.summary: Chinese state capital is dramatically reshaping the AI investment landscape, with government-linked investors participating in over 140 AI deals in 2025 compared to fewer than 10 annually before 2018. DeepSeek's $45-50 billion valuation raise led by China's Big Fund exemplifies this shift, as state entities strategically deploy capital into semiconductors and compute infrastructure where they can compete, even as overall Chinese AI deal volume has cooled to $10-11 billion annually since 2024. China has also tightened regulatory control, instructing companies like Moonshot AI and StepFun to seek government approval before accepting U.S. capital, marking a strategic pivot toward state-championed national champions over traditional venture models.
keyTakeaways: State-backed capital is becoming the dominant funding mechanism for China's AI champions, with median semiconductor deal sizes reaching $30.48 million in 2026—a structural shift that creates a bifurcated global AI market with different competitive dynamics than the U.S. private-capital model.

Companies identified as strategic national priorities by Beijing now require government approval for foreign capital, effectively nationalizing deal flow in critical sectors; Western VCs and strategics should expect increasing friction accessing Chinese AI and semiconductor investments.

The median deal size inflation in Chinese AI and semiconductors suggests government capital is consolidating around fewer, larger bets in specific sectors rather than diversifying across the startup ecosystem, indicating policy is weaponizing venture capital for industrial policy goals.
newsletters.originalTitle: Chinese state capital is the new VC

newsletters.title: Pro Rata
newsletters.summary: Congressional Democrats introduced the Let Kids Play Act to ban private equity from investing in youth sports, citing a 46% rise in youth sports costs since 2019 and PE's practice of vertical integration to drive up prices through owning facilities, leagues, and ancillary services simultaneously. Several youth sports deals are currently in market including 3Step Sports and FloSports, though sponsors could potentially sidestep legislation by taking companies public. Meanwhile, Elon Musk lost his lawsuit against OpenAI, removing a potential tailwind for SpaceX's imminent IPO filing.
keyTakeaways: PE-backed youth sports deals face significant regulatory risk if the Let Kids Play Act gains Republican co-sponsors; sponsors should model exit scenarios including forced divestitures or public market transitions as alternatives to private ownership.

The proposed legislation targets PE's vertical integration playbook in youth sports specifically, suggesting increased regulatory scrutiny on bundled service models across other consumer-facing PE investments where price inflation is visible to end users.

Active youth sports fundraising (3Step Sports, FloSports, League One Volleyball) occurs against a backdrop of bipartisan parent frustration with costs, creating political momentum for restrictions that could reshape deal structures and valuations in the sector.
newsletters.originalTitle: Axios Pro Rata: PE sports ban

newsletters.title: Benedict Evans
newsletters.summary: Google and Apple are racing to deploy AI features on-device, with Google limiting advanced Android AI to flagship devices while Apple's redesigned Siri is expected to reach broader audiences. The AI funding market continues to show frothy valuations, with Anthropic raising $30 billion at a $900 billion valuation and Cerebras reaching a $95 billion peak market cap on IPO, while competition in AI security tools intensifies between Anthropic, OpenAI, and Microsoft.
keyTakeaways: Edge AI deployment constraints are creating hardware stratification: Google's approach limits features to premium devices, while Apple's broader addressable base gives it distribution advantage—investors should watch whose on-device AI reaches critical user mass first.

Late-stage AI funding valuations ($900bn for Anthropic) dwarf entire cohorts of venture-backed IPOs from the dot-com era, signaling either sustained conviction or significant risk of downside repricing when growth expectations normalize.

AI-powered cybersecurity tools are transitioning from research projects (Anthropic's Mythos deemed 'too dangerous') to commercial offerings (OpenAI's Daybreak for approved clients), creating new enterprise software categories that incumbents like Microsoft are rushing to integrate.
newsletters.originalTitle: Benedict's Newsletter: No. 643

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Toyota's bZ electric vehicle has unexpectedly captured the third-place position in monthly EV sales rankings, signaling the automaker's successful pivot into the electric vehicle market after years of hesitation. The achievement is notable given the competitive landscape where Tesla dominates the top two spots with the Model Y and Model 3, while other traditional automakers like Chevrolet have also competed for this positioning with models like the Equinox EV and original Bolt.
keyTakeaways: Toyota's market performance shift demonstrates that late-mover disadvantage in EVs is not insurmountable for established automakers with manufacturing scale and brand loyalty, potentially reshaping competitive dynamics in the EV sector.

The battle for third place in EV sales is emerging as a critical indicator of competitive success among non-Tesla players, suggesting investors should monitor market share rankings beyond Tesla as a proxy for execution capability among legacy automakers.

Toyota's entry into EV top-3 positions indicates the EV market is maturing beyond Tesla's monopoly, with increasing consumer choice creating opportunities for diversified automotive portfolios and potentially supporting higher valuations for traditional automakers pivoting to electrification.
newsletters.originalTitle: How Toyota Became an EV Winner

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Span, a smart electrical panel startup, is positioning itself as a new kind of utility by leveraging unused grid capacity to meet surging AI data center power demands. CEO Arch Rao argues that because the electric grid is oversized to handle peak demand spikes lasting only hours annually, demand shifting technologies can extract significantly more power from existing infrastructure faster and cheaper than traditional grid expansion.
keyTakeaways: Span has identified a arbitrage opportunity in grid underutilization—data centers face long waits for grid upgrades, but smart panel technology could unlock immediate capacity through demand management, positioning the company as critical infrastructure for AI deployment.

The smart electrical panel market is consolidating around utility partnerships and major brand deals, suggesting institutional validation and potential for significant market expansion as AI infrastructure demands accelerate.

Grid modernization through software-based demand shifting could become a more capital-efficient alternative to traditional utility infrastructure spending, potentially disrupting traditional utility business models and creating new investment opportunities in distributed energy management.
newsletters.originalTitle: A New Kind of Utility

newsletters.title: Maddyness
newsletters.summary: French AI startup Dust has raised $40 million to accelerate its U.S. expansion, offering a platform that enables enterprises to build custom AI agents. The newsletter also features content on UK market entry strategies for European founders and highlights the 5th edition of LBP AM's innovation awards seeking tomorrow's French unicorns across all sectors.
keyTakeaways: Dust's $40M funding signals strong investor appetite for enterprise AI agent platforms, suggesting this vertical is becoming a crowded but capital-rich segment competing with players like Zapier and Make.
European AI startups are increasingly targeting the U.S. market as their primary growth engine, indicating that European capital and talent alone are insufficient for venture-scale returns in AI.

The UK market positioning content and French innovation awards suggest European VCs are actively looking beyond domestic markets—UK expansion and cross-border talent/funding access are now competitive necessities for European tech founders.
newsletters.originalTitle: 19.05 - IA : Dust lève 40 millions de dollars

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: Anthropic has reached a $930 billion post-money valuation, surpassing OpenAI's $852 billion for the first time, with leading investors including Dragoneer, Greenoaks, Sequoia, and Altimeter Capital each committing at least $2 billion. The milestone reflects Anthropic's superior equity return ratio (9.0x versus OpenAI's 4.9x) and strong enterprise adoption momentum, with 34.4% of US businesses now using Claude versus 32.3% for OpenAI—marking the first recorded crossover. Anthropic's cleaner path to IPO, lack of single-customer concentration, and improving gross margins (above 70%, up from 38% annually) position it favorably for public market pricing relative to its rival.
keyTakeaways: Anthropic commands a 1.8x valuation premium per equity dollar deployed compared to OpenAI, with this gap widening for two consecutive rounds—a signal that frontier AI investors believe Anthropic's business fundamentals (enterprise adoption, margins, product-market fit) are outpacing OpenAI's despite the latter's historical lead.

For VCs allocating to AI infrastructure, OpenAI's 35% Microsoft revenue concentration and higher historical cap tables present material IPO repricing risk; Anthropic's diversified customer base and margin expansion create more predictable public market comparables and valuation anchoring for the category.

The crossover in enterprise adoption (Anthropic now leading) validates that product differentiation (Claude Code and 140%+ net retention) drives market share in AI, not first-mover advantage—this has implications for downstream AI application funding and suggests OpenAI's platform dominance is contestable.
newsletters.originalTitle: Charted: Anthropic vs. OpenAI

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: A proposed $400 billion merger between Dominion Energy and NextEra Energy would create a utility giant spanning Virginia's data center heartland to Florida's 6 million-customer base, capitalizing on surging electricity demand from AI infrastructure. The deal signals the beginning of a consolidation phase in the electricity sector driven by the need for massive capital investments in grid modernization and renewable energy, while an escalating Iran War energy crisis pushes oil prices toward $180/barrel and threatens nuclear plant security.
keyTakeaways: Mega-utility consolidation is accelerating: A $400B+ Dominion-NextEra merger would combine critical AI data center infrastructure in Virginia with Florida's largest power provider, suggesting that utilities with geographic diversity and nuclear assets are positioning for premium valuations in a capital-intensive energy transition.

Geopolitical energy shocks are creating market volatility: The Iran War-triggered oil crisis and Strait of Hormuz blockade are pushing 80+ countries toward emergency measures, with Brent crude potentially reaching $180/barrel—creating hedging opportunities and renewed interest in diversified energy portfolios that include nuclear and renewables.

Nuclear infrastructure and grid reliability are becoming competitive moats: Drone attacks near nuclear plants in the Middle East and Ukraine, combined with soaring AI electricity demand, are making utilities with nuclear assets and geographic redundancy more strategically valuable, driving M&A in the sector.
newsletters.originalTitle: A $400 Billion Megamerger

newsletters.title: Term Sheet
newsletters.summary: European AI startups are accelerating their U.S. expansion at unprecedented speed, driven by enterprise demand that captures 80-81% of global venture capital in early 2026. While visa friction from the Trump administration poses challenges, deregulatory tailwinds favoring AI are outweighing immigration headwinds, as evidenced by Swedish startup Lovable reaching $6.6 billion valuation and $400 million ARR. The funding gap is widening dramatically, with U.S. companies capturing 83% of global VC in Q1 2026 versus Europe's modest 9% growth in 2025.
keyTakeaways: European founders targeting enterprise AI solutions must prioritize U.S. market entry earlier in their lifecycle—the traditional Series B timing for American expansion is collapsing as competition for enterprise AI budgets intensifies.

Visa policy headwinds (including $100K H-1B fees) are real but secondary to regulatory tailwinds; investors should evaluate European AI teams' ability to relocate key talent via O-1 visas rather than viewing immigration as a dealbreaker.

Europe's AI talent advantage (30% more per capita) is being structurally drained to the U.S., creating a persistent capital allocation problem—European VCs may face talent scarcity even with sufficient dry powder, suggesting U.S.-focused exits will remain the dominant exit strategy.
newsletters.originalTitle: America is becoming European founders’ Series B

newsletters.title: Pro Rata
newsletters.summary: LIV Golf, the Saudi PIF-backed golf league struggling since losing its primary backer's support, is raising up to $250 million from new investors with a target of reaching profitability within 20 months. Meanwhile, deep-tech focused venture firm Playground Global closed its fourth fund at $475 million, maintaining its contrarian approach by investing in semiconductor materials, compute architectures, and energy technologies rather than following market trends toward orbital solutions.
keyTakeaways: LIV Golf's fundraising pitch hinges on rebranding away from Saudi association, betting that new non-PIF capital can attract sponsors and audiences that were previously deterred by sports-washing concerns—a key risk for investors considering this $5 billion-invested asset.

The $250 million raise represents a significant capital requirement with a tight October deadline, suggesting the league's cash runway is constrained and could require bridge financing if institutional investors prove skeptical of the profitability timeline.

Playground Global's $475 million fourth fund signals that contrarian deep-tech investing remains viable and well-capitalized, even as broader VC markets consolidate around proven trends, indicating potential bifurcation between trend-following and thesis-driven venture strategies.
newsletters.originalTitle: Axios Pro Rata: LIV fights for life

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Heatmap News is hosting its second annual Energy Entrepreneurship 2026 conference, bringing together founders, investors, manufacturers, and bipartisan policymakers to discuss emerging clean energy technologies including batteries, next-generation geothermal, and nuclear fusion. The event will feature speakers including U.S. Representative Don Beyer, Qcells VP Scott Moskowitz, and Commonwealth Fusion Systems' head of federal affairs Trent Bauserman, with a focus on both technology deployment and the industrial manufacturing backbone required to scale the energy transition.
keyTakeaways: Nuclear fusion and advanced geothermal are positioning themselves as mainstream investment categories alongside batteries—Commonwealth Fusion Systems' presence signals investor confidence in near-term commercialization timelines for fusion technology.

Clean energy manufacturing and workforce development are emerging as equal-priority investment themes alongside technology innovation, reflecting capital's shift toward supply chain resilience and domestic production capacity.

Bipartisan political engagement at the policymaker level indicates regulatory clarity and potential government support mechanisms (likely tied to IRA implementation) are becoming key value drivers for clean energy entrepreneurs seeking to scale.
newsletters.originalTitle: Upcoming Event: Energy Entrepreneurship 2026

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: NextEra Energy agreed to acquire Dominion Energy in a $67 billion deal that would create a major battery storage giant. The merger is particularly significant for Virginia and the PJM Interconnection (13-state electricity market) as both the region and grid operator face substantial battery storage needs, while NextEra is already a storage leader in the renewable energy sector.
keyTakeaways: Consolidation in energy storage: The NextEra-Dominion merger combines America's largest renewable developer with a major regional utility operator, creating potential market dominance in battery storage—a critical infrastructure bottleneck in competitive electricity markets.

Regional grid implications: The deal addresses pressing storage deficits in Virginia and the PJM region, suggesting regulatory approval could hinge on demonstrating benefits to local grid reliability and data center growth (Dominion serves Virginia's booming data center sector).

Energy transition infrastructure play: Battery storage has emerged as the limiting factor for renewable deployment; large-scale M&A consolidation around storage capabilities signals this is where significant capital and strategic value concentration will occur in the energy transition.
newsletters.originalTitle: Creating a Storage Giant

newsletters.title: Maddyness
newsletters.summary: A French venture capital newsletter highlighting five investment stories: Fakto raised €3.6M to use AI for detecting billing discrepancies in supplier invoices (claiming 14% error identification rates); Harvey, an $11B US legal AI unicorn, opened a Paris office and secured partnerships with major French law firms; Superprof completed its 19th acquisition in 13 years, targeting €100M revenue this year; The Bitcoin Society shuttered its bitcoin treasury operations due to market headwinds and fundraising difficulties; and predictive AI successfully forecasted the French national football team's World Cup roster.
keyTakeaways: Enterprise AI for process automation in procurement and finance is attracting meaningful capital—Fakto's €3.6M validates demand for AI-driven invoice verification that identifies hidden cost recovery opportunities for large enterprises

US AI unicorns are aggressively expanding into European markets with local partnerships; Harvey's Paris office and law firm integrations signal confidence in European enterprise AI adoption despite macro uncertainty

The shutdown of The Bitcoin Society's core treasury operations demonstrates selective market discipline even among prominent founders, suggesting crypto-native projects face genuine traction challenges in the current environment
newsletters.originalTitle: 18.05 - Ce qu'il faut savoir pour bien débuter la semaine

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: Humanoid robotics has captured over $5 billion in VC investment in 2026 alone, with one in four robotics dollars now flowing to humanoids as companies like Apptronik and Robotera close mega-rounds. However, industry insiders question whether current valuations are justified by real use cases and deployment paths, citing a psychological obsession with humanoids rather than rational fundamentals, though falling manufacturing costs—down 40% from 2023 to 2024—are making unit economics increasingly viable.
keyTakeaways: Humanoid robotics has reached frothy valuations with $5B+ invested in 2026, yet VCs and roboticists openly acknowledge unclear paths to returns and practical use cases, signaling potential bubble dynamics in the sector.

Manufacturing cost declines (40% year-over-year) are fundamentally improving unit economics and could validate current investments if deployment accelerates, but this remains the critical variable separating hype from sustainable business models.

The humanoid trend demonstrates how macro tailwinds (cheaper components, design improvements) can drive investment flows that outpace demonstrable market demand, raising questions about capital efficiency across deep tech verticals.
newsletters.originalTitle: The limits of  VC’s  humanoid bet

newsletters.title: Fintech Business Weekly
newsletters.summary: Yotta CEO expressed concerns about Synapse's brokerage migration potentially disrupting operations. The newsletter covers fintech industry updates including Varo's growth to 7.7 million accounts with $57 million in small-dollar loans outstanding (up 43% YoY), Evolve's Q1 performance, bunq's pursuit of a Mexican bank license, PayPal's DOJ fair lending settlement, and a community bank's AI-related cybersecurity incident.
keyTakeaways: Varo is experiencing user growth (5.1M to 7.7M accounts) and loan portfolio expansion, but continues to face elevated credit losses with 11% charge-offs on unsecured loans and 0.40% losses on its Varo Believe card product, signaling potential profitability challenges despite top-line metrics.

Regulatory and operational risks are materializing in fintech infrastructure—Synapse migration concerns at Yotta and a community bank's AI cybersecurity incident highlight fragility in third-party dependencies and emerging technology implementation in financial services.

Fintech expansion strategies are diversifying geographically (bunq in Mexico) and across lending products, but regulatory scrutiny is intensifying (PayPal's DOJ settlement), suggesting margin compression and compliance costs will pressure unit economics across the sector.
newsletters.originalTitle: “Synapse Is Gonna F— Everything Up,” Yotta CEO Worried Before Brokerage Migration

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: This week's Heatmap newsletter covers critical developments in climate and energy policy affecting investment landscape: Burgum's renewable energy permitting freeze signals potential regulatory headwinds for clean energy developers, while funding for early-stage climate tech is contracting. Additional stories examine infrastructure vulnerabilities in the Strait of Hormuz energy crisis and emerging opportunities in climate-resilient infrastructure.
keyTakeaways: Regulatory risk is materializing: Burgum's permitting freeze on renewables projects creates immediate uncertainty for developers and signals potential policy reversals that could impact near-term deployment timelines and project valuations.

Capital markets are tightening for climate tech: The drying up of early-stage climate tech funding suggests investor appetite is shifting away from pre-revenue ventures toward more proven business models, requiring portfolio companies to demonstrate faster paths to profitability.

Energy security concerns are creating dual-use opportunities: Geopolitical vulnerabilities in chokepoints like the Strait of Hormuz and climate resilience needs (e.g., hurricane preparation) may accelerate investment in distributed energy, storage, and resilience infrastructure as alternatives to centralized systems.
newsletters.originalTitle: Burgum’s Double Down, the FEOC Freakout, and More

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: AI venture capital hit record highs in Q1 2026, but an IPO liquidity crunch is forcing VCs to rely on secondaries and reformed SPACs as workarounds. Anthropic's revenue is on pace for $30 billion annually with enterprise customers doubling, while AMD doubled its server chip TAM forecast to $120 billion, signaling rapid enterprise adoption of agentic AI systems that are reshaping the entire technology stack.
keyTakeaways: Enterprise adoption of agentic AI has begun in earnest, with infrastructure providers like Anthropic and AMD seeing dramatic acceleration—this represents a significant shift in where capital will flow across the tech stack over the next 24 months

The liquidity gap between record-setting AI VC deployment and narrow IPO windows is concentrating growth in a handful of winners; investors should reassess portfolio concentration risk and secondary market opportunities as traditional exit paths remain constrained

Cross-border AI M&A is becoming structurally more complex due to regulatory fragmentation, data localization, and model portability issues—deal teams must pressure-test AI integration and governance earlier in diligence or face significant value destruction
newsletters.originalTitle: A granular look at the AI tech stack

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: Cerebras Systems' AI chipmaker IPO on Nasdaq achieved a 68% first-day pop after pricing at $185 per share, raising $5.5 billion and reaching a $56.4 billion valuation—more than double its February Series H valuation. The 20x oversubscribed offering signals strong retail investor appetite for AI infrastructure plays and sets the stage for an anticipated wave of AI IPOs including SpaceX, Anthropic, and OpenAI. Early investors like Benchmark (which invested in 2016) stand to realize significant multiples, with some stakes potentially worth billions.
keyTakeaways: AI infrastructure companies can command extreme valuations in public markets—Cerebras doubled its private valuation in months, suggesting venture investors should anticipate significant dilution when portfolio companies go public and recalibrate return expectations accordingly.

The massive oversubscription and retail enthusiasm indicates a sustained appetite for AI-adjacent hardware plays that position themselves as Nvidia alternatives, creating potential exit windows for VC-backed semiconductor and inference-focused startups over the next 12-24 months.

With multiple mega-cap AI companies (OpenAI, Anthropic, SpaceX/xAI) queued for IPOs, the AI VC market is entering a liquidity inflection point that could reshape fund economics and LP return profiles across the venture ecosystem.
newsletters.originalTitle: Cerebras opens AI IPO floodgates

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Stardust Solutions, a geoengineering startup backed by $60 million in funding, has unveiled proprietary particle technology designed to reflect sunlight and cool the planet, with two formulations using silica and calcium carbonate compounds. The newsletter covers emerging climate intervention strategies moving from theoretical to commercial viability, alongside growing local resistance to data center development and Cuba's fuel shortage crisis.
keyTakeaways: Climate intervention technology is transitioning from R&D to commercialization—Stardust Solutions' disclosure of proprietary stratospheric aerosol particle designs signals investor appetite for geoengineering solutions and potential capital deployment in atmospheric intervention markets.

Local opposition to hyperscale data centers is intensifying; Cleveland's rejection of a $1.6 billion facility and 25 canceled projects in Q1 suggest real estate constraints for tech infrastructure, creating geographic arbitrage opportunities and potential headwinds for data center-dependent businesses.

Global energy disruptions (Cuba's fuel crisis, extreme weather events) underscore systemic vulnerabilities in energy supply chains, elevating investment thesis for renewable energy, LNG exports, and climate resilience infrastructure in developed markets.
newsletters.originalTitle: Silica Skies

newsletters.title: Term Sheet
newsletters.summary: Cerebras Systems completed its IPO on May 15, 2026, soaring 70% on its first day of trading after withdrawing its 2024 filing, driven largely by its multi-billion dollar partnership with OpenAI and improved fundamentals including larger customer base and more mature technology. While the blockbuster debut reflects strong investor appetite for AI chip alternatives to Nvidia, analysts warn the real test lies in manufacturing at scale and delivering to multiple hyperscaler customers, not just riding OpenAI momentum.
keyTakeaways: The IPO success validates the AI chip market's openness to Nvidia alternatives, but execution risk remains acute—investors specifically want to see proof of manufacturing capacity, on-time delivery, and multi-customer support at Nvidia's operational standards, not just headline partnerships.

OpenAI's shareholder stake and partnership appears to be the primary driver of renewed investor confidence after 2024 concerns about customer concentration and CEO Feldman's past accounting fraud conviction; overreliance on a single anchor customer poses significant downside risk if execution falters.

The broader AI compute market opportunity is potentially enormous (Feldman's $5 trillion TAM estimate from software engineers alone) but unproven at scale, suggesting Cerebras faces binary outcomes—either achieving breakout success or significant decline within a year, with little room for mediocre performance.
newsletters.originalTitle: Cerebras rising

newsletters.title: Pro Rata
newsletters.summary: Cerebras' IPO surged 89% above its offering price to $311, validating early-stage AI chip investors like Benchmark, whose $268 million investment is now worth $5.5 billion. Beyond Cerebras, Kimmeridge secured $9.75 billion in financing for its Commonwealth LNG project, and Anthropic is raising $30 billion at a $900 billion valuation, signaling a potential wave of AI-adjacent unicorn IPOs and mega-rounds.
keyTakeaways: Watch Cerebras' staggered lockup expirations—30 million shares unlock today with six more tranches through October—as insider selling could reverse the stock's explosive gains, similar to what happened with Figma.

The AI infrastructure wave is broadening beyond frontier labs: successful exits and mega-rounds in chips (Cerebras), energy (LNG financing), and AI safety (Anthropic) suggest venture investors should expect a multi-year IPO cycle across AI-adjacent sectors, not just AI applications.

Geopolitical supply chain concerns are creating tailwinds for U.S. infrastructure projects; Middle East gas disruptions are driving buyer demand for alternative LNG sources like Commonwealth, demonstrating how macro events redirect capital allocation in energy infrastructure.
newsletters.originalTitle: Axios Pro Rata: Cerebras surge

newsletters.title: Deep Tech Newsletter
newsletters.summary: A Deep Tech newsletter covering emerging technologies including implantable living materials using genetically engineered bacteria to deliver drugs on demand (published in Science), lunar helium-3 extraction opportunities, Virgin Galactic's commercial service launch, Cowboy's plans for 20,000 orbital data centers, non-viral gene therapy breakthroughs from Serif, and Unitree Robotics' mass-produced manned mech suit priced at $537,000.
keyTakeaways: Implantable biotech represents a nascent market segment: Harvard-led research on living hydrogel containers for bacteria-based drug delivery could enable preventative surgical care and autonomous infection response without traditional diagnostics or pills, attracting biotech and medtech investors.

Space infrastructure is attracting significant capital: multiple ventures (Blacknight Space Labs for helium-3, Cowboy for orbital data centers, Virgin Galactic for commercial spaceflight) indicate competitive commercialization of space assets, signaling investor appetite for non-traditional infrastructure plays.

Robotics and gene therapy breakthroughs suggest maturing deep-tech categories: Unitree's $537,000 mass-produced mech suit and Serif's non-viral gene therapy in primates indicate these once-speculative technologies are approaching commercial viability and scaling production, justifying expansion in these investment sectors.
newsletters.originalTitle: DTN 162: Implantable living materials deliver drugs on demand

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: The Beijing Auto Show has become the world's largest and most influential automotive exhibition, showcasing China's dominance in electric vehicle innovation with more EV models displayed in single rooms than exist across the entire U.S. market. Expert analysis from Asia Society and Johns Hopkins scholars reveals critical insights about China's EV market leadership and implications for U.S. policy under the Trump administration.
keyTakeaways: China's EV market has achieved scale and diversity that vastly outpaces the U.S., suggesting Chinese automakers have captured significant competitive advantages in battery technology, manufacturing efficiency, and consumer adoption that American companies will struggle to match without substantial policy intervention

The concentration of EV innovation at Beijing Auto Show signals that capital and talent flows are increasingly directing toward Chinese automotive manufacturers, making this a critical market signal for investors to reassess exposure to legacy U.S. automakers versus emerging Chinese EV competitors

The political framing around Trump administration policy suggests ongoing trade tensions and regulatory divergence between U.S. and China on EV standards will likely reshape global supply chains, pricing, and investment returns in the automotive sector over the next 2-4 years
newsletters.originalTitle: This Year’s Beijing Auto Show Has a Lesson for Trump

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Nearly a year after the One Big Beautiful Bill Act introduced foreign entity of concern (FEOC) rules for clean energy tax credits, the Trump administration has failed to provide implementing guidance, leaving developers and investors uncertain how to comply with construction deadlines approaching. The rules restrict credits for companies with 15% or more debt from China, Iran, Russia, or North Korea, or with foreign ownership thresholds of 25-40%, but without clear calculation methodologies, projects are proceeding amid significant legal uncertainty and mounting advisory costs.
keyTakeaways: Regulatory ambiguity is creating a massive advisory opportunity: developers are incurring substantial legal bills to navigate undefined FEOC compliance requirements, suggesting strong near-term demand for specialized tax and regulatory counsel services.

Project financing and M&A velocity in clean energy may face headwinds as investors struggle to underwrite foreign ownership exposure and debt structures; companies with any foreign capital involvement should immediately audit their cap tables and debt sources.

The failure to issue implementing rules nearly 12 months post-legislation suggests persistent policy uncertainty will remain a structural cost of capital for renewable energy projects, potentially favoring well-capitalized domestic firms with domestic capital stacks over international consortiums.
newsletters.originalTitle: Tax Credit Agita

newsletters.title: Maddyness
newsletters.summary: Éric Larchevêque, Ledger co-founder, is shutting down The Bitcoin Society's bitcoin treasury operations just six months after calling it "the most important project" of his life, citing unfavorable market conditions and fundraising difficulties. Separately, Fakto emerged from stealth with €3.6 million in funding to automate supplier invoice verification using AI, claiming to identify contractual deviations up to 14% on audited purchase volumes.
keyTakeaways: Strategic pivots driven by capital constraints: Even well-connected founders are forced to abandon core project components when market conditions deteriorate and institutional capital dries up—a signal of tightening VC discipline in 2025-2026.

Enterprise automation remains fundable: AI-driven spend analytics and procurement verification are attracting capital despite broader market skepticism, suggesting B2B operational efficiency tools with clear ROI metrics outperform speculative ventures.

Crypto infrastructure faces persistent headwinds: The abandonment of The Bitcoin Society's treasury function indicates that even prominent crypto figures cannot sustain capital-intensive blockchain projects without sustained favorable market sentiment and institutional backing.
newsletters.originalTitle: 15.05 - Éric Larchevêque met déjà fin à l’activité de trésorerie bitcoin de The Bitcoin Society

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: Private capital is decisively diverging between airline operators and aviation assets, as demonstrated by Spirit Airlines' May 2 bankruptcy versus the $28 billion take-private of Air Lease Corp. just weeks earlier. PE investors are pouring record capital into aircraft, engines, and aerospace suppliers while avoiding airline operating risks, with 2025 PE aerospace deal value hitting $55.6 billion. The gap reflects fundamental underwriting challenges: while airline margins remain volatile and unpredictable, aviation asset values have proven stable and resilient, making aircraft ownership and leasing far more attractive than carrier operations.
keyTakeaways: Aviation assets command premium valuations and deep institutional capital (Apollo, Brookfield, Sumitomo bidding $28B for Air Lease) while airlines face capital starvation even for survival—investors will finance the planes but not the operators flying them

The $2.8 billion in aviation-loan ABS issued by Apollo's PK AirFinance since July and new lending arms like Merit AirFinance signal PE is building systematic, scaled financing solutions around asset-backed aviation rather than equity bets on carriers

Software undergoes similar dynamics: PE sees 3x higher likelihood of take-privates in public software vs. the broader market, with buyout investors needing to identify defensible competitive moats and management execution capability amid AI disruption
newsletters.originalTitle: How airlines lost their Spirit

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Fervo Energy's Nasdaq debut surged nearly 40% on its first trading day, signaling strong investor appetite for next-generation geothermal technology as a solution for clean, reliable baseload power. The newsletter highlights three major clean energy developments: geothermal momentum, New York City's 1,250-megawatt Champlain Hudson Power Express hydropower project coming online ahead of schedule, and solar's continued expansion in Texas, all occurring as the U.S. faces critical energy infrastructure needs amid climate pressures.
keyTakeaways: Fervo Energy's successful IPO with 40% first-day gains demonstrates institutional capital is flowing into geothermal as an investable category; Fervo's plans for East Coast expansion within a decade suggest untapped U.S. markets are opening as drilling technology improves.

Major infrastructure projects like Champlain Hudson (1,250 MW, 16 years from proposal to operation) show the capital intensity and timeline challenges in clean energy, yet early completion suggests regulatory momentum is shifting in favor of rapid deployment.

The convergence of hydropower wins, geothermal IPO success, and solar expansion in Texas indicates a broadening clean energy portfolio across generation types, reducing single-technology risk and signaling sustained investor confidence in the transition regardless of political headwinds.
newsletters.originalTitle: Hot Rock, Hot Stock

newsletters.title: Term Sheet
newsletters.summary: Furientis, a defense startup founded by former Virgin Galactic and Castelion engineers, has raised $5 million in pre-seed funding to manufacture ship-based interceptor missiles at scale using automotive-style production methods. The company aims to reduce missile costs from $1-5 million to $250,000 per unit and achieve 1,000 units annually per factory, addressing a critical U.S. military supply gap where interceptor production can't match adversary capabilities or the cost-mismatch of firing million-dollar missiles at thousand-dollar threats.
keyTakeaways: Defense manufacturing consolidation creates opportunity: With only 5 major defense primes remaining (down from 50+ in the 1990s) and a single supplier for ship-borne interceptors, new entrants applying commercial manufacturing techniques can capture significant market share in a supply-constrained market.

Cost-competitive manufacturing unlocks new unit economics: By achieving 4-5x cost reduction through COTS components and automotive assembly processes rather than bespoke aerospace engineering, Furientis enables the military to deploy interceptors at scale—investors should watch for similar manufacturing arbitrage plays across defense subsystems.

Geopolitical urgency drives defense innovation capital: Depleted U.S. missile stockpiles and China's claimed capacity of thousands of cruise missiles weekly create acute procurement pressure, signaling sustained venture capital opportunity in defense tech and manufacturing startups that can solve production velocity over the next 3-5 years.
newsletters.originalTitle: Rocket men

newsletters.title: Pro Rata
newsletters.summary: Cerebras Systems completed a blockbuster $5.6 billion IPO at $185 per share, significantly exceeding its initial pricing guidance and achieving a $40 billion market cap — a 73% jump from its Series H valuation four months prior. The AI chipmaker's success reflects surging investor appetite for semiconductor stocks, while other major venture deals in AI infrastructure and robotics continued to attract significant capital from top-tier firms.
keyTakeaways: Cerebras' IPO success validates the AI chip infrastructure thesis, with early backers like Tiger Global, Benchmark, and Eclipse gaining substantial returns on a company that had previously shelved IPO attempts in 2024 and 2025.

The scale of venture capital flowing into AI-adjacent sectors remains massive — Recursive Superintelligence raised $650M, Fractile secured $220M for inference hardware, and Mind Robotics pulled in $400M, suggesting no slowdown in upstream infrastructure funding despite public market exits.

The semiconductor supply chain benefiting from AI demand extends across hardware layers (chips), software optimization (inference), and industrial application (robotics), indicating the venture market is diversifying bets across the full AI stack rather than concentrating in generative AI applications.
newsletters.originalTitle: Axios Pro Rata: Cerebras cash

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Corpus Christi, Texas faces an acute water crisis with freshwater reservoirs at less than 10% capacity and Day Zero projected for November 2026 unless the city receives 20-30 inches of rainfall. The city's century-long struggle to secure adequate water supply—despite being on the Gulf Coast—has led to multiple failed infrastructure projects, and a contentious 2024 local election where environmentally focused candidates opposing an expensive desalination plant largely lost, raising questions about whether different governance could have prevented the crisis.
keyTakeaways: Water infrastructure and desalination represent a critical investment opportunity as American cities face acute supply crises; Corpus Christi's failed planning and expensive desalination projects signal both market demand and execution risks in the sector.

Climate resilience and water management will increasingly drive municipal governance and capital allocation; investors should monitor how cities address water scarcity through infrastructure bonds, public-private partnerships, and alternative supply technologies.

Western water stress is moving from abstract long-term risk to immediate crisis—Corpus Christi could become the first major U.S. city to exhaust freshwater reserves, making this a bellwether for broader municipal credit risk and climate-adaptation spending across the Sunbelt.
newsletters.originalTitle: Hoping for a Hurricane

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: Software-focused PE funds are not trading at the steep discounts that secondary buyers might expect, despite public market turmoil in the sector. According to HarbourVest and Partners Group executives, secondary market discounts on software assets average around 13% (87% of NAV), with many software-heavy funds unable to sell standalone as cautious buyers demand deeper markdowns than sellers are willing to provide.
keyTakeaways: Secondary buyers seeking software bargains at 50-cent-on-the-dollar prices will be disappointed—current discounts average only 13% of NAV, making it difficult for sellers to offload software-heavy fund positions

PE's increased exposure to software (rising from 14% to 18% of US deal value over the past decade) has created a supply-demand imbalance in secondaries, where sellers hold firm on valuations while buyers demand risk compensation

A significant inventory of software-heavy PE funds cannot be marketed as standalone assets, signaling potential portfolio challenges ahead as public market corrections force repricing throughout the private capital stack
newsletters.originalTitle: Software secondaries discount is a myth

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: South Korea's state-owned Korea Hydro & Nuclear Power has signed a memorandum of understanding with Southern Company to collaborate on nuclear reactor engineering, marking a significant shift in U.S. efforts to re-shore nuclear expertise after decades of project delays. The EPA is simultaneously proposing regulatory changes to accelerate construction of data centers and power plants by allowing non-emitting structural work to begin before federal air permits are obtained, addressing long-standing infrastructure bottlenecks.
keyTakeaways: KHNP's partnership with Southern Company represents a strategic opening for advanced reactor deployment in the U.S. market despite previous Westinghouse IP restrictions, creating opportunities for investors focused on nuclear construction and technology licensing.

EPA's permitting reform could materially reduce capital deployment timelines for infrastructure projects, benefiting companies in power generation, data centers, and industrial construction—a meaningful tailwind for capex-heavy energy transition plays.

The convergence of Korean nuclear expertise entering U.S. markets alongside deregulatory momentum suggests accelerated buildout of both traditional and next-generation reactor capacity, signaling sector maturation and reduced execution risk in nuclear infrastructure.
newsletters.originalTitle: K-Nuclear

newsletters.title: Pro Rata
newsletters.summary: Anthropic is preparing to raise tens of billions at a record valuation while cracking down on unauthorized secondary stock sales through eight named platforms including Hiive and Forge, though enforcement is unlikely given potential IPO delays. Meanwhile, Fervo Energy completed a $1.9 billion IPO at $27 per share, significantly exceeding its original $21-24 range, marking a major win for climate tech investors amid reduced federal renewable energy support.
keyTakeaways: Secondary market trading in private tech shares remains largely unpoliced despite issuer warnings—investors should verify share authenticity through official cap table records, as Anthropic's blanket rejection of unauthorized transfers could leave some secondary buyers holding worthless claims.

Anthropic's inability to enforce transfer restrictions across SPVs and derivative structures signals the limits of private company control over their own cap tables, a problem that will likely plague mega-IPO transitions unless regulators intervene.

Climate tech IPO activity is rebounding with outsized demand—Fervo's ability to price 12.5% above range guidance despite political headwinds suggests strong institutional appetite for decarbonization infrastructure, creating opportunities for downstream investment in the geothermal energy supply chain.
newsletters.originalTitle: Axios Pro Rata: Anthropic authorization

newsletters.title: Latitude Media
newsletters.summary: Breakthrough Energy Ventures led a $105 million Series A funding round for Sygaldry Technologies, a quantum computing startup focused on making AI models more energy efficient by integrating quantum processors as co-processors alongside classical GPUs in data centers. The investment reflects growing climate tech investor interest in quantum computing as a potential solution to the unsustainable energy demands of large language models, with Sygaldry's co-founder claiming quantum integration could deliver "dramatic" speed-ups of hundreds of thousands of times faster for algorithms.
keyTakeaways: Quantum computing is emerging as a climate tech investment thesis within clean energy VCs, with BEV positioning quantum-AI integration as addressing the fundamental energy sustainability problem of modern LLMs rather than a peripheral play.

Sygaldry's modular architecture strategy—designing quantum systems as plug-and-play co-processors in existing data center infrastructure rather than requiring complete platform redesigns—lowers adoption barriers and could accelerate commercial deployment across hyperscalers.

Enterprise AI customers' rising pressure on Big Tech over energy consumption is directly influencing infrastructure investment decisions, creating tailwinds for efficiency-focused quantum and alternative compute solutions in the $100M+ funding rounds.
newsletters.originalTitle: The promise of quantum for AI

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: A months-long closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a critical global energy chokepoint, has failed to trigger expected market volatility, with oil trading below $110 per barrel despite representing the largest supply disruption on record. The newsletter features an interview with Jason Bordoff, founding director of Columbia's Center for Global Energy Policy, exploring why markets have remained resilient, whether the crisis signals a structural shift in global energy systems, and what policy lessons emerge for managing future energy crises.
keyTakeaways: Energy market resilience to supply shocks may be higher than historical models suggest, indicating either substantial spare capacity elsewhere, demand destruction, or market pricing mechanisms that are more efficient than previously understood—with implications for infrastructure investment priorities.

A sustained Hormuz closure could accelerate permanent shifts in global energy architecture, including diversified shipping routes, strategic reserves deployment, and renewable energy adoption, creating both stranded asset risks and growth opportunities in alternative energy infrastructure.

The muted market response challenges conventional geopolitical risk premiums and may influence how policymakers and investors price tail risks in energy markets going forward, potentially reducing hedging demand and affecting energy company valuations.
newsletters.originalTitle: What’s Truly Baffling About the Strait of Hormuz Energy Crisis

newsletters.title: Latitude Media
newsletters.summary: The Trump administration is considering an executive order to accelerate deployment of advanced transmission technologies (ATTs) that can increase grid capacity by 10-100% within years, addressing a critical bottleneck for AI data center expansion and manufacturing reshoring. ATTs like dynamic line rating and reconductoring offer a faster alternative to the decade-long traditional transmission line construction timeline, with the Department of Energy reporting potential capacity increases of up to 100 GW if deployed at scale.
keyTakeaways: Policy catalyst emerging: A pending executive order could unlock rapid deployment of ATTs, creating immediate investment opportunities in grid modernization technologies and removing a major constraint on hyperscaler expansion into new markets

Hyperscaler demand creating pull-through: Data center operators' willingness to directly fund grid infrastructure improvements is shifting financial incentives and could accelerate ATT adoption faster than traditional utility-led approaches

Market timing window: The convergence of AI infrastructure demand, regulatory support, and proven ATT technologies suggests significant capital allocation opportunities in grid tech and utility modernization over the next 2-3 years before traditional transmission buildout accelerates
newsletters.originalTitle: Latitude Dispatch: Will the AI boom speed up advanced transmission?

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Enhanced geothermal pioneer Fervo Energy completed its Nasdaq IPO this week, raising nearly $1.9 billion—50% above its initial $1.3 billion target—as investor enthusiasm for next-generation geothermal technology intensifies. The company's 2023 breakthrough using fracking technology to extract energy from hot, dry rocks found virtually anywhere globally has catalyzed a dramatic shift in investor interest, with the stock surging over 30% on its first trading day to above $36 per share.
keyTakeaways: Fervo's successful IPO and significant oversubscription signals strong institutional appetite for dispatchable renewable baseload power, particularly as AI data center energy demands accelerate—suggesting geothermal may attract substantial capital flows previously concentrated in nuclear and solar.

The company's $1.9 billion war chest targets its flagship 500-megawatt Cape Station plant in Utah, indicating early-stage commercialization of enhanced geothermal systems (EGS) technology could achieve rapid scaling if performance targets are met.

Geothermal funding has shifted from marginal ($84 million federally three years ago) to mainstream, reflecting a macro reallocation toward 24/7 carbon-free baseload solutions that can leverage existing oil-and-gas infrastructure and supply chains.
newsletters.originalTitle: Fervo Is a Hot Stock

newsletters.title: Maddyness
newsletters.summary: Harvey, an $11 billion American legal AI unicorn, is expanding into Europe by opening a Paris office and deploying its platform with major French law firms including August Debouzy, Bredin Prat, and CMS Francis Lefebvre, while also securing a partnership with Paris Saint-Germain. The newsletter also highlights Superprof's consolidation strategy with its 19th acquisition in 13 years targeting €100M revenue, and Alexandre Yazdi's acquisition of DC Company (Konbini and Le Gorafi media properties).
keyTakeaways: Enterprise AI software is driving cross-Atlantic expansion: Harvey's Paris entry signals successful product-market fit in legal tech, with immediate adoption by marquee French law firms—indicating strong European demand for specialized AI tools in high-value professional services

Consolidation plays are maturing: Superprof's acquisition strategy (19 deals in 13 years) demonstrates how European startups scale through serial M&A rather than organic growth alone, with clear revenue targets ($100M) driving acquisition rationale

Media/content M&A remains active: Voodoo's CEO acquiring Konbini and Le Gorafi suggests digital media consolidation continues as a capital allocation strategy, though valuations and strategic rationale warrant scrutiny given platform commoditization

newsletters.title: Latitude Media
newsletters.summary: AI-powered development tools are enabling renewable energy and power infrastructure teams to dramatically scale project pipelines without proportional headcount increases. Paces Agent, an automation platform for energy development workflows, has deployed on 3.2 gigawatts of project pipeline and allows teams to manage 5x more projects with identical staffing by automating siting, permitting, environmental diligence, and grid interconnection processes.
keyTakeaways: Productivity multipliers in capital-intensive infrastructure are reaching 5x throughput with flat headcount—this signals a structural shift where AI-augmented development becomes table stakes for competing in energy buildout, favoring well-capitalized or AI-integrated developers
