The bottleneck in renewable deployment is shifting from capital availability to execution speed and regulatory navigation; winners will be teams that can move fastest through permitting and interconnection queues, creating competitive moats around AI-enabled workflows

With AI demand, data centers, and electrification driving unprecedented pipeline requirements, automation of backend development workflows (siting, permitting, diligence) becomes a critical infrastructure for the energy transition, potentially creating venture-scale opportunities in vertical SaaS for climate tech
newsletters.originalTitle: The teams building more megawatts aren't adding headcount

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: AI startup valuations have reached unprecedented levels, with Series A pre-money valuations hitting $78 million in Q1 2026—31% higher than 2025 and 84% above non-AI peers—but venture returns math is increasingly stressed as the IPO market remains weak and public companies accept minimal step-ups from private valuations. Fund performance data shows 2019-2020 vintage VCs have five-year DPIs of just 0.12x-0.13x, the worst since the financial crisis, suggesting the exit market has not yet proven it can support current AI valuations without significant haircuts similar to what software companies experienced.
keyTakeaways: AI companies command 55% higher Series B valuations than non-AI peers, but 2025 IPOs required median haircuts to just 1.1x of the last private round—a major red flag for whether current AI valuations will survive public market scrutiny

Fund DPI data from 2019-2020 vintages shows 0.12x-0.13x five-year returns, the weakest since 2008, meaning VCs deploying into AI today are building on a foundation of failed exits and need to recalibrate return expectations

Secondary market concentration—top 20 startups accounting for 86.4% of Q4 trading value—reveals investor skepticism about supporting anything outside elite AI companies at current valuations, signaling a potential valuation reset ahead
newsletters.originalTitle: AI's unproven return math

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: The Trump administration is considering a federal gasoline tax suspension following state-level moves in Indiana and Kentucky, with the White House acknowledging the roughly $500 million weekly cost but framing it as necessary relief from a 50% price surge since the Iran War began. Meanwhile, major pipeline operators Williams Companies and Enbridge are advancing natural gas infrastructure projects in the Northeast, signaling a shift as Democratic governors previously opposed to pipelines show newfound openness to the infrastructure needed to transport Marcellus Shale gas.
keyTakeaways: Federal gas tax suspension legislation is actively being pursued by both parties (Senator Josh Hawley introduced a bill Monday), creating near-term political momentum for passage despite the $500M weekly revenue hit—investors should monitor Congressional action and potential energy sector volatility from price relief measures.

Major pipeline operators (Williams Companies, Enbridge) are seizing the regulatory window to expand natural gas infrastructure in the Northeast, suggesting significant capex deployment opportunities as Democratic opposition softens under energy security concerns.

The shift in Democratic governors' stance on pipeline infrastructure represents a potential inflection point in climate-vs-energy policy debates, potentially unlocking billions in stranded natural gas infrastructure projects across the Northeast and other regions.
newsletters.originalTitle: Pilgrim’s Pipeline

newsletters.title: Term Sheet
newsletters.summary: Roadrunner, an AI-native configure-price-quote (CPQ) software startup, has raised $27 million in seed and Series A funding from Kleiner Perkins and Founders Fund, marking Kleiner's first incubation since Glean. Founded in 2025 by Joubin Mirzadegan (a Kleiner partner), Ajay Natarajan, and Eugene Shao, the company targets the quote-to-cash space with a new approach called PQA (prompt, quote, approve) that leverages AI to handle increasingly complex pricing models.
keyTakeaways: VCs are backing enterprise software founders solving unglamorous but high-friction problems—CPQ is a $10B+ market opportunity because it controls deal velocity for software companies with complex pricing, making it a natural wedge for market expansion.

The insider incubation model continues to work: Kleiner partners spinning out to found startups with internal validation and fast founder-investor alignment significantly increases odds of early traction (Roadrunner closed seven-figure deals within three months of prototype).

AI is reshaping SaaS infrastructure layers—traditional CPQ systems built for static subscription tiers are obsolete as pricing models proliferate (usage-based, seat-based, hybrid), creating a wide-open greenfield for new entrants with AI-native architectures.
newsletters.originalTitle: Meet the first startup incubated at Kleiner since Glean

newsletters.title: Pro Rata
newsletters.summary: Yale University's Budget Lab released a major analysis arguing that taxing carried interest as ordinary income could generate over $100 billion in federal revenue over a decade—substantially more than previous Congressional Budget Office estimates. The research, based on granular K-1 tax data rather than SEC filings, reframes the carried interest debate from a fairness issue to a significant revenue opportunity, potentially shifting political momentum toward reform despite industry opposition.
keyTakeaways: Carried interest tax reform could generate $100B+ in federal revenue over 10 years, making it a fiscally material policy change that politicians can now justify on both fairness and funding grounds—this dramatically strengthens reform advocates' position versus the historical $47.5B estimates.

VC and PE firms face heightened regulatory risk as the data foundation for policy debates shifts from extrapolation to granular partnership-level analysis, suggesting future tax policy will be harder to counter with broad industry arguments about economic impact.

The timing and credibility of Yale's research during an election cycle creates a window where carried interest taxation could move from a niche policy debate to mainstream fiscal legislation, making tax treatment a critical planning variable for fund managers and their limited partners.
newsletters.originalTitle: Axios Pro Rata: Carried intrigue

newsletters.title: Benedict Evans
newsletters.summary: OpenAI and Anthropic are establishing new deployment-focused ventures with major financial backing to help enterprise clients integrate AI into their operations, addressing the complexity of AI adoption at scale. The White House is shifting toward more cautious AI regulation focused on tangible risks like energy consumption, cybersecurity threats, and privacy concerns. Meanwhile, the competitive landscape is intensifying as incumbents match AI capabilities and PE firms position themselves as intermediaries driving AI-driven operational efficiency.
keyTakeaways: AI labs are betting on dedicated deployment companies (Anthropic's partnership with PE and Goldman Sachs, OpenAI's '$10bn Deployment Company') as their go-to-market strategy, recognizing that enterprise AI adoption requires professional services integration and management support, not just raw technology access.

PE investors are emerging as critical middlemen in AI commercialization, leveraging portfolio company access and operational expertise to drive efficiency gains—this represents a significant new capital allocation vector where AI becomes a lever for operational returns rather than standalone technology investment.

Regulatory environment is shifting from 'move fast' ideology toward specific, actionable concerns (cybersecurity, data center energy demand, education, privacy), creating both compliance costs and political opportunities—this will likely accelerate consolidation around compliant platforms and disadvantage smaller competitors.
newsletters.originalTitle: Benedict's Newsletter: No. 642

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Climeworks CEO Christoph Gebald argues that carbon removal technology buildout should be considered an integral cost of AI infrastructure development. As data centers consume massive amounts of power to train and run AI models—often sourced from natural gas due to deployment speed constraints—the carbon emissions created necessitate investment in direct air capture and similar carbon removal solutions as an offset mechanism.
keyTakeaways: Carbon removal is emerging as a necessary operational cost layer for AI infrastructure, creating a new market opportunity for cleantech companies like Climeworks as AI data centers scale rapidly across the U.S.

The constraint of natural gas powering new data centers due to speed-to-market advantages over renewable energy creates a structural demand for carbon offset solutions, presenting a potential de facto mandate for carbon removal adoption.

This positions carbon removal from a speculative cleantech bet into essential enabling infrastructure for the AI boom, potentially accelerating venture and growth capital into the direct air capture sector.
newsletters.originalTitle: The AI Boom Needs Carbon Removal

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Democratic Maine Senate candidate Graham Platner, challenging Republican incumbent Susan Collins, released an energy plan focused on a national electricity rate freeze to combat high power prices driven by data center construction. In an exclusive interview, Platner expressed deep concerns about AI and data center infrastructure's regulatory gaps, labor market impacts, and surveillance risks, signaling his campaign is developing a specific policy plan on the issue while remaining cautiously open to construction slowdowns paired with policy development.
keyTakeaways: Data center regulation is emerging as a critical political issue in 2024 midterms, with candidates staking positions on moratoriums and construction freezes—this signals potential federal policy risk for cloud infrastructure companies and AI-driven tech buildouts.

Platner's emphasis on pairing any data center restrictions with comprehensive AI/infrastructure policy suggests future regulations will likely include labor protections, surveillance safeguards, and grid reliability measures, not just construction bans—infrastructure investors should prepare for multi-faceted compliance requirements.

The framing of data centers as a driver of electricity rate inflation creates populist political momentum for restrictions, meaning real estate developers, power companies, and hyperscalers face accelerating regulatory headwinds that could reshape capital allocation toward mature, proven energy infrastructure over speculative buildouts.
newsletters.originalTitle: What a Ban on Data Centers Has to Do

newsletters.title: Maddyness
newsletters.summary: French startups raised €126 million this week with an average ticket size of €14 million, with AI-focused companies accounting for €109 million of that total across four deals. OpenAI is strategically embedding itself into the French startup ecosystem through partnerships with key infrastructure players like Station F and Hexa, mirroring successful cloud giant playbooks. The newsletter also highlights how predictive AI models are now capable of real-world applications, with Avisia's model successfully predicting France's 2026 World Cup squad three days before official announcement.
keyTakeaways: AI startups are capturing 86% of weekly funding volume in France (€109M of €126M), signaling strong investor conviction in the sector and potential crowding in this subsegment

OpenAI's systematic partnerships with early-stage infrastructure (Station F, Hexa) represent a direct-to-source strategy to influence startup selection and adoption, which could disadvantage competing cloud and AI platforms seeking French market penetration

Predictive AI models demonstrating real-world accuracy on complex domain problems (sports team selection) validates commercial applications beyond generative AI, suggesting expanding investment opportunities across vertical-specific AI solutions
newsletters.originalTitle: 12.05 - Coupe du monde 2026 : l’IA prédit la liste de Deschamps trois jours avant son annonce

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: Limited Partners have significantly lowered return expectations for private equity funds, with realistic targets now at 15-20% net IRR for top managers and 1.8x-2.3x net MOIC at the fund level, down from the 3-4x returns expected during the 2010-2019 ultra-low interest rate era. This recalibration reflects the impact of higher interest rates and longer holding periods, though the VC secondary market continues expanding with $112B in activity as it works to democratize liquidity beyond elite managers.
keyTakeaways: LP return expectations have contracted materially: top-quartile PE funds should now target 1.8x-2.3x net MOIC versus the historical 2.5x-3.5x gross targets, a fundamental shift driven by post-pandemic interest rate increases and extended company holding periods.

Lower bar on returns does not translate to easier fundraising—managers still face intense pressure to deliver meaningful performance, suggesting that valuation discipline and operational improvements matter more than ever in a capital-constrained environment.

The VC secondary market's growth to $112B signals institutional acceptance of alternative liquidity solutions, positioning secondary funds to capture value as GPs struggle with longer J-curves and LPs demand earlier exit opportunities.
newsletters.originalTitle: LPs lower the bar

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: A climate and energy newsletter covering critical infrastructure challenges including Qatar's first LNG export through the Strait of Hormuz since the Iran War began, Eversource Energy's explicit rejection of data center development in New England to protect electricity prices for residential customers, and emerging geopolitical tensions affecting battery and solar panel supply chains. The piece also highlights Florida's new mandate requiring large data centers to cover their own service costs rather than passing them to ratepayers, signaling a political backlash against AI infrastructure subsidies across both Democratic and Republican jurisdictions.
keyTakeaways: Data center buildout is becoming a utility regulation flashpoint with bipartisan opposition emerging—Eversource and Florida's DeSantis both explicitly rejecting cost-shifting to ratepayers, creating an unfavorable capital allocation environment for hyperscale AI infrastructure in these regions and potentially constraining AI company expansion plans.

Supply chain vulnerabilities for renewable energy components remain acute; Strait of Hormuz closure is squeezing battery storage and solar panel ingredients, creating margin pressure and investment risks for clean energy hardware manufacturers dependent on Persian Gulf trade routes.

Regional grid stability is becoming a competitive advantage—New England's restraint on data centers has kept electricity prices more stable than mid-Atlantic PJM systems, potentially attracting other industries and residential investment while penalizing AI infrastructure operators seeking cheaper power markets.
newsletters.originalTitle: Strait Through

newsletters.title: Term Sheet
newsletters.summary: Frame Security, a New York and Tel Aviv-based cybersecurity startup, raised $50 million led by Index Ventures to address the evolution of social engineering attacks beyond traditional phishing. The company's platform uses AI to create hyper-personalized simulations—including deepfake voice calls and video attacks—to train employees on realistic threats, with early customers including AlphaSense and Louis Dreyfus Company signing multi-year deals valued from mid-five to mid-six figures.
keyTakeaways: The security awareness training market is shifting from generic, compliance-driven programs to AI-powered, company-specific threat simulations—representing a potential $10B+ market opportunity by 2027, with Frame already securing 20-30 enterprise customers at significant contract values.

Despite 96% of organizations having security awareness training, 90% of breaches still involve human error, creating a large TAM for vendors who can move beyond checkbox training to measurable risk reduction; investors should evaluate whether companies are building replaceable tools or defensible platforms.

Israeli cyber talent with proven startup pedigree (founders from Unit 8200, Wiz, and CrowdStrike-acquired Bionic) combined with $50M in capital signals investor conviction that human-centric security is becoming a critical infrastructure category as AI makes social engineering attacks exponentially more convincing and cheaper to execute.
newsletters.originalTitle: The (bad) phishing email is dead

newsletters.title: Pro Rata
newsletters.summary: OpenAI launched DeployCo, a $4 billion consulting services venture with a $10 billion pre-money valuation, backed by major PE firms and notably three top consulting companies (Bain, McKinsey, Capgemini) that may face disintermediation risk. In a major milestone after five years of fundraising struggles, OnlyFans secured $535 million from Architect Capital at a $3.15 billion valuation, suggesting that even highly profitable platforms face investor reluctance due to content concerns despite generating $1.4 billion in net revenue.
keyTakeaways: Large consulting firms are directly funding AI infrastructure plays, potentially creating conflicts of interest and accelerating their own disruption as clients gain direct access to frontier lab capabilities through DeployCo partnership.

OnlyFans' $3.15 billion valuation despite $684 million in pre-tax profit signals that investor sentiment around business models remains a significant valuation constraint, even when fundamentals are strong—a cautionary tale for founders in morally ambiguous sectors.

The frontier AI lab ecosystem is consolidating around major PE capital (TPG, Advent, Bain Capital leading DeployCo), suggesting that venture capital competition for these deals is diminishing and mega-funds are securing preferred founder relationships.
newsletters.originalTitle: Axios Pro Rata: OpenAdvisory

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Climate tech funding is experiencing a bifurcation as early-stage companies face a funding drought amid Trump administration policy uncertainty, while mature technologies with proven cost curves continue attracting capital. New reports from Sightline Climate, Silicon Valley Bank, and J.P. Morgan reveal that investors are shifting risk tolerance away from pre-commercial ventures toward established solutions, creating a two-tier market in the climate tech sector.
keyTakeaways: Early-stage climate tech companies face significantly reduced access to capital as venture investors adopt a risk-averse posture during political uncertainty, suggesting founders should focus on demonstrating commercialization pathways rather than pure innovation metrics.

The bifurcation trend indicates a flight to quality—mature climate technologies with competitive cost structures are de-risking and may see consolidation opportunities as weaker early-stage competitors struggle for funding.

Policy volatility (particularly around wind, solar, and EV support) is creating structural headwinds for the climate tech sector that cannot be offset by technological progress alone; investors should model scenario planning around regulatory rollback rather than assuming policy support.
newsletters.originalTitle: The Climate Tech Funding Drought

newsletters.title: Maddyness
newsletters.summary: French startup OpsMill raised €12 million to build infrastructure automation platforms for AI-driven systems, addressing the challenge of fragmented and difficult-to-exploit IT data. The newsletter also reports a concerning trend: follow-on funding rates for French startups have collapsed from 46% in 2020 to just 13.6% for the 2023 cohort, while major tech companies like Cisco are establishing acceleration hubs in Paris to support AI infrastructure startups.
keyTakeaways: OpsMill's €12M funding signals strong investor appetite for AI infrastructure plays that solve data integration problems—a critical bottleneck for enterprise AI adoption in Europe

The dramatic decline in Series B completion rates (46% to 13.6%) indicates a significant capital crunch for French startups post-seed, suggesting selectivity is intensifying and only the strongest companies will secure follow-on rounds

Strategic corporate investment in European AI infrastructure (Cisco's Station F hub) indicates multinational tech firms are hedging their AI bets by building direct relationships with early-stage infrastructure companies rather than acquiring later
newsletters.originalTitle: 11.05 - OpsMill lève 12 millions d’euros pour devenir la colonne vertébrale des infrastructures pilotées par l’IA

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: VC firms are increasingly deploying AI agents as junior analysts to handle deal sourcing, research, and portfolio monitoring tasks traditionally done by junior staff. Firms like Patron Fund and Pebblebed are creating agents with distinct personalities and backgrounds to avoid groupthink, with some agents collaborating via Slack-style platforms and daily stand-ups. The trend raises questions about headcount economics in VC while highlighting challenges in ensuring AI diversity and preventing homogenized decision-making.
keyTakeaways: AI agents are moving beyond automation into substantive analysis roles—VC firms are now evaluating agents with the same rigor as hiring junior analysts, considering background, perspective, and fit with firm culture, suggesting this is a permanent shift in VC staffing models.

Venture firms are discovering that agent diversity prevents analytical blind spots; ensuring agents pull from different data sources and possess distinct viewpoints is becoming a critical operational concern for avoiding groupthink in deal evaluation.

This adoption signals a structural change in VC unit economics where firms can scale deal flow analysis and monitoring without proportional headcount growth, potentially accelerating deal velocity while raising questions about institutional knowledge retention and human judgment in final decision-making.
newsletters.originalTitle: The new star employees of VC firms

newsletters.title: Fintech Business Weekly
newsletters.summary: Parker, a fintech startup offering SMB banking and credit card products, abruptly filed for Chapter 7 bankruptcy on May 4th after a potential $90 million acquisition by tax software company Avalara fell through at the last minute. The company, which had raised $58 million in equity funding from investors including Valar Ventures and Y Combinator, reported $50-100 million in assets and liabilities with 100-200 creditors. The shutdown was executed by Patriot Bank, Parker's banking partner, rather than by Parker itself, despite the company having remaining operational runway.
keyTakeaways: Acquisition-dependent fintech business models create binary risk: Parker had sufficient capital and operations to continue but was shut down when its acquirer withdrew, highlighting how dependent growth-stage fintechs have become on exit scenarios rather than sustainable unit economics.

Banking partnerships hold veto power over fintech viability: Patriot Bank's decision to terminate the program essentially forced Parker's bankruptcy, demonstrating that fintech companies are vulnerable to partner decisions outside their direct control, regardless of their own runway or funding position.

The SMB fintech lending space continues to consolidate downward: This adds to a pattern of failures in this sector (Synapse/Evolve referenced as two-year anniversary), suggesting structural challenges in unit economics or market demand for these products despite substantial venture capital deployment.
newsletters.originalTitle:
Another Fintech Bankruptcy: SMB Banking & Credit Card Startup Parker Files
 Chapter 7

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: This week's newsletter covers Microsoft's pullback on clean energy commitments, growing local opposition to data center expansion in 2026, and the Trump administration's impact on the American energy industry. The publication also highlights emerging opportunities in fast-charging battery technology for robotics and discusses permitting reform prospects under the current administration.
keyTakeaways: Microsoft's retreat from clean energy commitments signals potential challenges in corporate sustainability pledges, suggesting investors should scrutinize whether major tech companies can meet their climate goals while scaling AI infrastructure demands.
Rising local opposition to data centers presents both a risk to tech expansion plans and a capital allocation opportunity in distributed energy solutions and permitting acceleration technologies.

Regulatory uncertainty around energy policy creates volatility in clean tech investments, but permitting reform momentum under the current administration could accelerate deployment of alternative energy infrastructure and fast-charging battery solutions.
newsletters.originalTitle: Microsoft’s Clean Energy Pullback, the Murder of an Energy Industry, and More

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: Private equity fundraising is consolidating dramatically around the largest managers despite mediocre returns, with the top 10 funds capturing 61.1% of capital raised through mid-April 2026. LPs are prioritizing scale, convenience, and established relationships over performance differentiation, as rising rates and distribution droughts have fundamentally shifted capital allocation behavior. This trend will likely persist until meaningful exit activity accelerates, which remains constrained by macroeconomic uncertainty.
keyTakeaways: Scale is winning regardless of performance: megafunds with ordinary historical returns are capturing the majority of LP capital inflows, suggesting that institutional comfort and portfolio convenience now outweigh raw performance metrics in LP decision-making.

Middle-market funds offer structural opportunity: despite historically outperforming megafunds (with top-to-bottom decile spreads of 13 percentage points versus 7 for megafunds), they are being starved of capital, creating potential alpha for LPs who can identify differentiated managers.

Exit activity remains the critical macro catalyst: continued realization pipeline weakness keeps LP deployment capacity constrained and fundraising recovery stalled; when exits normalize, capital distribution will shift unevenly and permanently reshape the PE competitive landscape.
newsletters.originalTitle: Scale wins, even when returns don't

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Microsoft is reconsidering its ambitious 2030 clean energy target of achieving hourly renewable energy matching, a significant step beyond its 2025 milestone of matching 100% of annual electricity consumption with solar and wind power. While the potential pullback could signal waning corporate climate commitment, the article suggests the company's original goal was unrealistic and the strategic shift may not undermine broader climate ambition.
keyTakeaways: Microsoft's 2030 hourly matching goal was essentially unachievable with current infrastructure, making the reported delay a rational recalibration rather than climate backtracking—important for assessing which corporate sustainability commitments are credible long-term bets

The distinction between annual renewable matching (accomplished) and hourly matching (abandoned) reveals how vague ESG commitments can mislead investors; clearer metrics and realistic timelines are needed for due diligence on climate-focused portfolio companies

Regulatory challenges to utility business models (Pennsylvania, New Jersey, Berkshire Hathaway disputes with PJM) and political headwinds against wind energy suggest the bottleneck for corporate clean energy goals is infrastructure and policy, not corporate willingness—a macro constraint for energy transition investments
newsletters.originalTitle: The Holy Grail of Energy Goals

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: Healthcare services PE activity fell 16% year-over-year in Q1 2026 to 79 deals worth $8.4 billion, but elder care and home-based care remain attractive due to demographic tailwinds and active strategic buyers. Enterprise SaaS unicorn valuations are recovering from the software sell-off, packaged food dealmaking hit a nine-year high with 144 Q1 transactions, and brain-computer interface technology is drawing significant VC investment despite unclear market size and user acquisition dynamics.
keyTakeaways: Elder care and home-based care are outperforming other healthcare services subsectors because aging demographics create predictable demand and multiple strategic exit options, making them easier to underwrite than clinic-rollup platforms.

Healthcare services PE has shifted from indiscriminate capital deployment to selective investing—deals are down but big transactions like General Atlantic's $3 billion TEAM Services Group acquisition and Kinderhook's Enhabit buyout show conviction remains where operators add measurable local value.

Packaged food and packaged goods are emerging as PE favorites with 144 Q1 transactions, while emerging tech like quantum computing and brain-computer interfaces are attracting major VC rounds, suggesting capital is rotating toward both defensive sectors and high-upside moonshots as traditional software exits remain uncertain.
newsletters.originalTitle: Who wants a brain implant?

newsletters.title: Deep Tech Newsletter
newsletters.summary: The U.S. Department of Defense has announced sweeping production agreements with Lockheed Martin, BAE Systems, Honeywell, and RTX to quadruple output of critical missile systems over seven years, representing tens of billions in investment and the largest guided-munitions production expansion since the Cold War. The expansion was triggered by rapid depletion of U.S. missile stockpiles during Ukraine support, with concerns that even quadrupled production may prove insufficient for national defense requirements.
keyTakeaways: Major defense contractors (LMT, RTX, BAE, HON) are securing multi-billion dollar production contracts with guaranteed long-term demand, creating compelling investment thesis for defense industrial base consolidation and modernization plays through 2033.

The missile shortage reveals critical supply chain vulnerabilities in U.S. defense capabilities, signaling sustained elevated government spending on military production and potential tailwinds for the broader aerospace & defense sector beyond traditional procurement cycles.

Geopolitical tensions are reshaping capital allocation priorities: climate tech faces headwinds while defense tech, energy generation, AI infrastructure, and grid modernization are receiving accelerated funding and policy support, reflecting shifting risk assessments among institutional investors.
newsletters.originalTitle: DTN 161: The Great US Missile Shortage

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: The Trump administration is systematically blocking onshore wind projects through Department of Defense approvals while paying developers like TotalEnergies $1 billion to abandon offshore wind leases, creating an "uninvestable climate" for the renewable energy sector. This strategy is generating significant blowback, including investigations from House Democrats and California regulators, and causing institutional investors like the New York State Common Retirement Fund to reconsider their stakes in companies accepting the buyout offers.
keyTakeaways: Regulatory obstruction rather than legal victories is emerging as the primary mechanism to kill wind development—blocking 150+ onshore projects through non-transparent DoD approvals creates deal risk for wind developers and investors regardless of courtroom outcomes

The $1 billion buyout scheme is unraveling as scrutiny reveals no mandated fossil fuel investments and potential misuse of federal settlement funds, suggesting government-sponsored project cancellations face political and legal vulnerability that could reverse under future administrations

Institutional capital is beginning to penalize companies accepting anti-renewable deals (e.g., TotalEnergies' $1.6B pension fund stake under review), signaling that ESG-focused LPs may create investment friction for companies perceived as abandoning climate commitments for government subsidies
newsletters.originalTitle: Blowback

newsletters.title: Pro Rata
newsletters.summary: Investors are increasingly viewing sports as a defensive hedge against AI disruption, betting that live athletic competition remains immune to automation—driving up valuations across professional leagues, youth sports, and luxury motorsports. The Seattle Seahawks sale, potentially led by ArcelorMittal CEO Aditya Mittal, exemplifies this trend, while parallel moves in micro-mobility show the IPO market testing lesser-known unicorns like Lime, which lost $59 million on $887 million in revenue in 2025 despite peak valuations of $2.4 billion.
keyTakeaways: Sports investments are viewed as recession-resistant and AI-proof assets; expect continued premium valuations and PE participation in team ownership despite franchise risks from league fragmentation and upcoming NFL media rights negotiations.
Youth sports present better risk-adjusted returns than spectator leagues because parents are the paying customer base rather than fickle audiences—PE funds are shifting capital accordingly.

Late-stage venture companies with persistent losses are still accessing IPO markets; Lime's $250 million raise target at a massive loss suggests investor appetite for growth over profitability, though Bird's bankruptcy shows the downside risk in this sector.
newsletters.originalTitle: Axios Pro Rata: Playing defense

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: PJM Interconnection, which operates the nation's largest electricity grid serving 65 million Americans across multiple states including New Jersey and Pennsylvania, is facing proposed regulatory reforms that could fundamentally restructure how utilities operate under the traditional regulatory compact. These reforms address concerns about rising electricity prices and surging demand from data centers, with both state-level and grid operator proposals seeking to reimagine infrastructure planning and capital cost recovery mechanisms.
keyTakeaways: Regulatory overhaul of the largest U.S. grid operator signals growing pressure on the traditional utility monopoly model, creating opportunities for infrastructure modernization companies and potential disruption for legacy utility investors

Data center-driven load growth is forcing a reassessment of how grid capacity is planned and funded, which could redirect capital allocation toward distributed energy resources, grid modernization tech, and alternative infrastructure financing models

Multi-state coordination on grid regulation (PJM, New Jersey, Pennsylvania) suggests regulatory fragmentation risk for utilities but potential upside for companies positioned in grid flexibility, demand management, and resilient infrastructure solutions
newsletters.originalTitle: Regulatory Reform Is Headed for the Nation’s Largest Grid

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: Limited partners are increasingly pursuing co-investments, secondaries, and direct channels to access high-valued AI startups, but face significant challenges including company approval requirements, reliance on GP judgment, and expensive intermediaries charging unverified premiums. With Series D+ AI companies commanding median pre-money valuations of $4.7 billion—nearly four times non-AI peers—LPs must balance the cost of missing out against idiosyncratic venture risk and fee discipline, while private credit emerges as AI infrastructure's largest funding source at $800 billion through 2028.
keyTakeaways: Co-investments are becoming LP relationship deepening tools, but most sought-after AI startups tightly control cap tables, meaning LP access depends heavily on GP allocation, company approval, and timeline flexibility—introducing significant execution and relationship risk.

Intermediaries pose hidden cost and verification problems; brokers and secondary dealers charge substantial fee premiums often without proven access to target companies, requiring the same fee scrutiny LPs apply to co-investment programs.

Private credit now finances the majority of AI infrastructure buildout ($800 billion of $1.5 trillion needed through 2028), concentrating lending power among five lender groups including Blue Owl/PIMCO joint ventures and hyperscaler-backed funds, which could reshape capital availability and pricing for AI buildout.
newsletters.originalTitle: The price of chasing AI direct stakes

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: U.S. electricity prices reached new peaks in April 2026, rising 6.7% year-over-year with some regional markets like New Jersey and Washington D.C. experiencing 21-25% increases due to supply-demand imbalances in the PJM grid. Meanwhile, data center project cancellations hit record levels with at least 20 projects scrapped in Q1 2026 amid intense local political opposition, while Elon Musk's xAI facility in Memphis failed to deliver promised water recycling infrastructure, exemplifying growing sustainability concerns around AI infrastructure development.
keyTakeaways: Data center deployment faces unprecedented headwinds with 20+ cancellations in Q1 2026 alone—investors backing AI infrastructure, cloud computing, or energy-intensive tech should expect significantly longer approval timelines and higher community engagement costs

Regional electricity markets show acute stress signals, particularly in PJM where demand is outstripping supply by enough to drive double-digit price increases; this creates investment opportunities in grid modernization, storage, and distributed generation but also raises operating costs for power-intensive industries

The failure of major tech companies to fulfill sustainability commitments on data center projects signals reputational and regulatory risk for large-scale infrastructure plays; ESG compliance and community benefit agreements are now critical deal requirements rather than optional enhancements
newsletters.originalTitle: Up and Up

newsletters.title: Term Sheet
newsletters.summary: Gusto, the HR and payroll software company founded in 2012, has crossed $1 billion in 12-month trailing revenue as of February 2026, achieving a $9.3 billion valuation while serving over 500,000 customers. CEO Josh Reeves emphasized clarity by specifying this as actual cash revenue rather than ARR, highlighting how the company has evolved at the intersection of consumer and enterprise dynamics while maintaining a focus on underserved small business markets. The milestone reflects how dedicated execution in the SMB software segment can scale to enterprise-level success with major institutional investors including Ontario Teachers' Pension Plan, T. Rowe Price, General Catalyst, and CapitalG.
keyTakeaways: SMB-focused software can achieve billion-dollar revenue scale by operating at consumer-like efficiency rather than chasing large enterprise contracts, suggesting persistent capital opportunity in this segment despite traditional enterprise software assumptions.

The emphasis on reporting actual trailing revenue rather than ARR signals growing investor pressure for transparency in SaaS metrics, potentially reshaping how growth-stage companies present financial performance.

Gusto's 13+ year customer retention and accelerating growth trajectory indicates that foundational software categories (payroll, HR) serving small business remain defensible and expanding markets despite competitive pressures and new entrants.
newsletters.originalTitle: Gusto hits $1 billion

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: U.S. electricity prices rose 6.7% year-over-year in April 2026, marking another record high in a consistent upward trend, though the growth rate remains comparable to the past five years excluding the 2022 natural gas spike. Unlike Europe and Asia, the Iran-U.S. conflict has not impacted domestic power prices due to America's natural gas self-sufficiency and already-maxed export capacity, though individual states like New Jersey and Washington D.C. are experiencing much steeper increases of 21-25% due to supply-demand imbalances in the PJM electricity market.
keyTakeaways: Regional electricity markets are diverging significantly: while the national average grows at historical rates, PJM-region states face 3-4x faster price increases due to demand outpacing supply, creating targeted investment opportunities in grid modernization and generation capacity in the Northeast.

U.S. energy independence in natural gas provides insulation from geopolitical shocks that are hammering European and Asian power markets, but this advantage may erode if export infrastructure expands—monitoring LNG export policy changes is critical for long-term utility and energy infrastructure valuations.

Regulatory arbitrage and policy shifts create near-term volatility: California's carbon tax dividend timing shift illustrates how government decisions can create 50%+ price swings within months, suggesting investors should model policy calendars alongside commodity fundamentals when evaluating utility stocks and renewable energy contracts.
newsletters.originalTitle: The Relentless Rise of Electricity Prices

newsletters.title: Maddyness
newsletters.summary: Doctolib, a French healthcare unicorn, acquired British competitor Medicus Health and is entering the UK market with over £100 million in planned investment, marking its fifth European expansion. Alongside this strategic consolidation, European startups are aggressively pursuing M&A to strengthen AI capabilities: Batch acquired Moonfish AI to accelerate growth in AI-driven marketing software, while Genesis AI unveiled its robotics-focused large language model after raising $105 million.
keyTakeaways: Healthcare SaaS consolidation is accelerating across Europe with unicorns like Doctolib using M&A to enter new markets and compete against legacy medical software vendors with AI-enhanced tools.

AI-powered capabilities have become table stakes for growth; mid-stage European startups (Batch, Fairglow) are now pursuing external growth strategies to acquire AI/ML talent and technology rather than building in-house.

The regulatory environment and cybersecurity concerns around AI are rising in parallel with deployment—the Campus Cyber warning signals that enterprises remain unprepared for AI-accelerated vulnerability discovery, creating both risk and opportunity in security-focused investments.
newsletters.originalTitle: 07.05 - Doctolib rachète son concurrent britannique et s'implante au Royaume-Uni

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: The SEC is moving to eliminate Biden-era climate disclosure rules for public companies and shift from quarterly to semi-annual reporting, a deregulatory move that paradoxically could benefit climate action by reducing short-term pressure on companies. Meanwhile, wave energy startup Panthalassa raised $140 million in Series B funding led by Peter Thiel, signaling renewed investor interest in previously overlooked offshore renewable energy as data center demand grows.
keyTakeaways: Wave energy is emerging from dormancy with major venture capital backing; Panthalassa's $140M Series B suggests investor conviction that floating data centers solving thermal dissipation can unlock this long-stalled renewable sector.

Reduced quarterly reporting requirements could paradoxically accelerate climate-related investments by removing short-term profit pressure, allowing companies to pursue longer-horizon decarbonization projects without quarterly earnings penalties.

The regulatory shift creates a bifurcated market: companies may face less mandatory climate disclosure but gain operational flexibility to pursue sustained clean energy transitions, potentially reshaping how ESG and energy transition investments are evaluated.
newsletters.originalTitle: SEC Won’t Let Me See

newsletters.title: Term Sheet
newsletters.summary: District, an AI-powered marketplace platform founded by three Snapchat alumni, raised $14.7 million in seed funding led by Andreessen Horowitz and Kindred Ventures to help independent sellers build community-driven stores without technical expertise. The startup addresses a market shift toward personalized, real-time commerce experiences, positioning itself against legacy platforms like eBay and Amazon, with early traction including 1,000 businesses on the platform and notable customers like Stacked Golf generating $150,000 in weekly sales.
keyTakeaways: A16z and other top-tier VCs are betting on marketplace consolidation plays that aggregate multiple selling channels into single platforms—suggesting strong market demand for sellers to reduce platform fragmentation and operational complexity

The $7 trillion global ecommerce market is fragmenting away from centralized giants toward community-driven, creator-centric models, creating a genuine beachhead opportunity for platforms that can bundle commerce, community, and real-time interactions in one interface

Live shopping and creator-led commerce (evidenced by Whatnot's $11.5B valuation and creator influencers like Crazy Lamp Lady with 800K+ followers) represent the next wave of ecommerce disruption, and District is positioning itself as the infrastructure layer for this shift
newsletters.originalTitle: A16z-backed District wants to oust eBay

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: PitchBook's debut Unicorn Tracker reveals that Q1 2026 set records for unicorn funding and exits, but the growth is dangerously concentrated—just four companies (OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI, Waymo) captured 76.8% of $245.6 billion raised, while SpaceX's $250 billion acquisition of xAI alone represented 72.8% of exit value. Beyond these mega-deals, over half of 1,680 active unicorns haven't raised in two years, leaving $8.6 trillion in stale valuations that remain untested by market transactions and vulnerable to broad repricing.
keyTakeaways: Extreme deal concentration creates fragility: any single repricing of top-tier deals could flip market narratives from 'record-breaking' to 'disappointing' overnight, making this fundamentally different from the broad-based activity of 2021.

Massive cohort of 'zombie unicorns' with unpriced risk: over 840 active unicorns haven't raised in two years with valuations set during 2020-2021, creating a potential shock if forced repricing occurs—investors should reassess portfolio exposure to mid-tier unicorns before market correction.

Strategic mega-deals at scale are winning while mid-market fundraising stalls: the narrative of a broad exit market recovery is premature; real market reopening won't be confirmed until the pending IPOs of SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic price and perform, signaling whether demand extends beyond the elite tier.
newsletters.originalTitle: 'You have to sell at some point'

newsletters.title: Fintech Business Weekly
newsletters.summary: A new wave of AI-powered personal financial management (PFM) tools is emerging, but the podcast discussion questions whether AI can finally solve the fundamental challenges that have prevented a breakout company from emerging in this persistently difficult category. The episode also covers Bolt's continued struggles with layoffs and vendor payment issues, Congress's bipartisan PACE Act attempt to grant non-banks direct access to payment rails, and broader skepticism about American appetite for financial super apps.
keyTakeaways: AI-powered PFM remains an attractive but unproven category—investors should scrutinize whether current AI implementations address the core UX and monetization problems that have defeated previous entrants, not just add incremental features.

Bolt's deteriorating financial condition and inability to pay vendors signals that even well-funded fintech super apps struggle with unit economics and customer acquisition costs in the U.S. market, raising questions about the viability of the super app model domestically.

The PACE Act's potential passage reflects regulatory openness to non-bank payment access, but its practical utility is questionable given easier bank charter pathways and existing compliance burdens—this suggests regulatory solutions may lag behind market realities in fintech consolidation.
newsletters.originalTitle: Fintech Recap: Is AI the Unlock PFM Has Been Waiting For?

newsletters.title: Pro Rata
newsletters.summary: Private equity firms face unprecedented modeling challenges in deal underwriting due to rapid AI advancement, with executives admitting confidence in three-to-five year projections is essentially guesswork. Meanwhile, Nvidia and Corning announced a partnership to shift AI infrastructure from copper to glass optics, potentially reducing energy consumption significantly, signaling major infrastructure implications for the AI buildout.
keyTakeaways: PE firms must recalibrate exit multiple assumptions and hold period valuations as AI disruption timelines remain highly uncertain—traditional financial modeling frameworks are inadequate for sectors facing potential technology-driven obsolescence within standard holding periods.

The Nvidia-Corning partnership represents a fundamental shift in AI infrastructure economics that could impact both capex requirements and operational costs across data center portfolios, potentially affecting exit valuations for existing PE-backed infrastructure and software assets.

Despite acknowledged modeling uncertainty, PE dry powder continues to deploy aggressively, suggesting market participants are accepting higher risk or using shorter time horizons and more frequent mark-to-market adjustments than the traditional long-term hold thesis suggests.
newsletters.originalTitle: Axios Pro Rata: Model mess

newsletters.title: Latitude Media
newsletters.summary: A Brookings study reveals that data center job creation estimates are overstated by a factor of three when accounting for pre-existing regional growth trends. The real employment opportunity lies not in operating the facilities themselves—which require only 25-40 permanent staff per 100 megawatts—but in the energy infrastructure built to support them, particularly renewable generation projects like solar and storage that create significantly more jobs per dollar invested.
keyTakeaways: Data center developers bundling renewable energy generation (solar, wind, storage) with facility announcements create lasting local employment of 400-450 jobs per project, while standalone facilities offer minimal permanent job benefits—this distinction should drive site selection and community negotiation strategies for developers and investors seeking local support.

Political and media narratives around data center projects systematically overstate employment benefits by 3x, creating community backlash when promised jobs fail to materialize; transparent communication about the distinction between construction jobs and permanent staffing is becoming a competitive advantage as opposition to data centers reaches all-time highs.

The true value chain for data center investments extends to energy infrastructure providers—renewable generation companies, storage developers, and grid operators stand to capture more economic value and employment than data center operators themselves, suggesting capital should flow upstream to power generation projects rather than compute facilities.
newsletters.originalTitle: Where the data center jobs actually are

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Data center project cancellations due to local community opposition hit record levels in Q1 2026, with the trend accelerating nationwide and expected to set new all-time records in coming weeks, according to exclusive data from Heatmap Pro's intelligence platform. The bipartisan backlash reflects growing concerns about environmental and infrastructure impacts of large-scale data center development, exemplified by projects like the Sentinel Grove Technology Park near Port St. Lucie, Florida. This emerging opposition pattern signals potential regulatory and permitting challenges for the data center and AI infrastructure build-out that has been central to recent market valuations.
keyTakeaways: Data center developers and their investors face accelerating permitting and community opposition risks—Q1 2026 saw record cancellations, suggesting this is not a localized phenomenon but a structural headwind for infrastructure projects

Companies heavily dependent on rapid data center expansion (hyperscalers, AI infrastructure plays) may face extended timelines and higher project costs as permitting becomes more contested across regions

The bipartisan nature of opposition suggests this could translate into regulatory action at state and federal levels, making permitting reform a critical policy variable for capital-intensive tech infrastructure investments
newsletters.originalTitle: Exclusive: Data Center Opposition Explodes

newsletters.title: Maddyness
newsletters.summary: LegalPlace raised €70 million and acquired its historic rival Legalstart, consolidating France's legal tech market to strengthen competitive positioning and prepare for European expansion. Separately, Lithosquare secured €22 million in funding for an AI-powered platform designed to accelerate the discovery of critical mineral deposits, addressing the urgent need for over 1,000 new mining sites by 2040.
keyTakeaways: Consolidation in legal tech is accelerating in Europe, with market leaders deploying significant capital (€70M+) to acquire competitors rather than compete independently, signaling a maturation phase where scale and geographic reach are critical competitive advantages.

AI-driven mineral discovery represents a high-growth opportunity in the critical metals space, with venture investors backing solutions that address supply chain bottlenecks for battery and semiconductor manufacturing.

European tech continues to attract substantial growth-stage funding across B2B services and deep tech, indicating investor confidence in scaling European winners beyond domestic markets into continental expansion strategies.
newsletters.originalTitle: 06.05 - LegalPlace lève 70 millions d’euros et s'empare de Legalstart

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: PE fundraising is consolidating around top-tier managers and specialists, with middle-market funds ($1-5B) facing intense pressure as LP capital concentrates through re-ups with established firms rather than new relationships. The industry's 2021-2024 boom driven by near-zero rates and surging valuations is irreversible, forcing smaller managers to differentiate or face fundraising headwinds. Meanwhile, SpaceX's upcoming IPO introduces a wild card for early investors as Nasdaq's new 15-day fast entry rule could accelerate stock price appreciation and force lock-up decisions earlier than typical.
keyTakeaways: Specialist PE managers captured 74% of capital raised in 2025 versus a historical average of 64%, signaling that generalist mid-market funds must demonstrate unique expertise or risk being squeezed out of capital flows entirely.

LP distributions averaging 16% of NAV annually (2022-2025) versus the historical 25% norm are leaving investors over-allocated and reluctant to deploy fresh capital—only a meaningful rebound in exit realizations will materially ease this constraint.

SpaceX's Nasdaq fast entry rule creates unprecedented timing pressure for long-tenured private investors seeking liquidity, potentially forcing earlier-than-expected sell decisions and reshaping traditional IPO lock-up dynamics for mega-cap private companies.
newsletters.originalTitle: SpaceX's IPO wild card

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: The North American Electric Reliability Corporation issued a rare Level 3 warning about grid instability, citing exponential growth in computational loads from data centers as a critical threat to power infrastructure over the next four years. Meanwhile, the Trump administration's $120 million payout to Golden State Wind to abandon its Morro Bay offshore wind lease is drawing regulatory scrutiny from California, while the state's grid operator launched a new regional electricity market enabling cross-state renewable energy trading.
keyTakeaways: Data center growth poses an immediate and severe threat to grid reliability—this creates urgent demand for distributed power solutions, grid modernization technologies, and alternative energy sources that can scale rapidly to meet computational loads.

Government buyouts of renewable energy projects signal policy volatility that could undermine investor confidence in clean energy infrastructure, but may paradoxically accelerate private-sector solutions and create opportunities in grid resilience and energy storage companies.

Regional electricity markets are expanding across the West, enabling efficient renewable energy trading—this structural shift favors utilities and energy retailers positioned to participate in interstate transmission and intermittent renewable integration.
newsletters.originalTitle: ‘Big Deal’ Blackout Warning

newsletters.title: Term Sheet
newsletters.summary: Astrocade, an AI-powered game creation platform cofounded by brothers Ali and Amir Sadeghian, has raised $56 million across Series A (led by Sea) and Series B (led by Sequoia Capital) funding rounds. The platform enables users to create games through natural language prompts and has grown to 5 million monthly active users with 140 million monthly game plays across 75,000 games from creators in 80 countries. Investors including Google, Nvidia, and LG Ventures are betting on Astrocade's model as a YouTube-like creator economy play that monetizes game creation, with top creators earning thousands of dollars monthly.
keyTakeaways: AI-democratized game creation is attracting top-tier VC capital with strong traction: 5M MAUs and 140M monthly plays in just 8 months post-launch suggest significant product-market fit, validating the thesis that removing technical barriers dramatically expands the addressable creator market.

The creator economy expansion beyond traditional creator platforms is well-funded: Sequoia and Sea's backing signals confidence that interactive content creation (games vs. social media) represents an underexploited monetization opportunity with higher per-creator revenue potential.

GPU and AI infrastructure vendors are actively investing in consumption use cases: Google and Nvidia's participation indicates strategic positioning around AI-driven content generation and GPU utilization, suggesting this category could drive meaningful demand for computational resources.
newsletters.originalTitle: YouTube for video game creators

newsletters.title: Pro Rata
newsletters.summary: Private equity firms are forming AI consulting joint ventures with Anthropic and OpenAI as both offensive and defensive moves—potentially building AI-native versions of McKinsey while gaining essential AI expertise for portfolio companies. Separately, crypto exchange Bullish agreed to acquire transfer agent Equiniti for $4.25 billion, bridging traditional capital markets with cryptocurrency infrastructure, while major venture funds raised significant capital for AI and biotech startups.
keyTakeaways: PE is hedging massive software portfolio losses by partnering with AI leaders through structured JVs: Anthropic's $1.5B entity positions investors as equal stakeholders, while OpenAI's $4B entity guarantees 17.5% minimum returns with capped upside—a structure choice that signals confidence disparity between the two AI leaders.

The shift toward AI-native consulting could cannibalize traditional management consulting revenues while creating $100B+ return opportunities for PE firms that successfully build AI-powered portfolio company infrastructure.

Crypto infrastructure is consolidating around tokenized assets and regulated exchange models (Bullish acquiring Equiniti), suggesting venture capital is betting on institutional adoption of blockchain infrastructure at scale rather than speculative trading.
newsletters.originalTitle: Axios Pro Rata: PE ❤️s AI

newsletters.title: Benedict Evans
newsletters.summary: Major cloud providers are dramatically escalating AI infrastructure spending, with Amazon, Meta, Microsoft, and Alphabet collectively planning $700 billion in data center capex for 2026 versus $400 billion in 2025. OpenAI's exclusivity deal with Microsoft has ended, allowing AWS to offer ChatGPT APIs and signaling ongoing commoditization of foundation models. AI pricing models are rapidly shifting from flat-rate subscriptions to usage-based billing as agentic coding and token consumption threaten to make current pricing unsustainable across the industry.
keyTakeaways: The $300 billion year-over-year capex increase signals massive over-provisioning risk: investors should scrutinize individual company ROI metrics on AI infrastructure rather than assuming the spending correlates with revenue growth, particularly for Meta which lacks enterprise cloud revenue to offset its $100+ billion metaverse losses.

The Microsoft-OpenAI exclusivity breakup indicates foundation model capabilities are becoming commoditized faster than expected—expect accelerating price competition and consolidation around companies with distribution advantages (Microsoft, Amazon, Google) rather than pure AI model providers.

The shift to usage-based pricing from flat-rate subscriptions mirrors the mobile data market 15+ years ago and suggests the industry is entering a sustained margin compression phase; companies like Anthropic are forced to eliminate loss-making pricing models before establishing market dominance, fundamentally altering the path to profitability.
newsletters.originalTitle: Benedict's Newsletter: No. 641

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: The traditional utility business model is facing fundamental disruption due to climate pressures—wildfires, rising customer bills, and political backlash—prompting even major figures like Pennsylvania Governor Josh Shapiro and Berkshire Hathaway CEO Greg Abel to acknowledge that the century-old 'regulatory compact' governing electric utilities is breaking down. The newsletter signals a pivotal moment where the foundational assumptions of how utilities operate and are regulated are being questioned by elected officials, regulators, and industry executives alike.
keyTakeaways: The regulatory compact that has protected utility revenues and margins for a century is eroding, creating investment risk for traditional utility stocks and potential opportunities for companies offering alternative energy solutions or grid modernization technologies.

Climate events (wildfires) combined with rate increases are triggering political pressure that could force regulatory and business model changes, making utilities a contested sector where capital allocation decisions must account for stranded assets and shifting regulatory risk.

The sector transition from centralized utility models suggests tailwinds for distributed energy resources, microgrids, storage, and industrial chemical alternatives (as mentioned with Trillium's raise), while traditional utility business models face structural headwinds.
newsletters.originalTitle: Utilities Under Threat

newsletters.title: Maddyness
newsletters.summary: OneFlash, a mobile charging kiosk startup, raised €3.5 million in Series A funding led by Anthony Bourbon's Blast Club investor community, following Xavier Niel's Kima Ventures investment in 2022. The funding aims to expand OneFlash's European footprint and solidify its market leadership. French tech fundraising showed moderation in April 2026 with 45 deals totaling €375 million, below the 2025 monthly average of €410 million, though AI-focused startups captured €32 million of €108 million raised in a recent week.
keyTakeaways: Blast Club and angel-led syndicates are becoming viable Series A funding sources for European deeptech, demonstrating that French startups no longer depend solely on institutional VCs for mid-stage rounds
April 2026 fundraising data signals market normalization after 2025 peaks—expect more selective capital deployment with focus on proven unit economics rather than scale-at-all-costs narratives

AI startups continue commanding investor attention (31% of weekly capital) while consumer hardware (mobile charging infrastructure) attracts established angels, indicating bifurcated market dynamics between hot sectors and infrastructure plays
newsletters.originalTitle: 05.05 - Après Xavier Niel, OneFlash accueille Anthony Bourbon et son Blast Club au capital

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: Private markets are experiencing uneven growth post-2022 rate reset, with unicorn formation slowing dramatically—half as many startups crossed $1 billion valuations in April 2026 versus March. Notable deals include Boop, a new insurtech startup for humanoid robots founded by Soylent's Rob Rhinehart, which raised $200,000 at a $10 million valuation and is targeting $2.5 million in seed funding; separately, CoreWeave secured a $3.1 billion leveraged loan for GPU acquisition to support OpenAI and Cohere contracts. European university spinouts are tracking toward record deal values as deep tech venture accelerates.
keyTakeaways: Unicorn formation has collapsed to the slowest pace in six months, suggesting a fundamental shift in how venture capital is being deployed and which startups can achieve mega-valuations in a higher-rate environment.

Emerging categories like robot insurance and GPU-backed infrastructure financing are attracting significant capital, indicating investors are shifting from consumer tech toward AI-adjacent hardware, insurance tech, and compute infrastructure bets.

The 'end of easy money' is creating sharp divergence in private markets—while overall AUM growth continues toward $26.7 trillion by 2030, capital is consolidating around fewer strategies, making access to funding increasingly difficult for non-strategic startups.
newsletters.originalTitle: Private markets are splitting apart

newsletters.title: Term Sheet
newsletters.summary: Ethereal Ventures, a previously low-profile crypto VC firm cofounded by Ethereum's Joe Lubin and Min Teo in 2021, is emerging from obscurity with a strong track record despite market headwinds. Managing under $150 million across two funds with over 80 portfolio companies, the firm achieved a 0.12 DPI rate ranking in the top 10% of funds launched in 2021, bolstered by its breakout investment in EigenLayer, which reached a $1.05 billion valuation.
keyTakeaways: Disciplined early-stage focus and founder-friendly strategies outperform during crypto downturns—Ethereal's top decile performance in 2021 (a rough vintage year) suggests specialized expertise and selective deployment matter more than capital size or market trends.

Concentrated winners drive fund returns in venture: EigenLayer's success likely accounts for a significant portion of Ethereal's DPI outperformance, illustrating the importance of backable conviction picks and the concentration risk in emerging tech funds.

Resist trend-chasing in crowded sectors—Ethereal's deliberate avoidance of the 30+ prediction market competitors despite current hype reflects a contrarian discipline that protected capital and may signal where overheated subsectors risk correction.
newsletters.originalTitle: A shy VC firm sticks its head up

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: The Trump administration is halting construction on approximately 165 onshore wind projects citing national security concerns, mirroring a December order on offshore wind that faced legal challenges. Meanwhile, major oil producers Exxon Mobil and Chevron are resisting White House pressure to increase drilling despite crude prices hitting $126 per barrel amid Iran-related supply disruptions, with executives citing they are already operating at capacity.
keyTakeaways: Wind energy infrastructure faces significant regulatory headwinds with government-imposed construction halts that will likely trigger litigation; investors should monitor federal court rulings on national security justifications for wind project delays.

Oil majors are maintaining disciplined capital allocation despite geopolitical supply shocks and administration pressure, suggesting crude prices may need to rise substantially higher to trigger incremental U.S. production increases.

The clean energy transition faces policy uncertainty under current administration, while traditional energy benefits from supply constraints but lacks incentive to expand capacity, potentially creating volatile commodity markets and investment opportunities in alternative energy solutions.
newsletters.originalTitle: New Headwinds

newsletters.title: Pro Rata
newsletters.summary: Spirit Airlines' collapse has ignited debate over causation—whether the Biden DOJ's blocked JetBlue merger or jet fuel cost spikes and poor management are to blame—with implications for future airline consolidation policy. GameStop stunned markets by proposing a $55 billion acquisition of eBay at $125 per share, though CEO Ryan Cohen could not credibly explain the financing mechanics during a CNBC interview, raising questions about deal viability given GameStop's $11 billion market cap and the tightening capital environment among Middle Eastern sovereign investors.
keyTakeaways: Antitrust enforcement uncertainty now creates material risk for distressed assets: the blocking of JetBlue-Spirit appears to have accelerated Spirit's demise rather than preserved competition, signaling that aggressive antitrust positions may backfire when overleveraged companies lack exit optionality.

GameStop's audacious bid for eBay highlights desperation for transformative M&A in struggling legacy retail, but the financing math ($55B target with $9B cash, $20B committed debt, and 5% eBay stake) is structurally unsound without massive co-investment—a difficult ask in a capital-constrained geopolitical environment.

Macro trend: overleveraged, poorly hedged businesses face existential risk during inflationary shocks (jet fuel, debt service costs); investors should scrutinize hedging policies and capital structure flexibility as primary failure modes, especially in capital-intensive sectors vulnerable to commodity price volatility.
newsletters.originalTitle: Axios Pro Rata: Spirited debate

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Rising spring temperatures and historic low snowpack in Western mountain regions are creating dangerous climbing conditions and shortening the traditional climbing season, a trend that insurance companies and mountain-guiding businesses recognize as a direct climate impact. The article explores how extreme heat is affecting outdoor recreation infrastructure and the businesses that depend on mountain access, with broader implications for seasonal tourism and risk management in climate-vulnerable regions.
keyTakeaways: Mountain-guiding and outdoor recreation businesses face increased operational risk and shortened revenue windows due to climate-driven seasonal shifts, creating demand for adaptive business models and insurance products tailored to climate volatility

Insurance companies are increasingly exposed to climate-related claims in outdoor recreation and tourism sectors, suggesting opportunity for climate risk assessment tools and parametric insurance solutions

Climate adaptation startups addressing heat mitigation (cooling technology), water scarcity (desalination), and data center resilience to extreme weather represent emerging investment themes driven by physical climate impacts on infrastructure and real estate
newsletters.originalTitle: The Mountains Are Too Hot

newsletters.title: Maddyness
newsletters.summary: A new Interpath study reveals European and US venture capital exits have deteriorated further year-over-year, with bankruptcies now exceeding successful exits. Despite this darkening landscape, the report indicates progressive improvement is expected in the current year. The newsletter also covers emerging trends in AI-driven technology platforms and the strategic undervaluation of operational assets like office furniture in corporate financial planning.
keyTakeaways: Bankruptcy rates surpassing exit rates signals prolonged VC market stress in Europe and US; investors should expect extended holding periods and potential write-downs before stabilization occurs
Gradual improvement expected in 2024+ creates selective entry opportunities for contrarian investors and secondary market players positioned to acquire distressed positions

AI infrastructure is reshaping technology's operating system paradigm—capital should rotate toward foundational AI platforms rather than mobile-first applications as the next computing cycle takes shape
newsletters.originalTitle: 04.05 - Alerte maximale : les faillites dépassent désormais les exits

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: Limited partners are conducting unprecedented granular due diligence on mid-market fund managers, scrutinizing individual deal entry multiples, exit strategies, and company-level performance rather than relying on headline fund metrics. This shift reflects a broader realization that fund manager ability to generate actual distributions and achieve portfolio exits has become the primary differentiator, with LPs benchmarking specific portfolio company marks across multiple PE funds to validate performance claims.
keyTakeaways: Mid-market PE managers must prepare detailed transaction narratives—LPs are now requesting specific entry multiples, exit mechanics, and return drivers on individual deals, not just headline MOIC figures. This requires robust deal documentation and transparent portfolio management.

Operational rigor around distributions and realizations has become the primary investment thesis for allocating capital to fund managers. Funds that cannot demonstrate consistent cash returns and clear exit pathways will struggle to attract LP capital regardless of historical performance.

The shift toward line-by-line LP due diligence suggests a market correction where smaller fund managers without strong operational execution or differentiated exit strategies may face significant capital formation challenges, benefiting only the most disciplined operators.
newsletters.originalTitle: How mid-market investors tell a story

newsletters.title: Fintech Business Weekly
newsletters.summary: Bolt, the struggling fintech super app co-founded by Ryan Breslow, has signed term sheets for a $150M funding round ($100M from one investor, $50M from another) that will close within 30-90 days, contingent on the company achieving profitability. The newsletter uses Bolt's situation to illustrate broader challenges in partner banking, questioning whether the business model is fundamentally back, broken, or obsolete.
keyTakeaways: Bolt's significant funding round is conditioned on near-term profitability, signaling investor skepticism about the company's current trajectory and requiring aggressive cost-cutting and revenue acceleration within 30-60 days—a high bar that may not be achievable.

The newsletter frames partner banking in existential terms through Bolt's example, suggesting structural challenges in the BaaS model that go beyond single company execution issues and may affect the broader fintech ecosystem.

Bolt is pivoting toward higher-margin adtech revenue ('GCommerce') and merchant cross-selling rather than pure payments processing, indicating that pure-play payment platforms face margin pressure and must diversify or consolidate to survive.
newsletters.originalTitle: Partner Banking is: a) Back b) Broken c) Obsolete d) All of the above

newsletters.title: Heatmap
newsletters.summary: Heatmap News covers five climate and energy stories highlighting emerging investment opportunities: solar capacity shortfalls in PJM's grid, desalination technology gaining commercial traction, Trump administration wind policy developments, Gen Z's receptiveness to electric vehicles, and space-based solar technology advancement. The newsletter targets investors and policymakers interested in clean energy infrastructure and climate solutions.
keyTakeaways: Solar deployment is lagging grid operator expectations (PJM region), creating supply-side investment gaps for developers and grid modernization companies to capture underserved capacity additions

Desalination is reaching commercial viability as climate pressures intensify water scarcity, opening capital allocation opportunities in water technology and infrastructure sectors facing regulatory tailwinds

Space-based solar and EV adoption among younger demographics represent emerging technology bets with long-term structural growth potential, though policy volatility (Trump wind deals) creates near-term sector uncertainty
newsletters.originalTitle: PJM’s Missing Solar, the Desalination Moment, and More

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: Global M&A reached an all-time high of $1.6 trillion in Q1 2026, driven by historic deal activity despite geopolitical headwinds, though the figure is heavily skewed by xAI's $250 billion acquisition by SpaceX. Capital flows are rotating toward energy and B2C sectors while tilting toward European targets as cheaper euro financing attracts North American acquirers, though IT deal activity excluding the mega-deal actually fell 52.5% quarter-over-quarter. Separately, AI adoption among physicians has surged to 81%, but could add $5.9 trillion in cumulative excess healthcare costs by the late 2030s before long-term savings materialize.
keyTakeaways: The xAI-SpaceX merger is a statistical outlier masking underlying IT dealmaking weakness—stripping it out reveals IT deal value declined sharply, signaling potential caution ahead for tech M&A despite healthy headline numbers.

Cross-border capital is flowing decisively toward European targets due to euro currency strength and compressed valuations, suggesting U.S. acquirers see better risk-adjusted returns abroad than domestic alternatives.

AI-driven healthcare cost inflation may precede efficiency gains, creating a 5-10 year window where healthcare spending accelerates before productivity benefits materialize—critical for healthcare-focused investors to model alternative cost scenarios.
newsletters.originalTitle: Your roadmap to AI investing

newsletters.title: PitchBook
newsletters.summary: Crossover investors concentrated their venture bets into fewer, larger pre-IPO deals in Q1 2026, with deal value surging 11-fold to a record $220.9 billion despite only 178 transactions, signaling a shift toward mega-rounds like OpenAI's $110 billion and Anthropic's $30.6 billion Series G.
